## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`
**Q: Differentiate between Forward Kinematics and Inverse Kinematics in the context of a surgical robot.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| S.No. | Forward Kinematics | Inverse Kinematics |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Computes end-effector pose from joint variables. | Computes joint variables from desired end-effector pose. |
| 2 | Always has a **unique** solution. | May have **multiple** solutions (or none, if target is outside workspace). |
| 3 | Computationally simple — matrix multiplication. | Computationally complex — involves transcendental equations or iterative Jacobian-based methods. |
| 4 | Used for real-time **visualization** of instrument tip in tele-surgery. | Used for **path planning** and **autonomous targeting** of lesions. |

*(1 mark for each valid distinction point; 3 marks for 3 correct points.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question]`
**Q: What is meant by kinematic singularity? Why is it relevant in inverse kinematics for medical robots?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **kinematic singularity** is a configuration of the robot where the Jacobian matrix $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$ loses full rank, i.e., $\det(\mathbf{J}) = 0$.

At a singularity:
1. The manipulator **loses one or more degrees of freedom** in Cartesian space.
2. **Infinite joint velocities** would be required to achieve a finite Cartesian velocity (resolved-rate IK fails: $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}\dot{\mathbf{x}}$ diverges).
3. In **medical robots**, singularities must be **rigorously avoided** because an unexpected loss of dexterity during surgery could cause unintended motion and **patient injury**.

*(1 mark each for definition, mathematical condition, and medical relevance.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern Reminder:** Each Part B question has **internal choice** — students must answer **either Option A or Option B**. Each option has **two sub-parts** of **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Question]`

**A (a)** For a **2-link planar manipulator** with link lengths $L_1 = 4$ units and $L_2 = 3$ units, derive the **forward kinematics equations** using the DH convention. Given $\theta_1 = 30°$ and $\theta_2 = 45°$, compute the **end-effector position and orientation**.

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — DH Parameter Table** *(1 mark)*

| Link $i$ | $a_i$ | $\alpha_i$ | $d_i$ | $\theta_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_1 = 4$ | $0°$ | $0$ | $\theta_1 = 30°$ |
| 2 | $L_2 = 3$ | $0°$ | $0$ | $\theta_2 = 45°$ |

**Step 2 — Construct $^{0}\mathbf{A}_1$ and $^{1}\mathbf{A}_2$** using the standard DH matrix formula *(2 marks)*:

$$^{0}\mathbf{A}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} c\theta_1 & -s\theta_1 & 0 & L_1 c\theta_1 \\ s\theta_1 & c\theta_1 & 0 & L_1 s\theta_1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.866 & -0.5 & 0 & 3.464 \\ 0.5 & 0.866 & 0 & 2.0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$^{1}\mathbf{A}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} c\theta_2 & -s\theta_2 & 0 & L_2 c\theta_2 \\ s\theta_2 & c\theta_2 & 0 & L_2 s\theta_2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 & -0.707 & 0 & 2.121 \\ 0.707 & 0.707 & 0 & 2.121 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply** $^{0}\mathbf{T}_2 = {}^{0}\mathbf{A}_1 \cdot {}^{1}\mathbf{A}_2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
p_x &= L_1 c\theta_1 + L_2 c(\theta_1+\theta_2) = 4(0.866) + 3\cos(75°) \\
&= 3.464 + 3(0.2588) = 3.464 + 0.776 = \mathbf{4.240 \text{ units}} \\[4pt]
p_y &= L_1 s\theta_1 + L_2 s(\theta_1+\theta_2) = 4(0.5) + 3\sin(75°) \\
&= 2.0 + 3(0.9659) = 2.0 + 2.898 = \mathbf{4.898 \text{ units}} \\[4pt]
\phi &= \theta_1 + \theta_2 = 30° + 45° = \mathbf{75°}
\end{aligned}$$

*(2 marks for substitution; 2 marks for final numerical values.)*

---

**A (b)** Explain the **geometric method of solving inverse kinematics** for a 2-link planar manipulator. Discuss the **multiple solutions** and **elbow-up / elbow-down** configurations with a neat diagram.

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Setup** *(1 mark)*: Given target $(p_x, p_y)$, treat the manipulator as two connected rigid links forming a triangle with the origin and the target point.

**Step 2 — Apply Law of Cosines** *(2 marks)*:

The squared distance from origin to target is:

$$r^2 = p_x^2 + p_y^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2$$

Solving for $\theta_2$:

$$\theta_2 = \pm \arccos\left(\frac{p_x^2 + p_y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2}\right)$$

The $\mathbf{+}$ sign corresponds to **elbow-down** (link 2 bent *below* the line to target) and $\mathbf{-}$ sign to **elbow-up** (link 2 bent *above*). *(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Solve for $\theta_1$** *(2 marks)*:

$$\theta_1 = \operatorname{atan2}(p_y, p_x) - \operatorname{atan2}(L_2 \sin\theta_2, L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2)$$

**Step 4 — Discussion of Multiple Solutions** *(1 mark)*: For each target within the reachable workspace, **two distinct configurations** exist (unless on the workspace boundary). The surgeon or motion planner must select the **clinically appropriate** one (e.g., avoid collisions with surrounding tissue).

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question — Alternate Choice]`

**B (a)** With reference to the **Denavit–Hartenberg (DH) convention**, explain each of the **four link parameters** ($a_i$, $\alpha_i$, $d_i$, $\theta_i$) with the help of a labeled diagram. State the standard form of the **homogeneous transformation matrix** $^{i-1}\mathbf{A}_i$.

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Diagram Description** *(2 marks)*: Show a 3D sketch with two consecutive links $i-1$ and $i$, marking axes $X_{i-1}, Y_{i-1}, Z_{i-1}$ and $X_i, Y_i, Z_i$, the common normal between two joint axes, link length $a_i$, twist $\alpha_i$, offset $d_i$, and joint angle $\theta_i$.

**Step 2 — Parameter Definitions** *(3 marks — 0.75 each)*:

- **$a_i$ (Link Length):** Distance measured along $X_i$ from the intersection of $X_i$ with $Z_{i-1}$ to the origin of frame $\{i\}$.
- **$\alpha_i$ (Link Twist):** Angle measured about $X_i$ from $Z_{i-1}$ to $Z_i$ (positive = right-hand rule).
- **$d_i$ (Link Offset):** Distance measured along $Z_{i-1}$ from the origin of frame $\{i-1\}$ to the intersection of $Z_{i-1}$ with $X_i$.
- **$\theta_i$ (Joint Angle):** Angle measured about $Z_{i-1}$ from $X_{i-1}$ to $X_i$.

**Step 3 — Standard DH Matrix** *(2 marks)*:

$$^{i-1}\mathbf{A}_i = \begin{bmatrix} c\theta_i & -s\theta_i c\alpha_i & s\theta_i s\alpha_i & a_i c\theta_i \\ s\theta_i & c\theta_i c\alpha_i & -c\theta_i s\alpha_i & a_i s\theta_i \\ 0 & s\alpha_i & c\alpha_i & d_i \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

**B (b)** A **3-DOF planar manipulator** has link lengths $L_1 = 2$, $L_2 = 1.5$, $L_3 = 1$ (all in meters). The joint angles are $\theta_1 = 0°$, $\theta_2 = 60°$, $\theta_3 = -30°$. Determine the **end-effector position** using forward kinematics.

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — DH Table** *(1 mark)*:

| Link $i$ | $a_i$ | $\alpha_i$ | $d_i$ | $\theta_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $2.0$ | $0°$ | $0$ | $0°$ |
| 2 | $1.5$ | $0°$ | $0$ | $60°$ |
| 3 | $1.0$ | $0°$ | $0$ | $-30°$ |

**Step 2 — Compute cumulative angles** *(1 mark)*:

$$\theta_{12} = \theta_1 + \theta_2 = 60°, \quad \theta_{123} = \theta_1 + \theta_2 + \theta_3 = 30°$$

**Step 3 — Position Equations for 3-Link Planar RR Manipulator** *(2 marks)*:

$$\begin{aligned}
p_x &= L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) + L_3\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2 + \theta_3) \\
p_y &= L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) + L_3\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2 + \theta_3)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Substitution** *(2 marks)*:

$$\begin{aligned}
p_x &= 2\cos(0°) + 1.5\cos(60°) + 1.0\cos(30°) \\
&= 2(1) + 1.5(0.5) + 1.0(0.866) \\
&= 2.0 + 0.75 + 0.866 = \mathbf{3.616 \text{ m}} \\[4pt]
p_y &= 2\sin(0°) + 1.5\sin(60°) + 1.0\sin(30°) \\
&= 0 + 1.5(0.866) + 1.0(0.5) \\
&= 1.299 + 0.5 = \mathbf{1.799 \text{ m}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Orientation** *(1 mark)*:

$$\phi = \theta_1 + \theta_2 + \theta_3 = 0° + 60° + (-30°) = \mathbf{30°}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Wrong rotation order in DH matrix multiplication** — many students write $\mathbf{T} \cdot \mathbf{R}$ instead of $\mathbf{R} \cdot \mathbf{T}$. Remember: rotation about $Z$ must come **first**, then translation along $Z$, then translation along $X$, then rotation about $X$.
> 2. **Forgetting joint offset $d_i$** — when $d_i \neq 0$ (as in prismatic joints or scara arms), omitting it produces wrong $z$-component.
> 3. **Sign confusion in $\operatorname{atan2}$** — KTU expects $\operatorname{atan2}(y, x)$ format (note the order), not $\tan^{-1}(y/x)$. Loss of 1 mark per occurrence.
> 4. **Skipping singularity check in IK** — KTU 2024 papers explicitly ask about Jacobian singularity in medical robot context. Always state $\det(\mathbf{J}) = 0$ and its clinical implication.
> 5. **Not labeling diagrams** — for 7-mark FK derivations, unlabeled diagrams cost up to **2 marks** even if the math is correct.
> 6. **Units mismatch** — link lengths must be in consistent units (m or mm throughout); orientation in degrees or radians (state explicitly).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Forward Kinematics** maps **joint space → Cartesian space** (one-to-one, always solvable).
- **Inverse Kinematics** maps **Cartesian space → joint space** (many-to-one, may have no solution outside workspace).
- **DH convention** uses 4 parameters per link: $(a_i, \alpha_i, d_i, \theta_i)$.
- The DH transformation matrix formula is the **most important** formula to memorize.
- For a **2-link planar arm**: FK gives $(p_x, p_y, \phi)$ in closed form; IK uses the **law of cosines** + $\operatorname{atan2}$ trick.
- **Multiple IK solutions** exist: elbow-up vs elbow-down, and for 6-DOF arms, up to **16 solutions**; for 7-DOF (da Vinci), **infinitely many**.
- **Singularities** occur when $\det(\mathbf{J}) = 0$; critical concern in surgical robotics due to patient safety.
- **Algebraic IK** uses $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$ identity; **Geometric IK** uses triangle decomposition; **Numerical IK** uses Jacobian pseudo-inverse or transpose.
- **Velocity-level IK**: $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{x}}$.
- **FK uses**: visualization, master–slave mapping, real-time tip tracking in tele-surgery.
- **IK uses**: autonomous path planning, image-guided targeting, collision-free trajectory generation.
- **Workspace** = set of all reachable Cartesian points. **Dexterous workspace** = set of points reachable with all orientations.
- **Redundancy** = DOF > 6 (task space dimension); exploited in medical robots to avoid obstacles and singularities.
- **Compound angle identities** ($\cos(A+B)$, $\sin(A+B)$) frequently appear in FK derivations — memorize them.
- **KTU exam trick**: "Write DH table first" — it earns easy partial marks even if you make a later math error.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forward and Reverse Kinematics of Manipulators with Two, Three, and Four Degrees of Freedom (2D and 3D)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT724 - MEDICAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Forward and Reverse Kinematics of Manipulators with Two, Three, and Four Degrees of Freedom (2D and 3D)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Manipulator in Medical Robotics?

A **manipulator** is a mechanically articulated chain of rigid links interconnected by joints, terminated by an **end-effector** (gripper, scalpel holder, camera mount, or surgical instrument). In medical robotics, manipulators form the kinematic backbone of surgical robots (e.g., Da Vinci's Patient Side Cart), rehabilitation exoskeletons, and robotic ultrasound probes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of a manipulator = the minimum number of independent joint variables (generalised coordinates) required to completely specify the configuration (position + orientation) of the end-effector with respect to the base frame.
> - For a planar (2D) manipulator working in the XY-plane without rotation about the Z-axis, the practical DOF needed for *positioning only* is **2**. Adding an end-effector orientation (yaw) makes it **3 DOF planar**.
> - For a spatial (3D) manipulator, a typical surgical arm needs **6 DOF** (3 for position + 3 for orientation). The course scopes down to **2, 3, and 4 DOF** for foundational clarity.

## 1.2 Forward Kinematics (FK) — The "Given Joints, Find Hand" Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Forward Kinematics (FK)** is the process of computing the **position and orientation** of the end-effector as a function of the **known joint variables** $q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n$, given the fixed geometric parameters of the manipulator (link lengths, twists, offsets).

Mathematically,
$$
\mathbf{x}_{ee} = f(\mathbf{q}), \qquad \mathbf{q} = \begin{bmatrix} q_1 \\ q_2 \\ \vdots \\ q_n \end{bmatrix}
$$
where $\mathbf{x}_{ee} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the task-space vector and $\mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the joint-space vector. For a planar manipulator, $m = n$.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition 🦾):**
Imagine your own arm. Your **shoulder angle** ($\theta_1$), **elbow angle** ($\theta_2$), and **wrist angle** ($\theta_3$) are the joint variables. If a friend tells you exactly how much each joint is bent, you can — without thinking — instantly know where your fingertip is in 3D space. That instantaneous computation your brain performs is **Forward Kinematics**. It is a **unique, deterministic mapping** — one set of joint angles maps to exactly one tip position.

## 1.3 Reverse (Inverse) Kinematics (IK) — The "Given Hand, Find Joints" Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inverse Kinematics (IK)** is the inverse mapping: given a desired end-effector pose (position $\mathbf{p}$ and orientation $\mathbf{R}$), compute the joint variables $\mathbf{q}$ that achieve it.
> $$
> \mathbf{q} = f^{-1}(\mathbf{x}_{ee})
> $$

Unlike FK, IK is generally **non-linear**, may have **multiple solutions (branching)**, **no solutions** (target outside workspace), or **infinitely many solutions** (kinematic redundancy, $n > m$).

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition 🤚):**
Now reverse the situation: you are told to touch a specific dot on a wall with your fingertip. Your brain must decide *how much to bend each joint*. You will quickly notice that there are **multiple ways** to reach the same point — elbow up or elbow down. This multiplicity is the hallmark of IK.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why FK is taught before IK:** FK is a **direct matrix multiplication**, while IK requires solving non-linear trigonometric equations, exploiting geometry, or iteratively inverting the Jacobian. A strong FK foundation (especially via Denavit–Hartenberg parameters) is the prerequisite for all IK methods.

## 1.4 Relevance to Medical Robotics

In surgical robotics:
- **FK** is used in real-time **virtual reality overlays** (the surgeon sees the instrument tip's 3D position computed from joint encoder readings).
- **IK** is used in **master–slave teleoperation** (the surgeon's hand motion at the console is mapped to desired slave joint angles).
- A **4-DOF manipulator** (e.g., SCARA-type) is used in laparoscopic camera holders (AESOP, ViKY) — only **4 DOF** are needed because the trocar acts as a **remote centre of motion (RCM)**, fixing one rotational DOF.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Module 2 explicitly scopes FK/IK to **2, 3, and 4 DOF** in both 2D and 3D. Questions on **6-DOF Puma-style arms** are out of syllabus scope here, but their FK pipeline uses the same DH framework.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Joint Types and the Configuration Space

A manipulator is built from two primitive joint types:

| Joint Type | Symbol | Variable | Physical Example (Medical Robot) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Revolute (R)** | R | Angle $\theta_i$ (rad) | Shoulder, elbow of surgical arm |
| **Prismatic (P)** | P | Displacement $d_i$ (m) | Linear slide of a stereotactic frame |

A manipulator with the configuration **RR** has 2 DOF; **RRR** has 3 DOF; **RRRP**, **PRRR**, **RRPR** all have 4 DOF.

## 2.2 Coordinate Frame Assignment — The Denavit–Hartenberg (DH) Convention

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Denavit–Hartenberg (DH) parameter convention** is the standardised method taught at KTU for assigning a coordinate frame to each link and reducing the 6-DOF general spatial transform between two links to **only 4 parameters** (because two constraints — common normal, perpendicular axes — eliminate 2 DOF).

For each link $i$, four DH parameters are defined:

| DH Parameter | Symbol | Geometric Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Link length | $a_i$ | Distance along $X_i$ from $Z_{i-1}$ to $Z_i$ |
| Link twist | $\alpha_i$ | Rotation about $X_i$ from $Z_{i-1}$ to $Z_i$ |
| Link offset | $d_i$ | Distance along $Z_{i-1}$ from $X_{i-1}$ to $X_i$ |
| Joint angle | $\theta_i$ | Rotation about $Z_{i-1}$ from $X_{i-1}$ to $X_i$ |

For a **revolute joint**, $\theta_i$ is the variable; for a **prismatic joint**, $d_i$ is the variable. All others are constant geometric parameters.

## 2.3 The DH Transformation Matrix

The homogeneous transformation from frame $\{i-1\}$ to frame $\{i\}$ is

$$
^{i-1}\!T_i \;=\;
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & \sin\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\cos\theta_i \\
\sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & -\cos\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\sin\theta_i \\
0 & \sin\alpha_i & \cos\alpha_i & d_i \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

For a **planar manipulator** (all $\alpha_i = 0$, all $d_i = 0$, all $a_i$ in the XY-plane), this collapses to the simpler 2D rotation–translation:

$$
^{i-1}\!T_i \;=\;
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i & a_i\cos\theta_i \\
\sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i & a_i\sin\theta_i \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in medical robotics:** Once the FK chain is built, the **position of a surgical instrument tip** in the patient frame is obtained by simply multiplying these 2×2 (planar) or 4×4 (spatial) matrices in sequence. The same matrix is reused for collision checking against CT-derived anatomical models.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DOF (Grübler, planar) | $\text{DOF} = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | $n$ links, $j_1$ lower-pair, $j_2$ higher-pair |
| 2 | DOF (Grübler, spatial) | $\text{DOF} = 6(n-1) - 5j_5 - 4j_4 - 3j_3 - 2j_2 - j_1$ | $j_k$ = $k$-DOF joints |
| 3 | Planar 2R FK position | $x = a_1\cos\theta_1 + a_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$ | $(x,y)$ in metres |
| 4 | Planar 2R FK position | $y = a_1\sin\theta_1 + a_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$ | $(x,y)$ in metres |
| 5 | Planar 2R workspace radius | $r_{\max} = a_1 + a_2$, $r_{\min} = \vert a_1 - a_2 \vert$ | Annulus |
| 6 | Planar 2R IK (cosine law) | $\cos\theta_2 = \dfrac{x^2 + y^2 - a_1^2 - a_2^2}{2 a_1 a_2}$ | Two solutions: $\pm$ |
| 7 | Planar 2R IK (elbow) | $\theta_2 = \text{atan2}\!\left(\pm\sqrt{1-\cos^2\theta_2},\, \cos\theta_2\right)$ | Use $\text{atan2}$ for quadrant |
| 8 | Planar 2R IK (shoulder) | $\theta_1 = \text{atan2}(y,x) - \text{atan2}\!\left(a_2\sin\theta_2,\, a_1+a_2\cos\theta_2\right)$ | Two-branch solution |
| 9 | Spatial 3R FK tip position | $\mathbf{p} = \begin{bmatrix} c_1(a_2 c_2 + a_3 c_{23}) \\ s_1(a_2 c_2 + a_3 c_{23}) \\ a_2 s_2 + a_3 s_{23} \end{bmatrix}$ | $c_i \equiv \cos\theta_i,\ s_i \equiv \sin\theta_i$ |
| 10 | Jacobian (planar 2R) | $J(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} -a_1 s_1 - a_2 s_{12} & -a_2 s_{12} \\ a_1 c_1 + a_2 c_{12} & a_2 c_{12} \end{bmatrix}$ | Used in IK iteration |
| 11 | Singularity condition | $\det J(\mathbf{q}) = 0$ | Jacobian rank-deficient |
| 12 | 4-DOF SCARA FK end pose | $^{0}\!T_4 = {}^{0}\!T_1 \cdot {}^{1}\!T_2 \cdot {}^{2}\!T_3 \cdot {}^{3}\!T_4$ | Product of 4 DH matrices |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** Students often confuse $\text{atan}(y/x)$ (range $\pm\pi/2$) with $\text{atan2}(y,x)$ (full $\pm\pi$, quadrant-aware). Always use **atan2** in IK solutions or you will silently lose 2 marks for the elbow-down/right-half-plane case.

## 2.5 Workspace Concepts

The **workspace** is the set of all points reachable by the end-effector.

- **Dexterous workspace**: all orientations reachable at a point.
- **Reachable workspace**: all points reachable in at least one orientation.

For a planar 2R arm:
- Outer reach: $a_1 + a_2$
- Inner reach (hole): $\vert a_1 - a_2 \vert$

For a 3R spatial arm, the workspace is a 3D solid (sphere-like) and for 4-DOF (e.g., RRRP cylindrical) it becomes a cylinder with hemispherical caps.

## 2.6 Why These Equations Matter in Engineering

- **Surgical path planning**: The FK must be evaluated thousands of times per second to render the live instrument position on the surgeon's display.
- **Collision avoidance**: The Jacobian maps joint velocities to tip velocities; $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = J(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$. Singularities correspond to **joint lock-up** and must be avoided.
- **Haptic feedback**: In teleoperated systems, the slave IK is solved in real time at ~1 kHz; closed-form IK formulas are mandatory (no iterative solvers).
- **Rehabilitation robots (Lokomat, Armeo)**: IK is used to map therapist-defined Cartesian trajectories to patient joint trajectories that respect safety limits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Case Study A — 2-DOF Planar Manipulator (RR)

### 3.1.1 Forward Kinematics Derivation

Consider a planar 2R arm with link lengths $a_1, a_2$ and joint angles $\theta_1, \theta_2$ (measured from the $X$-axis of the preceding frame).

**Step 1** — Write the DH table for the planar case (all $d_i = 0, \alpha_i = 0$):

| Link $i$ | $a_i$ | $\alpha_i$ | $d_i$ | $\theta_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a_1$ | 0 | 0 | $\theta_1$ |
| 2 | $a_2$ | 0 | 0 | $\theta_2$ |

**Step 2** — Substitute into the planar DH matrix:

$$
^{0}\!T_1 \;=\;
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta_1 & -\sin\theta_1 & a_1\cos\theta_1 \\
\sin\theta_1 & \cos\theta_1 & a_1\sin\theta_1 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
^{1}\!T_2 \;=\;
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta_2 & -\sin\theta_2 & a_2\cos\theta_2 \\
\sin\theta_2 & \cos\theta_2 & a_2\sin\theta_2 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3** — Compute $^{0}\!T_2 = {}^{0}\!T_1 \cdot {}^{1}\!T_2$:

$$
^{0}\!T_2 \;=\;
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & a_1\cos\theta_1 + a_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\
\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & a_1\sin\theta_1 + a_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4** — Extract the end-effector position (top-right 2-vector):

$$
\boxed{\;x \;=\; a_1\cos\theta_1 + a_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;y \;=\; a_1\sin\theta_1 + a_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **complete closed-form FK** for a planar 2R manipulator. It is a one-line evaluation per cycle in C/MATLAB — ideal for the 1 kHz inner loop of a surgical robot controller.

### 3.1.2 Inverse Kinematics Derivation

**Given**: Desired end-effector position $(x_d, y_d)$.

**Goal**: Solve for $(\theta_1, \theta_2)$.

**Step 1** — Square and add the two FK equations:

$$
x_d^2 + y_d^2 \;=\; a_1^2 + a_2^2 + 2 a_1 a_2 \cos\theta_2
$$

**Step 2** — Solve for $\cos\theta_2$ (this is the **cosine law** for the triangle with sides $a_1, a_2$ and hypotenuse $\sqrt{x_d^2+y_d^2}$):

$$
\cos\theta_2 \;=\; \frac{x_d^2 + y_d^2 - a_1^2 - a_2^2}{2 a_1 a_2}
$$

**Step 3** — Use the identity $\sin^2\theta_2 + \cos^2\theta_2 = 1$ to obtain:

$$
\sin\theta_2 \;=\; \pm\sqrt{1 - \cos^2\theta_2}
$$

(The $\pm$ gives the **two solutions**: elbow-up and elbow-down.)

**Step 4** — Compute $\theta_2$ using the quadrant-aware atan2:

$$
\theta_2 \;=\; \text{atan2}\!\left(\sin\theta_2,\; \cos\theta_2\right)
$$

**Step 5** — Substitute into the FK equation for $\theta_1$:

Rearrange $x_d = (a_1 + a_2\cos\theta_2)\cos\theta_1 - a_2\sin\theta_2\sin\theta_1$ and $y_d = (a_1 + a_2\cos\theta_2)\sin\theta_1 + a_2\sin\theta_2\cos\theta_1$ as a standard rotation:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x_d \\ y_d \end{bmatrix}
\;=\;
\begin{bmatrix}
a_1 + a_2\cos\theta_2 & -a_2\sin\theta_2 \\
a_2\sin\theta_2 & a_1 + a_2\cos\theta_2
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_1 \\ \sin\theta_1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Applying the substitution $\phi = \text{atan2}(a_2\sin\theta_2,\, a_1 + a_2\cos\theta_2)$ gives:

$$
\theta_1 \;=\; \text{atan2}(y_d, x_d) \;-\; \text{atan2}\!\left(a_2\sin\theta_2,\; a_1 + a_2\cos\theta_2\right)
$$

**Step 6** — Validity check:

A real solution exists **iff** the desired point lies inside the annulus:
$$
(\,a_1 - a_2\,)^2 \;\leq\; x_d^2 + y_d^2 \;\leq\; (\,a_1 + a_2\,)^2
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Marker Tip:** Showing the validity check above gives 1 extra mark — examiners reward it.

## 3.2 Case Study B — 3-DOF Planar Manipulator (RRR)

Adding a third revolute joint to the planar chain (typical of a 3-DOF elbow manipulator operating in 2D) yields three angles $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3$ and three link lengths $a_1, a_2, a_3$.

**Forward Kinematics** — by direct application of the chain rule on the previous derivation:

$$
x \;=\; a_1\cos\theta_1 + a_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) + a_3\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2+\theta_3)
$$

$$
y \;=\; a_1\sin\theta_1 + a_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) + a_3\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2+\theta_3)
$$

The end-effector orientation (yaw) is simply the cumulative angle:

$$
\phi_{ee} \;=\; \theta_1 + \theta_2 + \theta_3
$$

**Inverse Kinematics** — define the wrist-centre position by subtracting the third link:

$$
x_w \;=\; x_d - a_3\cos\phi_d, \qquad y_w \;=\; y_d - a_3\sin\phi_d
$$

Now solve the 2R sub-problem for $\theta_1, \theta_2$ using the formulas of §3.1.2, then:

$$
\theta_3 \;=\; \phi_d - \theta_1 - \theta_2
$$

This **wrist-partition decoupling** trick is the foundation of all 6-DOF Puma-style IK and earns full marks on KTU exams.

## 3.3 Case Study C — 3-DOF Spatial Manipulator (RRR, Anthropomorphic)

The classic 3R spatial arm has a vertical base joint, a shoulder, and an elbow — it is the spatial analogue of the human shoulder–elbow–wrist.

**DH Table (spatial, vertical base):**

| Link | $a_i$ | $\alpha_i$ | $d_i$ | $\theta_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 90° | $d_1$ | $\theta_1$ |
| 2 | $a_2$ | 0 | 0 | $\theta_2$ |
| 3 | $a_3$ | 0 | 0 | $\theta_3$ |

**Forward Kinematics** — write each DH matrix and multiply:

$$
^{0}\!T_1 \;=\;
\begin{bmatrix}
c_1 & 0 & s_1 & 0 \\
s_1 & 0 & -c_1 & 0 \\
0 & 1 & 0 & d_1 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
^{1}\!T_2 \;=\;
\begin{bmatrix}
c_2 & -s_2 & 0 & a_2 c_2 \\
s_2 & c_2 & 0 & a_2 s_2 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
^{2}\!T_3 \;=\;
\begin{bmatrix}
c_3 & -s_3 & 0 & a_3 c_3 \\
s_3 & c_3 & 0 & a_3 s_3 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Multiplying $^{0}\!T_1 \cdot {}^{1}\!T_2 \cdot {}^{2}\!T_3$ and extracting the translation column:

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
p_x &= (a_2 c_2 + a_3 c_{23})\cos\theta_1 \\
p_y &= (a_2 c_2 + a_3 c_{23})\sin\theta_1 \\
p_z &= d_1 + a_2 s_2 + a_3 s_{23}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

where $c_{23} = \cos(\theta_2+\theta_3)$, $s_{23} = \sin(\theta_2+\theta_3)$.

**Inverse Kinematics** — solve in two stages:

**Stage 1 — Base joint $\theta_1$:** from the first two equations,

$$
\theta_1 \;=\; \text{atan2}(p_y, p_x)
$$

**Stage 2 — Planar sub-problem in the r–z plane:** let

$$
r \;=\; \sqrt{p_x^2 + p_y^2}, \qquad z' \;=\; p_z - d_1
$$

Now apply the same cosine law as the planar 2R IK:

$$
\cos\theta_3 \;=\; \frac{r^2 + z'^2 - a_2^2 - a_3^2}{2 a_2 a_3}
$$

$$
\theta_3 \;=\; \text{atan2}\!\left(\pm\sqrt{1 - \cos^2\theta_3},\; \cos\theta_3\right)
$$

$$
\theta_2 \;=\; \text{atan2}(z', r) \;-\; \text{atan2}\!\left(a_3 s_3,\; a_2 + a_3 c_3\right)
$$

## 3.4 Case Study D — 4-DOF Manipulators (RRRP, RRPR, RROR)

A 4-DOF manipulator adds either a prismatic joint (linear insertion, common in laparoscopic tools) or a roll joint (tool spinning). Two industry-relevant configurations:

### 3.4.1 RRRP (Spherical/Revolute Configuration)

Used in **stereotactic neurosurgery frames** where the 4th DOF is a linear needle insertion $d_4$.

**DH Table:**

| Link | $a_i$ | $\alpha_i$ | $d_i$ | $\theta_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 90° | $d_1$ | $\theta_1$ |
| 2 | $a_2$ | 0 | 0 | $\theta_2$ |
| 3 | 0 | 90° | 0 | $\theta_3$ |
| 4 | 0 | 0 | $d_4$ | 0 |

**FK Tip Position (only the translation column matters for needle insertion):**

$$
p_x = (a_2 c_2 - d_4 s_{23})\cos\theta_1
$$

$$
p_y = (a_2 c_2 - d_4 s_{23})\sin\theta_1
$$

$$
p_z = d_1 + a_2 s_2 + d_4 c_{23}
$$

where $s_{23} = \sin(\theta_2+\theta_3)$, $c_{23} = \cos(\theta_2+\theta_3)$.

**Inverse Kinematics** — decouple the insertion depth:

$$
d_4 \;=\; \sqrt{(p_x - a_2 c_2 c_1)^2 + \cdots}
$$

A simpler closed-form arises by first solving the 3R base (Sections 3.3) for $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3$ to align the tool axis with the desired insertion direction, then:

$$
d_4 \;=\; \text{required penetration depth along the tool axis}
$$

### 3.4.2 RRPR (SCARA-type)

Used in **robotic pill-dispensing / IV-line preparation** in hospital pharmacies.

**DH Table (SCARA, vertical axes):**

| Link | $a_i$ | $\alpha_i$ | $d_i$ | $\theta_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a_1$ | 0 | $d_1$ | $\theta_1$ |
| 2 | $a_2$ | 0 | 0 | $\theta_2$ |
| 3 | 0 | 0 | $d_3$ | 0 |
| 4 | 0 | 0 | $d_4$ | $\theta_4$ |

**FK End-Effector Pose:**

$$
p_x = a_1 c_1 + a_2 c_{12}, \qquad p_y = a_1 s_1 + a_2 s_{12}
$$

$$
p_z = d_1 - d_3 - d_4, \qquad \phi_z = \theta_1 + \theta_2 + \theta_4
$$

**Inverse Kinematics** — independent in XY (planar 2R) and Z (sum of prismatic displacements):

$$
\theta_1, \theta_2 \;\leftarrow\; \text{planar 2R IK}(p_x, p_y) \quad\text{(as in §3.1.2)}
$$

$$
d_3 + d_4 \;\leftarrow\; d_1 - p_z
$$

$$
\theta_4 \;\leftarrow\; \phi_z - \theta_1 - \theta_2
$$

The decoupling makes SCARA IK trivial — a key reason for its use in high-speed pick-and-place medical automation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following **Mermaid block diagrams** illustrate the FK/IK pipelines and the DH frame-assignment logic at the architecture level, complementing the algebraic derivations above.

## 4.1 Forward Kinematics Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Joint Encoder Readings q1 q2 ... qn"] --> B["DH Parameter Table Lookup"]
    B --> C["Compute Individual DH Matrices T1 T2 ... Tn"]
    C --> D["Sequential Matrix Multiplication T_total = T1 x T2 x ... x Tn"]
    D --> E["Extract End Effector Position x y z"]
    D --> F["Extract End Effector Orientation Roll Pitch Yaw"]
    E --> G["Update Surgeon VR Overlay at 60 Hz"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["Collision Check Against Patient CT Model"]
```

## 4.2 Inverse Kinematics Solution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Desired End Effector Pose px py pz phi"] --> V{"Within Workspace?"}
    V -- "No" --> R["Return UNREACHABLE Error"]
    V -- "Yes" --> D["Apply Wrist Partition Decoupling if Applicable"]
    D --> S["Solve Closed Form Sub Problem cosine law + atan2"]
    S --> N{"Multiple Branches?"}
    N -- "Yes" --> B1["Branch 1 Elbow Up"]
    N -- "Yes" --> B2["Branch 2 Elbow Down"]
    B1 --> C["Collision and Joint Limit Filter"]
    B2 --> C
    C --> O["Selected Joint Angles q1 q2 ... qn"]
    N -- "No" --> O
    O --> M["Command Joint Actuators via PID Controller"]
```

## 4.3 DH Frame Assignment Logic (4-DOF Spatial Arm)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0["Base Frame 0 at Shoulder Mount"] --> L1["Frame 1 at Joint 1 axis after rotation theta1 about Z0"]
    L1 --> L2["Frame 2 at Joint 2 axis offset along X1 by a2"]
    L2 --> L3["Frame 3 at Joint 3 axis rotated by alpha2 90 deg about X2"]
    L3 --> L4["Frame 4 at End Effector Tip prismatic offset d4 along Z3"]
    L0 -- Z0 axis vertical --> L1
    L1 -- common normal X1 --> L2
    L2 -- twist alpha2 --> L3
    L3 -- prismatic stroke d4 --> L4
```

## 4.4 2R Planar Workspace (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OUTER["Outer Boundary r max = a1 + a2"]
        direction TB
        subgraph ANNULUS["Reachable Annulus"]
            direction TB
            subgraph INNER["Inner Boundary r min = abs a1 minus a2"]
                HOLE["Singular Configuration fully folded arm"]
            end
            REACH["All Reachable Points"]
        end
    end
```

## 4.5 IK Branches for a Planar 2R Arm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T["Target x y"] --> C["Compute cos theta2 from cosine law"]
    C --> SIN["sin theta2 = plus minus sqrt"]
    SIN --> UP["Branch A Elbow Up sin theta2 greater than 0"]
    SIN --> DN["Branch B Elbow Down sin theta2 less than 0"]
    UP --> TH1A["theta1 via atan2"]
    DN --> TH1B["theta1 via atan2"]
    TH1A --> SEL{"Joint Limits and Obstacle Check"}
    TH1B --> SEL
    SEL -- "Pass" --> OUT["Execute q1 q2"]
    SEL -- "Fail" --> REJ["Reject Branch Try Other"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🅰️ Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

> **Q:** Differentiate between **Forward Kinematics** and **Inverse Kinematics** of a robotic manipulator. Give one medical-robotics application of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Forward Kinematics (FK) | Inverse Kinematics (IK) |
|---|---|---|
| Input | Joint variables $\mathbf{q}$ | Desired end-effector pose $\mathbf{x}_{ee}$ |
| Output | End-effector pose $\mathbf{x}_{ee}$ | Joint variables $\mathbf{q}$ |
| Mapping | Direct, unique | Non-linear, possibly multiple solutions |
| Method | Matrix multiplication using DH parameters | Geometric / algebraic / iterative (Jacobian) |
| **Medical Example** | Computing the 3D position of a **Da Vinci instrument tip** from joint encoders for VR overlay | Computing the **slave joint angles** required when the surgeon moves the master console handle to a target point |

(3 marks: 1 for definition FK, 1 for definition IK, 1 for examples)

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **Q:** State the **four Denavit–Hartenberg parameters** of a link. Which parameter is the joint variable for (a) a revolute joint and (b) a prismatic joint?

**Model Answer:**
The four DH parameters linking frame $\{i-1\}$ to frame $\{i\}$ are:
1. **Link length** $a_i$ — distance along $X_i$ from $Z_{i-1}$ to $Z_i$.
2. **Link twist** $\alpha_i$ — rotation about $X_i$ from $Z_{i-1}$ to $Z_i$.
3. **Link offset** $d_i$ — distance along $Z_{i-1}$ from $X_{i-1}$ to $X_i$.
4. **Joint angle** $\theta_i$ — rotation about $Z_{i-1}$ from $X_{i-1}$ to $X_i$.

- (a) Revolute joint (R) → the variable is **$\theta_i$** (joint angle).
- (b) Prismatic joint (P) → the variable is **$d_i$** (link offset).

(3 marks: 2 for the four parameters with meaning, 1 for joint-type variables)

---

## 🅱️ Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B Module-2 questions carry **14 marks** and offer an internal choice. Below are **Question A** and **Question B** as alternate choices.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

> **Q:** Consider a **planar 2R manipulator** with link lengths $a_1 = 0.5$ m and $a_2 = 0.4$ m. The current joint angles are $\theta_1 = 30°$ and $\theta_2 = 45°$.
>
> **(a) (7 Marks)** Derive the **forward kinematics** of a planar 2R arm and compute the end-effector position.
>
> **(b) (7 Marks)** The surgeon now wants to move the tip to the target point $(x_d, y_d) = (0.7, 0.4)$ m. Compute the **inverse kinematics** solutions (both branches) and verify reachability.

#### Model Solution to A(a):

**Step 1 — DH Table (planar, all $d_i = 0$, $\alpha_i = 0$):**

| $i$ | $a_i$ | $\alpha_i$ | $d_i$ | $\theta_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.5 | 0 | 0 | 30° |
| 2 | 0.4 | 0 | 0 | 45° |

**Step 2 — Derive FK:**

Using the planar DH transformation:

$$
^{0}\!T_1 = \begin{bmatrix} c_1 & -s_1 & a_1 c_1 \\ s_1 & c_1 & a_1 s_1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
^{1}\!T_2 = \begin{bmatrix} c_2 & -s_2 & a_2 c_2 \\ s_2 & c_2 & a_2 s_2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Multiplying and extracting the position:

$$
x = a_1\cos\theta_1 + a_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2), \quad y = a_1\sin\theta_1 + a_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$\theta_1+\theta_2 = 75°$. $\cos 30° = 0.8660$, $\sin 30° = 0.5000$, $\cos 75° = 0.2588$, $\sin 75° = 0.9659$.

$$
x = 0.5(0.8660) + 0.4(0.2588) = 0.4330 + 0.1035 = 0.5365 \text{ m}
$$

$$
y = 0.5(0.5000) + 0.4(0.9659) = 0.2500 + 0.3864 = 0.6364 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{(x, y) = (0.5365,\; 0.6364)\ \text{m}}
$$

(Marks: 2 for DH table, 3 for derivation, 2 for numerical evaluation)

#### Model Solution to A(b):

**Step 1 — Reachability check:**

$$
(x_d, y_d) = (0.7, 0.4) \Rightarrow r = \sqrt{0.7^2 + 0.4^2} = \sqrt{0.65} = 0.8062 \text{ m}
$$

Workspace: $r_{\min} = \vert a_1 - a_2 \vert = 0.1$ m, $r_{\max} = a_1 + a_2 = 0.9$ m.

Since $0.1 \le 0.8062 \le 0.9$, the target is **reachable**. ✓

**Step 2 — Cosine law for $\theta_2$:**

$$
\cos\theta_2 = \frac{x_d^2 + y_d^2 - a_1^2 - a_2^2}{2 a_1 a_2} = \frac{0.65 - 0.25 - 0.16}{2(0.5)(0.4)} = \frac{0.24}{0.4} = 0.6
$$

$$
\theta_2 = \pm \cos^{-1}(0.6) = \pm 53.13°
$$

So **two branches**:
- Elbow-up: $\theta_2^{(1)} = +53.13°$, $\sin\theta_2^{(1)} = +0.8$
- Elbow-down: $\theta_2^{(2)} = -53.13°$, $\sin\theta_2^{(2)} = -0.8$

**Step 3 — Compute $\theta_1$ for each branch** using:

$$
\theta_1 = \text{atan2}(y_d, x_d) - \text{atan2}\!\left(a_2\sin\theta_2,\; a_1 + a_2\cos\theta_2\right)
$$

$\text{atan2}(0.4, 0.7) = 29.74°$.

Branch 1: $\text{atan2}(0.4 \times 0.8,\; 0.5 + 0.4 \times 0.6) = \text{atan2}(0.32,\; 0.74) = 23.41°$.

$$
\theta_1^{(1)} = 29.74° - 23.41° = 6.33°
$$

Branch 2: $\text{atan2}(-0.32,\; 0.74) = -23.41°$.

$$
\theta_1^{(2)} = 29.74° - (-23.41°) = 53.15°
$$

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{Branch 1 (elbow up)} &: (\theta_1, \theta_2) = (6.33°,\; 53.13°) \\
\text{Branch 2 (elbow down)} &: (\theta_1, \theta_2) = (53.15°,\; -53.13°)
\end{aligned}
}
$$

(Marks: 2 for reachability, 2 for cosine-law derivation, 3 for both branches numerical)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

> **Q:** A **spatial 3R anthropomorphic manipulator** (vertical base, horizontal shoulder, elbow) has DH parameters:
> $d_1 = 0.4$ m, $a_2 = 0.5$ m, $a_3 = 0.4$ m. All joint angles are initially zero.
>
> **(a) (7 Marks)** Derive the **forward kinematics** and find the end-effector position when $\theta_1 = 0°$, $\theta_2 = 30°$, $\theta_3 = -45°$.
>
> **(b) (7 Marks)** Compute the **inverse kinematics** joint angles needed to place the tip at $\mathbf{p} = (0.3,\ 0.0,\ 0.85)$ m. Discuss singularity.

#### Model Solution to B(a):

**Step 1 — DH Table:**

| $i$ | $a_i$ | $\alpha_i$ | $d_i$ | $\theta_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 90° | 0.4 | $\theta_1$ |
| 2 | 0.5 | 0 | 0 | $\theta_2$ |
| 3 | 0.4 | 0 | 0 | $\theta_3$ |

**Step 2 — Compute each DH matrix and multiply (using $c_{23}=\cos(\theta_2+\theta_3)$, $s_{23}=\sin(\theta_2+\theta_3)$):**

$$
p_x = (a_2 c_2 + a_3 c_{23})\cos\theta_1
$$

$$
p_y = (a_2 c_2 + a_3 c_{23})\sin\theta_1
$$

$$
p_z = d_1 + a_2 s_2 + a_3 s_{23}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation:** $\theta_2+\theta_3 = -15°$.

$\cos 30° = 0.8660$, $\sin 30° = 0.5$, $\cos(-15°) = 0.9659$, $\sin(-15°) = -0.2588$, $\cos 0° = 1$, $\sin 0° = 0$.

$$
p_x = (0.5 \times 0.8660 + 0.4 \times 0.9659)(1) = (0.4330 + 0.3864) = 0.8194 \text{ m}
$$

$$
p_y = (0.8194)(0) = 0 \text{ m}
$$

$$
p_z = 0.4 + 0.5 \times 0.5 + 0.4 \times (-0.2588) = 0.4 + 0.25 - 0.1035 = 0.5465 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{p} = (0.8194,\ 0.0000,\ 0.5465)\ \text{m}}
$$

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**Step 1 — Solve $\theta_1$:**

$$
\theta_1 = \text{atan2}(p_y, p_x) = \text{atan2}(0.0, 0.3) = 0°
$$

**Step 2 — Project into the r–z plane:**

$$
r = \sqrt{p_x^2 + p_y^2} = 0.3 \text{ m}, \qquad z' = p_z - d_1 = 0.85 - 0.4 = 0.45 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Cosine law for $\theta_3$:**

$$
\cos\theta_3 = \frac{r^2 + z'^2 - a_2^2 - a_3^2}{2 a_2 a_3} = \frac{0.09 + 0.2025 - 0.25 - 0.16}{2(0.5)(0.4)} = \frac{-0.1175}{0.4} = -0.2938
$$

$$
\theta_3 = \pm \cos^{-1}(-0.2938) = \pm 107.10°
$$

**Step 4 — Solve $\theta_2$:**

Using the planar-2R formula adapted to r–z:

$$
\theta_2 = \text{atan2}(z', r) - \text{atan2}(a_3 \sin\theta_3, a_2 + a_3\cos\theta_3)
$$

$\text{atan2}(0.45, 0.3) = 56.31°$.

Branch 1 ($\theta_3 = +107.10°$, $\sin\theta_3 = +0.9559$, $\cos\theta_3 = -0.2938$):
$\text{atan2}(0.4 \times 0.9559,\ 0.5 + 0.4(-0.2938)) = \text{atan2}(0.3824,\ 0.3825) = 45.00°$.
$\theta_2^{(1)} = 56.31° - 45.00° = 11.31°$.

Branch 2 ($\theta_3 = -107.10°$, $\sin\theta_3 = -0.9559$):
$\text{atan2}(-0.3824,\ 0.3825) = -45.00°$.
$\theta_2^{(2)} = 56.31° - (-45.00°) = 101.31°$.

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{Branch 1} &: (\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3) = (0°,\ 11.31°,\ 107.10°) \\
\text{Branch 2} &: (\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3) = (0°,\ 101.31°,\ -107.10°)
\end{aligned}
}
$$

**Step 5 — Singularity discussion:**

The 3R anthropomorphic arm is in a **boundary singularity** when the arm is **fully stretched** ($\theta_3 = 0$, $\det J = a_2 a_3 \sin\theta_3 = 0$) or **fully folded back** ($\theta_3 = \pm 180°$). At our target, $\theta_3 = \pm 107°$ — away from the singularity, so the Jacobian is full-rank and the IK is well-conditioned.

(Marks: 2 DH setup, 2 FK derivation, 2 IK closed form, 1 singularity discussion)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Using `atan` instead of `atan2`** → loses 2 marks and gives wrong quadrant in Branch-2 IK.
> 2. **Forgetting the reachability check** → loses 1 mark (the examiner explicitly looks for it in Part B).
> 3. **Mixing degrees and radians inside one calculation** → loses 2 marks. Convert everything to one unit **first**.
> 4. **Not stating the Jacobian singularity condition** $\det J = 0$ → loses the last mark in spatial IK problems.
> 5. **Skipping the alternative (elbow-down) branch** → KTU awards full marks only if both branches are listed.
> 6. **Wrong sign of $\sin\theta_2$** when squaring → silently puts both branches on the same side. Always derive $\sin\theta_2 = \pm\sqrt{1-\cos^2\theta_2}$ explicitly.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **DOF (planar)** = $3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$; **DOF (spatial)** = $6(n-1) - 5j_5 - 4j_4 - 3j_3 - 2j_2 - j_1$.
- **DH parameters**: $a_i, \alpha_i$ are geometric constants; $d_i$ (prismatic) or $\theta_i$ (revolute) is the joint variable.
- **FK = forward matrix multiplication**; **IK = solving non-linear trig equations** via cosine law + atan2.
- **Always use `atan2(y, x)`**, never `atan(y/x)`, in IK solutions.
- **Planar 2R FK**: $(x, y) = (a_1 c_1 + a_2 c_{12},\ a_1 s_1 + a_2 s_{12})$.
- **Planar 2R IK**: $\cos\theta_2 = \frac{x^2+y^2-a_1^2-a_2^2}{2 a_1 a_2}$; $\theta_1 = \text{atan2}(y,x) - \text{atan2}(a_2 s_2, a_1+a_2 c_2)$. Two branches via $\pm\sin\theta_2$.
- **Reachability**: $(a_1 - a_2)^2 \le x^2 + y^2 \le (a_1 + a_2)^2$ for planar 2R.
- **Spatial 3R FK**: $p_x = (a_2 c_2 + a_3 c_{23}) c_1$, $p_y = (a_2 c_2 + a_3 c_{23}) s_1$, $p_z = d_1 + a_2 s_2 + a_3 s_{23}$.
- **Spatial 3R IK**: $\theta_1 = \text{atan2}(p_y, p_x)$, then solve a 2R sub-problem in the $(r, z')$ plane with $r = \sqrt{p_x^2 + p_y^2}$.
- **Wrist-partition decoupling** (RRR + wrist): compute wrist centre, solve base 3R for position, last 3 joints for orientation.
- **SCARA (RRRP) IK**: decouples cleanly — planar 2R for $(x,y)$, sum of $d$'s for $z$, last revolute for orientation.
- **Singularity**: $\det J(\mathbf{q}) = 0$; corresponds to arm fully stretched or folded. Two-link IK degenerates to infinite solutions at the boundary.
- **Medical relevance**: FK powers VR overlays and CT-collision check; IK powers master–slave teleoperation and trajectory planning for surgical robots (Da Vinci, AESOP, neuro-stereotactic frames).
- **Worked numerical answers must show**: (i) DH table, (ii) formula, (iii) substitution, (iv) atan2 quadrant, (v) reachability check, (vi) all branches.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** *DH table → FK matrix multiply → extract pose for FK; target pose → reachability → cosine law → atan2 with quadrant awareness → both branches → singularity check for IK.* Print this on your revision card.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Jacobians, Velocity, and Forces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT724 - MEDICAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Jacobians, Velocity, and Forces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Jacobian in Robotics

The **Jacobian matrix** in medical robotics is a configuration-dependent linear mapping that relates the joint-space velocities of a serial manipulator to the corresponding linear and angular velocities of the end-effector (surgical instrument tip) in Cartesian task space.

Mathematically, for an *n*-joint manipulator operating in an *m*-dimensional task space:

$$\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}$$

where:
- $\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the spatial velocity vector (linear + angular stacked) of the end-effector,
- $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is the **geometric Jacobian**, a function of the current joint configuration $\mathbf{q}$,
- $\dot{\mathbf{q}} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the vector of joint velocities.

For a generic spatial manipulator, $m = 6$ (three linear and three angular components), so $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$ is a $6 \times n$ matrix.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Jacobian is the single most important mathematical object that bridges the **Joint Space** (where the surgeon commands movements) and the **Cartesian / Task Space** (where the surgical instrument actually moves inside the patient).

### 1.2 Linear and Angular Velocity of a Rigid Body

For any rigid body rotating with angular velocity $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ about a point *O*, the linear velocity of a point *P* located at position vector $\mathbf{r}_{OP}$ from *O* is given by the well-known rigid-body velocity equation:

$$\mathbf{v}_P = \mathbf{v}_O + \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{OP}$$

In robotic manipulators, this relation is applied repeatedly along the kinematic chain, from the base to the end-effector.

> [!NOTE]
> The angular velocity $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ of a rigid body is the same for **all points on the body**, regardless of the reference point chosen.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **laparoscopic surgical robot** (such as the *da Vinci* system). The surgeon sits at a console and moves two finger-grip master controllers. Behind the console, the robotic arms translate these tiny finger movements into precise instrument-tip motions inside the patient's abdomen.

- The **joints** of the robot (motor encoders) are the place where motions are *generated*.
- The **tip of the surgical instrument** is the place where motions *matter* clinically.
- The **Jacobian** is the *interpreter* — the mathematical dictionary that says: "If joint *i* moves one radian per second, the instrument tip will move this much (linear) and rotate this much (angular)."

Without the Jacobian, the robot would be deaf to the surgeon's intent; with it, motion is mapped **instantly and accurately**.

> [!TIP]
> Think of the Jacobian as the **gear ratio matrix** of the robot. Just as a bicycle gear ratio converts pedal revolutions into wheel revolutions, the Jacobian converts joint revolutions into tip translations and rotations.

### 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics (Standardised Values)

- **Joint velocity unit (revolute):** rad/s (typical surgical robot: **0 to 1.5 rad/s**)
- **Joint velocity unit (prismatic):** m/s
- **Linear velocity unit (Cartesian):** m/s
- **Angular velocity unit (Cartesian):** rad/s
- **Manipulability index** (Yoshikawa): $w = \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{T})}$, range $w \geq 0$
- **Condition number** $\kappa(\mathbf{J}) \geq 1$, with $\kappa = 1$ representing the *isotropic* (best dexterity) configuration.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Velocity Kinematics Pipeline

The relationship $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ is derived directly from differentiating the forward-kinematics position equation $\mathbf{x} = f(\mathbf{q})$ with respect to time.

- **Step 1** — Express end-effector pose as a function of joint variables.
- **Step 2** — Differentiate each Cartesian component with respect to time using the chain rule.
- **Step 3** — Collect the partial derivatives into a matrix — this matrix is the Jacobian.
- **Step 4** — Pre-multiply by the joint velocity vector to obtain task-space velocity.

### 2.2 Construction of the Jacobian (Column-by-Column Method)

For a **revolute joint** *i*, the *i*-th column of the Jacobian has the form:

$$\mathbf{J}_i = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{z}_{i-1} \times (\mathbf{o}_n - \mathbf{o}_{i-1}) \\ \mathbf{z}_{i-1} \end{bmatrix}$$

For a **prismatic joint** *i*, the *i*-th column is:

$$\mathbf{J}_i = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{z}_{i-1} \\ \mathbf{0} \end{bmatrix}$$

where:
- $\mathbf{z}_{i-1}$ is the unit vector along the axis of joint *i*,
- $\mathbf{o}_n$ is the position vector of the end-effector origin,
- $\mathbf{o}_{i-1}$ is the position vector of the origin of link *i-1*,
- $\mathbf{0}$ is the $3 \times 1$ zero vector.

### 2.3 Singularities — When the Jacobian Fails

A **singularity** occurs when the Jacobian loses full rank, i.e., $\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{T}) = 0$. At a singularity:

- The manipulator loses one or more degrees of freedom instantaneously.
- The end-effector cannot move in certain Cartesian directions.
- Infinitely large joint velocities are required to produce even a finite end-effector velocity (mathematically).
- The manipulator cannot resist forces in the null-space directions (control instability).

> [!WARNING]
> In surgical robotics, singularities are **safety-critical**. The trajectory planner must keep the manipulator *away* from singularities, or the safety controller must clamp joint velocities to avoid dangerous motions.

### 2.4 Static Force / Torque Relationship

When the manipulator is in **static equilibrium** (or quasi-static motion), the joint torques required to balance an external force $\mathbf{F}$ applied at the end-effector are given by the celebrated relation:

$$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^{T}(\mathbf{q})\,\mathbf{F}$$

This relation is derived from the **Principle of Virtual Work** and is the foundation of force feedback in haptic surgical systems.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table collects every equation you must memorise for the KTU 2024 ESE. Use the symbol `\vert` for absolute-value bars so the markdown table is not broken.

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Variables / Units | Notes |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | Velocity Kinematics | $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | $\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$, $\mathbf{J} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$, $\dot{\mathbf{q}} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ | Core relation, valid instantaneously |
| 2 | Jacobian definition | $J_{ij} = \partial x_i / \partial q_j$ | dimensionless or $m$, $rad$ depending on joint | Partial derivative form |
| 3 | Revolute joint linear column | $J_{v_i} = z_{i-1} \times (o_n - o_{i-1})$ | $m/rad$ | Cross-product construction |
| 4 | Revolute joint angular column | $J_{\omega_i} = z_{i-1}$ | $dimensionless$ | Unit axis vector |
| 5 | Prismatic joint linear column | $J_{v_i} = z_{i-1}$ | $dimensionless$ | Translation axis |
| 6 | Prismatic joint angular column | $J_{\omega_i} = 0$ | $dimensionless$ | No rotation |
| 7 | Static Force / Torque | $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^{T}(\mathbf{q})\mathbf{F}$ | $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ in $Nm$, $\mathbf{F}$ in $N$ | Virtual-work result |
| 8 | Singularity condition | $\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{T}) = 0$ | scalar $\geq 0$ | Loss of rank |
| 9 | Manipulability (Yoshikawa) | $w = \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{T})}$ | scalar $\geq 0$ | $w = 0$ at singularity |
| 10 | Condition number | $\kappa(\mathbf{J}) = \sigma_{\max} / \sigma_{\min}$ | $\kappa \geq 1$ | $\kappa = 1$ is isotropic |
| 11 | Inverse-velocity (non-singular) | $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}\mathbf{v}$ | valid when $m = n$ and $\det \mathbf{J} \neq 0$ | Square Jacobian case |
| 12 | Pseudo-inverse (redundant) | $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{\dagger}\mathbf{v}$ | $\mathbf{J}^{\dagger} = \mathbf{J}^{T}(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{T})^{-1}$ | $m < n$ redundant |
| 13 | Null-space velocity | $\dot{\mathbf{q}}_0 = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{J}^{\dagger}\mathbf{J})\mathbf{z}$ | arbitrary $\mathbf{z}$ | Self-motion (no tip motion) |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Surgical robotics (da Vinci, Versius, Hugo):** Real-time Jacobian is computed at 1 kHz inside the motion controller.
- **Haptic feedback devices:** $\mathbf{J}^{T}$ converts measured tip forces into motor torques for force rendering.
- **MRI-compatible robots:** Because Jacobian depends on geometry and not field, the same Jacobian framework is used inside the bore.
- **Rehabilitation exoskeletons:** Velocity mapping determines how patient intent maps to actuator speeds.
- **Neurosurgical micromanipulators:** The Jacobian governs tremor filtering — sub-millimetre tip velocities must be accurately commanded.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Velocity Relationship from Forward Kinematics

**Starting point:** End-effector position (a subset of Cartesian coordinates) is given by the forward-kinematics function

$$\mathbf{x} = f(\mathbf{q})$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate both sides with respect to time:**

$$\frac{d\mathbf{x}}{dt} = \frac{d f(\mathbf{q})}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the multivariate chain rule:**

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{q}} \cdot \frac{d\mathbf{q}}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the partial-derivative matrix as the Jacobian:**

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \dot{\mathbf{q}}$$

This is the **forward-velocity-kinematics** equation. It is exact and instantaneous — no small-angle approximation is required because the Jacobian is re-evaluated at every new configuration.

### 3.2 Derivation of Jacobian Column for a Revolute Joint

Consider the case where only joint *i* moves, with $\dot{q}_i = \delta$ and all other $\dot{q}_j = 0$ for $j \neq i$.

**Step 1 — Identify the rotation axis:** The instantaneous axis of rotation of link *i* is $\mathbf{z}_{i-1}$.

**Step 2 — Compute angular velocity contribution:**
All points on link *i* rotate about $\mathbf{z}_{i-1}$ at rate $\delta$. Hence the angular velocity of the end-effector due to this motion is:

$$\boldsymbol{\omega} = \mathbf{z}_{i-1}\,\delta$$

**Step 3 — Compute linear velocity contribution:** Using the rigid-body relation, the linear velocity of the end-effector origin is

$$\mathbf{v} = \boldsymbol{\omega} \times (\mathbf{o}_n - \mathbf{o}_{i-1}) = \mathbf{z}_{i-1} \times (\mathbf{o}_n - \mathbf{o}_{i-1})\,\delta$$

**Step 4 — Assemble the column:** Dividing by $\delta$ to obtain the Jacobian column:

$$\mathbf{J}_i = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{z}_{i-1} \times (\mathbf{o}_n - \mathbf{o}_{i-1}) \\ \mathbf{z}_{i-1} \end{bmatrix}$$

This is the canonical KTU-board-exam derivation. Every column of the Jacobian has this structure for revolute joints.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Static Force Relationship via Virtual Work

**Step 1 — State the Principle of Virtual Work:** For a manipulator in static equilibrium, the total virtual work done by all active forces must vanish.

$$\delta W = \mathbf{F}^{T}\,\delta\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\tau}^{T}\,\delta\mathbf{q} = 0$$

Here:
- $\mathbf{F}$ is the external force exerted by the end-effector on the environment,
- $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ is the vector of joint actuator torques,
- $\delta\mathbf{x}$ is a virtual Cartesian displacement,
- $\delta\mathbf{q}$ is the corresponding virtual joint displacement.

**Step 2 — Relate virtual displacements using the Jacobian:**

$$\delta\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\,\delta\mathbf{q}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the virtual-work equation:**

$$\mathbf{F}^{T}\mathbf{J}\,\delta\mathbf{q} - \boldsymbol{\tau}^{T}\,\delta\mathbf{q} = 0$$

$$\left(\mathbf{F}^{T}\mathbf{J} - \boldsymbol{\tau}^{T}\right)\delta\mathbf{q} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Conclude using arbitrariness of $\delta\mathbf{q}$:** Because $\delta\mathbf{q}$ can be any infinitesimal joint displacement (the system is unconstrained), the only way the equation can hold is for the coefficient to vanish:

$$\boldsymbol{\tau}^{T} = \mathbf{F}^{T}\mathbf{J}$$

$$\boxed{\;\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^{T}(\mathbf{q})\,\mathbf{F}\;}$$

This elegant transpose relationship is the cornerstone of **force control** and **haptic rendering** in medical robotics.

### 3.4 Worked Example — 2-Link Planar Manipulator Jacobian

Let the manipulator have joint angles $\theta_1, \theta_2$ and link lengths $L_1, L_2$.

**Forward Kinematics:**

$$\begin{aligned} x &= L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ y &= L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \end{aligned}$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate $x$ with respect to time:**

$$\dot{x} = -L_1\sin\theta_1\,\dot{\theta}_1 - L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate $y$ with respect to time:**

$$\dot{y} = L_1\cos\theta_1\,\dot{\theta}_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange into matrix form by grouping $\dot{\theta}_1$ and $\dot{\theta}_2$:**

$$\begin{aligned} \dot{x} &= \left[-L_1\sin\theta_1 - L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\right]\dot{\theta}_1 + \left[-L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\right]\dot{\theta}_2 \\ \dot{y} &= \left[L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)\right]\dot{\theta}_1 + \left[L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)\right]\dot{\theta}_2 \end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Read off the Jacobian matrix:**

$$\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1\sin\theta_1 - L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Verify singularity condition:** Compute the determinant:

$$\det(\mathbf{J}) = -L_1\sin\theta_1 \cdot L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) - (-L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2))(L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2))$$

$$\det(\mathbf{J}) = L_1 L_2 \sin\theta_2$$

Singularity arises when $\theta_2 = 0$ or $\theta_2 = \pi$ — the **fully stretched** and **fully folded** configurations. These are the classic *elbow singularities* of planar arms, exactly the same situation as the human elbow fully extended or fully bent.

### 3.5 Inverse Velocity Kinematics — Non-Singular Square Jacobian

For a 6-DOF manipulator (so $m = n = 6$ and $\mathbf{J}$ is square), when the configuration is non-singular:

$$\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}(\mathbf{q})\,\mathbf{v}$$

For redundant manipulators ($n > 6$), the Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse is used:

$$\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{\dagger}\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{J}^{T}(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{T})^{-1}\mathbf{v}$$

The **null-space projection** $(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{J}^{\dagger}\mathbf{J})$ allows additional self-motions that do not disturb the tip, used in surgical robots to avoid obstacles while keeping the instrument stationary.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Velocity Kinematics — Joint Space to Cartesian Space Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph JOINT["JOINT SPACE DOMAIN"]
        Q1[Desired Joint Position q]
        QD[Joint Velocity q_dot]
        QF[Joint Torques tau]
    end

    subgraph JACOBIAN["JACOBIAN OPERATOR"]
        JV[Geometric Jacobian J of q]
        JT[Jacobian Transpose J transpose of q]
    end

    subgraph TASK["CARTESIAN TASK SPACE"]
        XV[End Effector Linear and Angular Velocity v]
        XF[End Effector Force F]
        XS[End Effector Pose x]
    end

    Q1 --> FK[Forward Kinematics f of q] --> XS
    XS --> DIFF[Time Derivative d by dt] --> JV
    QD --> JV --> XV
    XF --> JT --> QF

    style JACOBIAN fill:#E6F2FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:2px
    style JOINT fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px
    style TASK fill:#E8F8E8,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture for Jacobian-Based Medical Robot Controller

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SURGEON[Surgeon Console Input]
    MAJ[Master Joint Encoders]
    VDES[Desired Tip Velocity v_des]
    JCOMP[Jacobian Computation J of q]
    JINV[Inverse Jacobian J inverse or Pseudo Inverse]
    QDOT[Joint Velocity Command q_dot]
    ACTUATOR[Robot Actuators]
    TIP[Surgical Instrument Tip]
    SENSE[Tip Force Sensor F_meas]
    JT2[Jacobian Transpose J transpose of q]
    TORQUE[Joint Torque Feedback tau]
    HAPTIC[Haptic Feedback Renderer]

    SURGEON --> MAJ --> VDES
    JCOMP --> JINV --> QDOT --> ACTUATOR --> TIP
    TIP --> SENSE --> JT2 --> TORQUE --> HAPTIC --> SURGEON

    style JCOMP fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF6347,stroke-width:2px
    style JINV fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF6347,stroke-width:2px
    style JT2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF6347,stroke-width:2px
    style HAPTIC fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Singularity Detection and Avoidance Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Read Current Joint Angles q]
    B[Compute Jacobian J of q]
    C[Compute Singular Values sigma_i]
    D{sigma_min less than threshold epsilon}
    E[Configuration is SAFE]
    F[SINGULARITY DETECTED]
    G[Compute Null Space Velocity]
    H[Clamp Joint Velocities]
    I[Trigger Replanning Module]
    J[Send Safe Velocity to Actuators]

    A --> B --> C --> D
    D -- No --> E --> J
    D -- Yes --> F --> G --> H --> I --> J

    style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:3px

```
