<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Jacobian matrix in the context of a robotic manipulator. Why is it said to be configuration-dependent?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Jacobian matrix $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$ is the matrix of first-order partial derivatives of the end-effector Cartesian coordinates with respect to the joint variables. It maps joint velocities to task-space velocities through $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$.

The Jacobian is *configuration-dependent* because its entries are functions of the joint variables $\mathbf{q}$. As the manipulator moves, the relative geometry between links changes, which alters the kinematic relationship between joint and Cartesian motions. Consequently, the same joint velocity produces different end-effector velocities at different configurations.

**(Award: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for configuration-dependence explanation.)**

---

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is a singular configuration of a robot manipulator? How is it detected mathematically?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A singular configuration is a specific joint position at which the manipulator loses one or more degrees of freedom instantaneously. Mathematically, it is detected by computing the determinant of the Jacobian (or the singular values of $\mathbf{J}$):

$$\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{T}) = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad \sigma_{\min}(\mathbf{J}) = 0$$

At singularities, the manipulator cannot move the end-effector in certain directions, and the joint velocities required to produce finite end-effector velocity become unbounded.

**(Award: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for mathematical condition, 1 mark for physical consequence.)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU ESE, each Part B question carries 14 marks and typically features two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B in the exam hall.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the Jacobian matrix for a 2-link planar manipulator with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$, joint angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$. Show clearly how each column is obtained. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Identify the singular configurations of this manipulator and explain what happens physically at these configurations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Forward Kinematics (1 mark):**

$$x = L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$$
$$y = L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$$

**Step 2 — Time differentiation (2 marks):**

$$\dot{x} = -L_1\sin\theta_1\,\dot{\theta}_1 - L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)$$
$$\dot{y} = L_1\cos\theta_1\,\dot{\theta}_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange into matrix form (2 marks):**

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1\sin\theta_1 - L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\theta}_1 \\ \dot{\theta}_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Read off the Jacobian (1 mark):**

$$\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1\sin\theta_1 - L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Verification via column method (1 mark):** Each column must also be derivable from $J_i = z_{i-1} \times (o_2 - o_{i-1})$ with $z_0 = [0,0,1]^T$, $o_1 = [L_1\cos\theta_1, L_1\sin\theta_1, 0]^T$, $o_2 = [x, y, 0]^T$.

**Part (b) — Singularities (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute the determinant (3 marks):**

$$\det(\mathbf{J}) = (-L_1\sin\theta_1)(L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)) - (-L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2))(L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2))$$

$$\det(\mathbf{J}) = -L_1 L_2 \sin\theta_1\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) + L_1 L_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\cos\theta_1 + L_2^2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$$

Using the identity $\sin(A)\cos(B) - \cos(A)\sin(B) = \sin(B-A)$:

$$\det(\mathbf{J}) = L_1 L_2 \sin\theta_2$$

**Step 2 — Set determinant to zero (1 mark):**

$$L_1 L_2 \sin\theta_2 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \theta_2 = 0 \text{ or } \theta_2 = \pi$$

**Step 3 — Physical interpretation (3 marks):**
- **$\theta_2 = 0$ — Arm fully stretched (outstretched configuration):** The two links align collinearly. The manipulator can no longer move the end-effector radially outward; it can only move tangentially. **One DOF is lost.**
- **$\theta_2 = \pi$ — Arm fully folded (doubled-back configuration):** The links overlap. The end-effector is at a fixed distance from the base equal to $\vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$. Again, **one DOF is lost.**

**Industrial / clinical implication:** At singularities, infinite joint velocities would be required to produce finite tip velocities, which is mechanically impossible. Surgical robots must plan trajectories that *avoid* these configurations or use damped least-squares inverse ($\mathbf{J}^{T}(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{T} + \lambda^2\mathbf{I})^{-1}$) to remain stable.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a clear diagram, explain how the Jacobian relates joint-space velocities to end-effector linear and angular velocities. Derive the column expression for a revolute joint. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from the Principle of Virtual Work, derive the relationship $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^{T}\mathbf{F}$ between joint torques and end-effector forces. Discuss its significance in haptic surgical systems. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Jacobian and Velocity Relationship (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Velocity relation statement (1 mark):**

$$\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{v} \\ \boldsymbol{\omega} \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}$$

**Step 2 — Forward kinematics statement (1 mark):** Position and orientation of the end-effector are functions $\mathbf{x} = f(\mathbf{q})$ and $\mathbf{R} = R(\mathbf{q})$; on differentiation with respect to time, both contribute to the spatial velocity $\mathbf{v}$.

**Step 3 — Diagram description (2 marks):** A 2-link or 3-link planar manipulator with joint axes $z_0, z_1, z_2$ and origins $o_0, o_1, o_2, o_3$ (end-effector) should be drawn. Each link rotates about its own joint axis, and the cumulative motion of the tip is the linear combination (matrix product) of the contributions.

**Step 4 — Derivation of revolute joint column (3 marks):**
For joint *i* rotating alone at $\dot{q}_i = \delta$, the angular velocity of link *i* (and hence of the end-effector) is $\mathbf{z}_{i-1}\,\delta$. Using the rigid-body relation $\mathbf{v}_P = \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{OP}$ with $P$ as the tip and $O$ as the origin of link *i-1*:

$$\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{z}_{i-1}\,\delta \times (\mathbf{o}_n - \mathbf{o}_{i-1})$$

Combining linear and angular parts, dividing by $\delta$:

$$\mathbf{J}_i = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{z}_{i-1} \times (\mathbf{o}_n - \mathbf{o}_{i-1}) \\ \mathbf{z}_{i-1} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Part (b) — Force / Torque Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — State Principle of Virtual Work (1 mark):**

$$\delta W = \mathbf{F}^{T}\,\delta\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\tau}^{T}\,\delta\mathbf{q} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Apply $\delta\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{J}\,\delta\mathbf{q}$ (1 mark):**

$$\mathbf{F}^{T}\mathbf{J}\,\delta\mathbf{q} - \boldsymbol{\tau}^{T}\,\delta\mathbf{q} = 0$$
$$(\mathbf{F}^{T}\mathbf{J} - \boldsymbol{\tau}^{T})\,\delta\mathbf{q} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Conclude using arbitrary $\delta\mathbf{q}$ (1 mark):**

$$\boldsymbol{\tau}^{T} = \mathbf{F}^{T}\mathbf{J} \;\Rightarrow\; \boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^{T}\mathbf{F}$$

**Step 4 — Significance in haptic surgical systems (4 marks):**
- **Bilateral feedback:** The force sensor at the instrument tip measures $\mathbf{F}$. The control computer multiplies by $\mathbf{J}^{T}$ to obtain motor torques $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ that the actuators must generate.
- **Transparency:** A good haptic system should feel *transparent* — the surgeon should feel the tissue directly. This requires accurate real-time computation of $\mathbf{J}^{T}$ at high rates (typically $\geq 1\,\text{kHz}$).
- **Scaling:** By inserting a scaling factor $k$ inside the force map (e.g., $\boldsymbol{\tau} = k\,\mathbf{J}^{T}\mathbf{F}$), motion can be **scaled down** while force is **scaled up** — useful in microsurgery.
- **Safety:** Force saturation limits can be enforced on $\boldsymbol{\tau}$, preventing tissue damage even if the surgeon's commands are excessive.
- **Tele-surgery latency compensation:** Modern systems predict the next $\mathbf{J}^{T}$ to compensate for communication delays between surgeon and remote robot.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks — Examiner's notes from past ESE papers:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the angular velocity block** — Many students write only the linear velocity part of the Jacobian column. For spatial manipulators, the full 6×n matrix must include $J_{\omega_i}$. **(−2 marks typical loss)**
> 2. **Mixing $z_{i-1}$ and $z_i$** — The axis used in the Jacobian column is **always the axis at the *previous* link** $i-1$, not the current link $i$. **(−1 to 2 marks)**
> 3. **Skipping determinant step for singularity** — Students often *state* that $\det \mathbf{J} = 0$ but never *derive* the determinant. The algebraic simplification using trigonometric identities is the main marking step. **(−3 marks)**
> 4. **Wrong direction in force relation** — A very common mistake is writing $\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{J}^{T}\boldsymbol{\tau}$. The transpose is on the *Jacobian*, not on the force vector. The correct relation is $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^{T}\mathbf{F}$.
> 5. **Missing units** — Always quote units: Jacobian columns for revolute joints are in $\text{m/rad}$, torques in $\text{Nm}$, forces in $\text{N}$. The KTU rubric awards half a mark for unit correctness.
> 6. **No physical interpretation of singularities** — Singularities are not just algebraic curiosities. You must state *what the manipulator cannot do* at those configurations.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Core relation:** $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ — joint velocity to tip velocity mapping.
- **Jacobian is configuration-dependent:** re-evaluated every controller cycle.
- **Revolute column:** $J_i = \begin{bmatrix} z_{i-1} \times (o_n - o_{i-1}) \\ z_{i-1} \end{bmatrix}$.
- **Prismatic column:** $J_i = \begin{bmatrix} z_{i-1} \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$.
- **Singularity:** $\det(\mathbf{J}) = 0$ — manipulator loses DOF, infinite joint velocities possible, force production fails in null directions.
- **Manipulability:** $w = \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{T})}$ — Yoshikawa's dexterity index; $w \to 0$ near singularities.
- **Isotropy:** $\kappa(\mathbf{J}) = 1$ — best manipulation capability, equal in all directions.
- **Static force equation:** $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^{T}(\mathbf{q})\mathbf{F}$ — derived from Virtual Work.
- **Inverse velocity:** $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}\mathbf{v}$ (square) or $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{\dagger}\mathbf{v}$ (redundant).
- **Null-space velocity:** $\dot{\mathbf{q}}_0 = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{J}^{\dagger}\mathbf{J})\mathbf{z}$ — produces self-motion without tip motion, used to avoid obstacles in surgery.
- **Medical-robot relevance:** Jacobian governs tip velocity mapping for surgical instruments, force feedback via $\mathbf{J}^{T}$ enables haptic rendering, and singularity avoidance is a safety-critical requirement for instruments operating inside the human body.
- **Units mantra:** Revolute $J$ in $\text{m/rad}$ and $\text{rad/rad}$; prismatic $J$ in $\text{m/m}$ and $\text{rad/m}$; $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ in $\text{rad/s}$ or $\text{m/s}$; $\mathbf{v}$ in $\text{m/s}$ and $\text{rad/s}$; $\mathbf{F}$ in $\text{N}$; $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ in $\text{Nm}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Manipulator Dynamics and Trajectory Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT724 - MEDICAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Manipulator Dynamics and Trajectory Generation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Manipulator Dynamics?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
> *Manipulator Dynamics* is the study of the mathematical relationships between the **forces/torques** applied at the robot joints and the resulting **motion (positions, velocities, and accelerations)** of the manipulator's links. It is formally expressed by the **generalized dynamic equation of motion**:*
> $$\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$$
> *where the matrices and vectors represent the manipulator's inertial, Coriolis/centrifugal, and gravitational properties respectively.*

Unlike **kinematics**, which only describes *motion without considering forces*, dynamics answers the deeper engineering question: **"How much motor torque is required to move a surgical robot arm along a desired path without vibration, overshoot, or instability?"**

### Conceptual Analogy — The Surgeon's Hand

Imagine a surgeon performing a **laparoscopic procedure**. The surgeon does not simply *place* the instrument at the operating site — they apply carefully controlled forces that depend on:
1. The **weight** of the instrument (gravity),
2. The **speed** of movement (inertia),
3. The **direction change** at corners (centrifugal effects).

The manipulator is a mechanical extension of this hand. Its dynamic model mathematically captures *exactly* how these physical effects propagate through each joint and link, enabling the controller to compensate for them in real-time.

---

## 1.2 What is Trajectory Generation?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Trajectory Generation* is the computational process of producing a **time-parameterized reference path** — a sequence of joint positions, velocities, and accelerations — that the manipulator must follow to move smoothly from an **initial pose** to a **final pose**, while respecting **kinematic constraints** (velocity, acceleration, jerk limits) and **task constraints** (e.g., avoiding critical anatomical structures in surgery).

In medical robotics, trajectory generation is the bridge between **surgical planning** (a static geometric path) and **real-time motion control** (continuous smooth execution). The trajectory must be **smooth, predictable, and bounded** to guarantee patient safety.

### Conceptual Analogy — GPS Navigation for Surgery

Think of **Google Maps** giving you turn-by-turn directions. The route is *static*, but the navigation app computes **when** you should be at each waypoint, at what speed, and with what acceleration/deceleration — so you arrive smoothly without harsh braking. Trajectory generation is the **surgical equivalent**: it converts a planned surgical path into a **time-stamped, jerk-limited motion plan**.

---

## 1.3 Why Are These Topics Critical for Medical Robotics?

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Reasons (KTU High-Yield Points):**
> - **Patient Safety**: A surgical robot cutting near a nerve or artery cannot tolerate sudden jerks; dynamics-aware trajectory planning prevents this.
> - **Haptic Feedback Realism**: Accurate dynamics models allow the surgeon to *feel* realistic interaction forces through the master console.
> - **Reduced Tremor**: Dynamic compensation filters out the natural hand tremor of surgeons (frequency **~8–12 Hz**).
> - **Teleoperation Stability**: The master–slave communication delay is bridged by predictive dynamics models.
> - **FDA Compliance**: Regulatory bodies demand deterministic, verifiable motion — only achievable via formal dynamic models.

### The Two Pillars — A Snapshot

| Aspect | Manipulator Dynamics | Trajectory Generation |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Question** | What torques produce a desired motion? | What motion respects kinematic/time constraints? |
| **Mathematical Core** | Euler-Lagrange / Newton-Euler equations | Polynomial interpolation (cubic, quintic, splines) |
| **Key Inputs** | Joint positions, velocities, accelerations | Initial pose, final pose, time bounds, jerk limits |
| **Key Outputs** | Joint torques $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ | Reference $\mathbf{q}(t), \dot{\mathbf{q}}(t), \ddot{\mathbf{q}}(t)$ |
| **Application in Surgery** | Motor control of da Vinci-style arms | Smooth needle insertion, suturing paths |

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry-Standard Metrics for Surgical Robots:**
> - **Positional accuracy**: $\pm \mathbf{0.1\ mm}$ (modern systems like da Vinci Xi)
> - **Repeatability**: $\pm \mathbf{0.05\ mm}$
> - **Maximum joint velocity (typical)**: $\mathbf{1.0\text{–}2.0\ rad/s}$
> - **Maximum joint acceleration**: $\mathbf{5\text{–}20\ rad/s^2}$
> - **Jerk limit (safety)**: $\mathbf{\le 50\ m/s^3}$ in Cartesian space
> - **Human hand physiological tremor frequency**: $\mathbf{8\text{–}12\ Hz}$ (used to design low-pass filtering)
> - **Sampling frequency of modern surgical controllers**: $\mathbf{1\text{–}10\ kHz}$
> - **Standard gravitational acceleration**: $\mathbf{g = 9.81\ m/s^2}$

---

## 1.5 Taxonomy of Approaches

### Dynamics Approaches

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Lagrangian (Euler-Lagrange) Formulation** — Energy-based; systematic but computationally heavy for $n \ge 6$.
> 2. **Newton-Euler Formulation** — Force-balance on each link; recursive, computationally efficient.
> 3. **Generalized D'Alembert Principle** — Combines virtual work with Newtonian mechanics.
> 4. **Kane's Equations** — Compact form, used in aerospace-style robotic dynamics.

### Trajectory Generation Approaches

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Joint-Space Planning** — Independent polynomial interpolation per joint.
> 2. **Cartesian-Space Planning** — Path in task space, then inverse kinematics.
> 3. **Point-to-Point Motion** — Single segment (e.g., cubic/quintic polynomial).
> 4. **Path with Via Points** — Multi-segment trajectory stitching.
> 5. **Trapezoidal / S-Curve Velocity Profiles** — Industrial standard.

This taxonomy forms the **KTU Module 2 assessment backbone**.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Generalized Dynamic Equation — Anatomy of the Equation

The **fundamental equation of manipulator dynamics** is the second-order nonlinear matrix differential equation:

$$\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) + \mathbf{F}(\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$$

Each term has a precise physical interpretation that the KTU examiner frequently tests.

### Term-by-Term Breakdown

> [!NOTE]
> **$\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ — The Mass (Inertia) Matrix**
> - **Symmetric**: $\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{M}^T$
> - **Positive definite**: $\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{M}\mathbf{x} > 0 \ \forall \mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$
> - Represents the **instantaneous inertia** felt at the joints as a function of configuration.
> - Depends *only* on position, not velocity.
> - In a surgical robot, $\mathbf{M}$ changes dramatically when the robot is folded vs. extended.

> [!NOTE]
> **$\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ — Coriolis and Centrifugal Forces**
> - $\mathbf{C}$ is **NOT unique** (depends on choice of parameterization via *Christoffel symbols*).
> - $\dot{\mathbf{M}} - 2\mathbf{C}$ is **skew-symmetric** for a proper formulation (used in passivity proofs).
> - Arises only when **joints are moving** — vanishes if $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{0}$.
> - Centrifugal: effect from angular velocity $\omega^2$ term.
> - Coriolis: coupling between joint velocities $\dot{q}_i \dot{q}_j$.

> [!NOTE]
> **$\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ — Gravitational Torque Vector**
> - Function of joint positions only.
> - Determines the **static holding torque** when $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \ddot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{0}$.
> - In medical robots, gravity compensation allows the surgeon to *let go* of the master and the slave stays in place.

> [!NOTE]
> **$\mathbf{F}(\dot{\mathbf{q}}) \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ — Friction Vector**
> - Viscous friction: $F_v \dot{q}$
> - Coulomb friction: $F_c \ \text{sgn}(\dot{q})$
> - Static friction (Stiction): $F_s \ \text{sgn}(\dot{q})$ for $|\dot{q}| \le \epsilon$
> - Critical for *haptic fidelity* — wrong friction models break the surgeon's "feel."

> [!NOTE]
> **$\boldsymbol{\tau} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ — Applied Joint Torques**
> - The control input vector.
> - In medical robots: motor torques, possibly augmented by remote-center-of-motion (RCM) constraints.

---

## 2.2 The Euler-Lagrange Derivation Path (Logic Flow)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Lagrangian Mechanics Recipe:**
> 1. Define the **kinetic energy** $K$ of the system as a function of $\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}$.
> 2. Define the **potential energy** $P$ as a function of $\mathbf{q}$.
> 3. Construct the **Lagrangian**: $\mathcal{L} = K - P$.
> 4. Apply the **Euler-Lagrange equation** for each generalized coordinate $q_i$:
>    $$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$$
> 5. Rearrange to the standard form: $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G} = \boldsymbol{\tau}$.

### Why the "Why" Matters

- The Lagrangian is **energy-based**, which means it handles **rotational inertia** elegantly (no need to track angular momentum vectors explicitly).
- It guarantees that **internal forces cancel** by construction — only actuator forces remain on the RHS.
- It naturally yields a **symmetric inertia matrix**, simplifying many proofs.

---

## 2.3 Newton's-Euler Formulation (The Alternative Path)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recursive Form — The Standard Approach in Real-Time Control:**
> - **Forward recursion** (link 1 to $n$): Compute link velocities, accelerations, and inertial forces.
> - **Backward recursion** (link $n$ to 1): Propagate forces and torques back to the joints.
>
> **Complexity**: $O(n)$ operations per recursion — much faster than the $O(n^4)$ symbolic complexity of the Euler-Lagrange method.

For a **single rigid link** of mass $m_i$, the Newton-Euler equations in the link frame $\{i\}$ are:

$$\mathbf{F}_i = m_i \mathbf{a}_{c_i}$$

$$\boldsymbol{\tau}_i = {}^{i}\mathbf{I}_i \boldsymbol{\alpha}_i + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \left({}^{i}\mathbf{I}_i \boldsymbol{\omega}_i\right)$$

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER TABLE — All Critical Equations for KTU Module 2**

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Generalized equation of motion | $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G} + \mathbf{F} = \boldsymbol{\tau}$ | $\boldsymbol{\tau} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| Lagrangian | $\mathcal{L} = K - P$ | Energy in Joules (J) |
| Euler-Lagrange (scalar form) | $\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$ | For each $i = 1 \dots n$ |
| Element of $\mathbf{M}$ | $M_{ij} = \sum_{k=\max(i,j)}^{n} \text{tr}\!\left(\frac{\partial {}^{0}\mathbf{T}_k}{\partial q_i} \mathbf{I}_k \frac{\partial ({}^{0}\mathbf{T}_k)^T}{\partial q_j}\right)$ | Symbolic, $O(n^4)$ |
| Christoffel symbol of 1st kind | $c_{ijk} = \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k} + \frac{\partial M_{ik}}{\partial q_j} - \frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\right)$ | Used to build $\mathbf{C}$ |
| Coriolis element | $C_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} c_{ijk} \dot{q}_k$ | Skew-symmetry property: $\dot{\mathbf{M}} = \mathbf{C} + \mathbf{C}^T$ |
| Gravity vector element | $G_i = -\sum_{j=1}^{n} m_j \mathbf{g}^T \frac{\partial {}^{0}\mathbf{r}_{c_j}}{\partial q_i}$ | $\mathbf{g} = [0, 0, -9.81]^T$ |
| Newton-Euler (translation) | $\mathbf{F}_i = m_i (\dot{\mathbf{v}}_i + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \mathbf{v}_i)$ | $m_i$ in kg |
| Newton-Euler (rotation) | $\boldsymbol{\tau}_i = {}^{i}\mathbf{I}_i \dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_i + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times ({}^{i}\mathbf{I}_i \boldsymbol{\omega}_i)$ | ${}^{i}\mathbf{I}_i$ at center of mass |
| Viscous friction model | $F_i = b_i \dot{q}_i$ | $b_i$ in N·m·s/rad |
| Coulomb friction model | $F_i = \mu_i \,\text{sgn}(\dot{q}_i)$ | $\mu_i$ in N·m |
| **Cubic polynomial trajectory** | $q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ | 4 constraints → 4 coefficients |
| **Cubic polynomial coefficient matrix** | $\begin{bmatrix} q_0 \\ q_f \\ \dot{q}_0 \\ \dot{q}_f \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & t_0 & t_0^2 & t_0^3 \\ 1 & t_f & t_f^2 & t_f^3 \\ 0 & 1 & 2t_0 & 3t_0^2 \\ 0 & 1 & 2t_f & 3t_f^2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_0 \\ a_1 \\ a_2 \\ a_3 \end{bmatrix}$ | Boundary-value system |
| **Quintic polynomial trajectory** | $q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 + a_4 t^4 + a_5 t^5$ | 6 constraints → 6 coefficients |
| **Linear segment with parabolic blend (LSPB)** | Trapezoidal velocity profile | Used for constant-velocity motion |
| Trapezoidal velocity peaks | $v_{\text{peak}} = \dfrac{q_f - q_0}{t_f - \tfrac{t_a}{2} - \tfrac{t_d}{2}}$ | $t_a, t_d$ = accel/decel times |
| Cubic jerk (3rd derivative) | $\dddot{q}(t) = 6 a_3$ | Constant for cubic |
| Quintic jerk (3rd derivative) | $\dddot{q}(t) = 6 a_3 + 24 a_4 t + 60 a_5 t^2$ | Time-varying, smoother |

> [!WARNING]
> **KRU Exam Pitfall:** When writing boundary conditions for cubic polynomials, students often confuse the time index — **$t_0$ is usually set to 0** for simplicity. If $t_0 = 0$, then $a_0 = q_0$ and $a_1 = \dot{q}_0$ directly, eliminating two rows of computation.

---

## 2.5 Trajectory Generation Methods — Deep Dive

### 2.5.1 Joint-Space Trajectory Planning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The path is interpolated **in joint coordinates**. Each joint has an independent time history $\mathbf{q}(t)$.
>
> **Advantages:**
> - No kinematic singularities during interpolation (joint variables are decoupled).
> - Trivially maps to motor commands.
> - Easy to enforce per-joint velocity/acceleration limits.
>
> **Disadvantages:**
> - The Cartesian path is **not a straight line** — unpredictable for the surgeon.
> - Unintended Cartesian motions may collide with anatomy.

### 2.5.2 Cartesian-Space Trajectory Planning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The path is interpolated **in task (Cartesian) coordinates**, then converted to joint variables via **inverse kinematics** at each time step.
>
> **Advantages:**
> - Predictable geometric path (straight lines, arcs between waypoints).
> - Surgeon sees the *exact* trajectory in the surgical scene.
>
> **Disadvantages:**
> - Computationally expensive (IK must run at every time step).
> - Risk of **singularities** — IK may fail or produce discontinuous joint trajectories.

### 2.5.3 Cubic Polynomial Trajectory (The KTU Workhorse)

**Setup:** Given initial position $q_0$, final position $q_f$, initial velocity $\dot{q}_0$, final velocity $\dot{q}_f$, and total time $t_f$ (with $t_0 = 0$).

$$q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$$

$$\dot{q}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2$$

**Boundary conditions** give the linear system:

$$q(0) = q_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a_0 = q_0$$

$$\dot{q}(0) = \dot{q}_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a_1 = \dot{q}_0$$

$$q(t_f) = q_f \quad \Rightarrow \quad a_0 + a_1 t_f + a_2 t_f^2 + a_3 t_f^3 = q_f$$

$$\dot{q}(t_f) = \dot{q}_f \quad \Rightarrow \quad a_1 + 2 a_2 t_f + 3 a_3 t_f^2 = \dot{q}_f$$

**Closed-form solutions** (with $t_0 = 0$):

$$a_2 = \frac{3(q_f - q_0) - \dot{q}_f t_f - 2 \dot{q}_0 t_f}{t_f^2}$$

$$a_3 = \frac{-2(q_f - q_0) + (\dot{q}_0 + \dot{q}_f) t_f}{t_f^3}$$

### 2.5.4 Quintic Polynomial Trajectory

Used when **jerk continuity** is required (jerk is the third derivative of position; in surgical robotics, jerk is the primary cause of tissue damage). Six constraints: $q_0, \dot{q}_0, \ddot{q}_0, q_f, \dot{q}_f, \ddot{q}_f$. This makes $\ddot{q}(t)$ continuous at waypoints, which the cubic polynomial cannot guarantee.

### 2.5.5 Linear Segments with Parabolic Blends (LSPB / Trapezoidal Profile)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Trapezoidal Velocity Profile — Industrial Standard:**
> - **Phase 1** (duration $t_a$): Linear acceleration from 0 to $v_{\text{peak}}$.
> - **Phase 2** (duration $t_v$): Constant velocity $v_{\text{peak}}$.
> - **Phase 3** (duration $t_d$): Linear deceleration from $v_{\text{peak}}$ to 0.
> - **Total time**: $t_f = t_a + t_v + t_d$.
>
> **Trade-off**: Simple, but **acceleration is discontinuous** at the transitions — causes jerk spikes. The **S-curve profile** fixes this.

### 2.5.6 Why Quintic > Cubic > LSPB for Surgery

| Criterion | LSPB | Cubic | Quintic |
|---|---|---|---|
| Position continuity | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Velocity continuity | ✗ (jumps) | ✓ | ✓ |
| Acceleration continuity | ✗ | ✗ (jumps) | ✓ |
| Jerk continuity | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ |
| Computation cost | Low | Low | Medium |
| Use in surgery | Rare | Common | **Preferred for safety** |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where These Equations Appear in the Real World:**
> - **Surgical robot controllers** (e.g., Intuitive Surgical's da Vinci) solve the inverse dynamics equation **thousands of times per second** to compute feedforward torques.
> - **Path planners for radiation therapy** (CyberKnife) use quintic polynomials to move the LINAC smoothly between beam angles.
> - **Rehabilitation robots** (Lokomat) use dynamics models to estimate patient-intended torques and assist proportionally.
> - **Capsule endoscopy** locomotion uses dynamics to model magnetically-coupled external fields.
> - **Haptic rendering** in virtual surgical simulators relies on accurate dynamics to compute realistic contact forces for training.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Euler-Lagrange Equation for a Simple 2-DOF Planar Manipulator

This is the **KTU Module 2 prototype problem**. Mastering it gives you the template for any manipulator.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider a **2-DOF planar manipulator** in the horizontal plane (so gravity is negligible in the vertical direction). The two links have lengths $l_1, l_2$, point masses $m_1, m_2$ at the distal ends, and joint angles $q_1, q_2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumptions:**
> - Point-mass model (mass concentrated at link ends).
> - Planar motion — no gravitational torque in the plane.
> - Frictionless joints (we will add viscous friction at the end).

### 3.1.2 Position of Mass Centers

Using forward kinematics (denavit-Hartenberg convention):

$$x_1 = l_1 \cos(q_1)$$

$$y_1 = l_1 \sin(q_1)$$

$$x_2 = l_1 \cos(q_1) + l_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2)$$

$$y_2 = l_1 \sin(q_1) + l_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2)$$

### 3.1.3 Velocities (Time Derivatives)

$$\dot{x}_1 = -l_1 \sin(q_1)\dot{q}_1$$

$$\dot{y}_1 = l_1 \cos(q_1)\dot{q}_1$$

$$\dot{x}_2 = -l_1 \sin(q_1)\dot{q}_1 - l_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2)(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)$$

$$\dot{y}_2 = l_1 \cos(q_1)\dot{q}_1 + l_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2)(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)$$

### 3.1.4 Kinetic Energy of Each Link

For point masses, $K_i = \tfrac{1}{2} m_i (\dot{x}_i^2 + \dot{y}_i^2)$.

**Link 1:**

$$K_1 = \frac{1}{2} m_1 \left(l_1^2 \sin^2(q_1) \dot{q}_1^2 + l_1^2 \cos^2(q_1) \dot{q}_1^2\right)$$

Using $\sin^2 + \cos^2 = 1$:

$$K_1 = \frac{1}{2} m_1 l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2$$

**Link 2:** (Expand using $\dot{x}_2^2 + \dot{y}_2^2$ — this is the key step)

$$K_2 = \frac{1}{2} m_2 \Big[\big(l_1^2 + l_2^2 + 2l_1 l_2 \cos(q_2)\big)\dot{q}_1^2 + l_2^2 \dot{q}_2^2 + 2\big(l_2^2 + l_1 l_2 \cos(q_2)\big)\dot{q}_1 \dot{q}_2\Big]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation Note (Expansion Logic):**
> - Square $\dot{x}_2$: get terms $l_1^2 \sin^2(q_1)\dot{q}_1^2$ and $l_2^2 \sin^2(q_1+q_2)(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)^2$ plus a cross-term $2l_1 l_2 \sin(q_1)\sin(q_1+q_2)\dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)$.
> - Square $\dot{y}_2$: similar with cosines.
> - Add them. The cross-terms combine: $2\sin(q_1)\sin(q_1+q_2) + 2\cos(q_1)\cos(q_1+q_2) = 2\cos(q_2)$ using $\cos(A-B)$ identity.
> - Pure $\dot{q}_2^2$ terms yield $l_2^2 \dot{q}_2^2$.

### 3.1.5 Total Kinetic Energy

$$K = K_1 + K_2$$

$$K = \frac{1}{2} m_1 l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2 + \frac{1}{2} m_2 \left[\left(l_1^2 + l_2^2 + 2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2\right)\dot{q}_1^2 + l_2^2 \dot{q}_2^2 + 2\left(l_2^2 + l_1 l_2 \cos q_2\right)\dot{q}_1 \dot{q}_2\right]$$

Combining $\dot{q}_1^2$ coefficients:

$$K = \frac{1}{2}\left[m_1 l_1^2 + m_2\left(l_1^2 + l_2^2 + 2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2\right)\right]\dot{q}_1^2 + \frac{1}{2} m_2 l_2^2 \dot{q}_2^2 + m_2 \left(l_2^2 + l_1 l_2 \cos q_2\right)\dot{q}_1 \dot{q}_2$$

### 3.1.6 Potential Energy

Since motion is in the **horizontal plane**, gravity does no work:

$$P = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathcal{L} = K - P = K$$

### 3.1.7 Apply Euler-Lagrange for Joint 1

Compute each piece:

$$\frac{\partial K}{\partial \dot{q}_1} = \left[m_1 l_1^2 + m_2\left(l_1^2 + l_2^2 + 2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2\right)\right]\dot{q}_1 + m_2 \left(l_2^2 + l_1 l_2 \cos q_2\right)\dot{q}_2$$

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial K}{\partial \dot{q}_1}\right) = \left[m_1 l_1^2 + m_2\left(l_1^2 + l_2^2 + 2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2\right)\right]\ddot{q}_1 - 2 m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_1 \dot{q}_2 + m_2 \left(l_2^2 + l_1 l_2 \cos q_2\right)\ddot{q}_2 - m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_2^2$$

$$\frac{\partial K}{\partial q_1} = 0 \quad \text{(since } K \text{ has no } q_1 \text{ dependence in the horizontal plane)}$$

Substituting into Euler-Lagrange:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial K}{\partial \dot{q}_1}\right) - \frac{\partial K}{\partial q_1} = \tau_1$$

### 3.1.8 Apply Euler-Lagrange for Joint 2

$$\frac{\partial K}{\partial \dot{q}_2} = m_2 l_2^2 \dot{q}_2 + m_2 \left(l_2^2 + l_1 l_2 \cos q_2\right)\dot{q}_1$$

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial K}{\partial \dot{q}_2}\right) = m_2 l_2^2 \ddot{q}_2 + m_2 \left(l_2^2 + l_1 l_2 \cos q_2\right)\ddot{q}_1 - m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_2 \dot{q}_1$$

$$\frac{\partial K}{\partial q_2} = -m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_1^2 - m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_1 \dot{q}_2$$

Substituting and simplifying (with $\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 = \dot{q}_2 \dot{q}_1$):

$$\tau_2 = m_2 l_2^2 \ddot{q}_2 + m_2 \left(l_2^2 + l_1 l_2 \cos q_2\right)\ddot{q}_1 - m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_1 \dot{q}_2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_1^2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_1 \dot{q}_2$$

The $-m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_1 \dot{q}_2$ and $+m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_1 \dot{q}_2$ cancel:

$$\tau_2 = m_2 l_2^2 \ddot{q}_2 + m_2\left(l_2^2 + l_1 l_2 \cos q_2\right)\ddot{q}_1 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_1^2$$

### 3.1.9 Final Matrix Form — The Equation of Motion

Gathering $\ddot{q}_1$ and $\ddot{q}_2$ terms across both equations:

$$\boxed{\;\begin{bmatrix} m_1 l_1^2 + m_2(l_1^2 + l_2^2 + 2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2) & m_2(l_2^2 + l_1 l_2 \cos q_2) \\ m_2(l_2^2 + l_1 l_2 \cos q_2) & m_2 l_2^2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \ddot{q}_1 \\ \ddot{q}_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} -2 m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_1 \dot{q}_2 - m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_2^2 \\ m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_1^2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \tau_1 \\ \tau_2 \end{bmatrix}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Result:**
> - The **$2\times 2$ matrix** is $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ — symmetric, positive definite, and configuration-dependent.
> - The **nonlinear velocity-product vector** is $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$.
> - $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \mathbf{0}$ (horizontal plane).
> - The result perfectly matches the general form $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \boldsymbol{\tau}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Cubic Polynomial Trajectory Coefficients

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

Given:
- Initial position $q_0 = q(0)$
- Final position $q_f = q(t_f)$
- Initial velocity $\dot{q}_0 = \dot{q}(0)$
- Final velocity $\dot{q}_f = \dot{q}(t_f)$

Find coefficients $a_0, a_1, a_2, a_3$ of the cubic polynomial $q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Apply $q(0) = q_0$:

$$a_0 + a_1(0) + a_2(0)^2 + a_3(0)^3 = q_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{a_0 = q_0}$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate: $\dot{q}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2$.

Apply $\dot{q}(0) = \dot{q}_0$:

$$a_1 + 0 + 0 = \dot{q}_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{a_1 = \dot{q}_0}$$

**Step 3:** Apply $q(t_f) = q_f$:

$$q_0 + \dot{q}_0 t_f + a_2 t_f^2 + a_3 t_f^3 = q_f$$

$$a_2 t_f^2 + a_3 t_f^3 = q_f - q_0 - \dot{q}_0 t_f \qquad \text{(Equation A)}$$

**Step 4:** Apply $\dot{q}(t_f) = \dot{q}_f$:

$$\dot{q}_0 + 2 a_2 t_f + 3 a_3 t_f^2 = \dot{q}_f$$

$$2 a_2 t_f + 3 a_3 t_f^2 = \dot{q}_f - \dot{q}_0 \qquad \text{(Equation B)}$$

**Step 5:** Solve the 2-equation system (A and B) for $a_2, a_3$.

Multiply Equation A by $2/t_f$:

$$2 a_2 t_f + 2 a_3 t_f^2 = \frac{2(q_f - q_0 - \dot{q}_0 t_f)}{t_f} \qquad \text{(A')}$$

Subtract (A') from (B):

$$a_3 t_f^2 = (\dot{q}_f - \dot{q}_0) - \frac{2(q_f - q_0 - \dot{q}_0 t_f)}{t_f}$$

$$a_3 t_f^2 = \frac{(\dot{q}_f - \dot{q}_0)t_f - 2(q_f - q_0) + 2\dot{q}_0 t_f}{t_f}$$

$$a_3 t_f^2 = \frac{\dot{q}_f t_f + \dot{q}_0 t_f - 2(q_f - q_0)}{t_f}$$

$$\boxed{a_3 = \frac{-2(q_f - q_0) + (\dot{q}_0 + \dot{q}_f) t_f}{t_f^3}}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $a_2$ from Equation A:

$$a_2 t_f^2 = (q_f - q_0) - \dot{q}_0 t_f - a_3 t_f^3$$

Substitute $a_3$:

$$a_2 t_f^2 = (q_f - q_0) - \dot{q}_0 t_f - \frac{[-2(q_f - q_0) + (\dot{q}_0 + \dot{q}_f) t_f]}{t_f^3} \cdot t_f^3$$

$$a_2 t_f^2 = (q_f - q_0) - \dot{q}_0 t_f + 2(q_f - q_0) - (\dot{q}_0 + \dot{q}_f) t_f$$

$$a_2 t_f^2 = 3(q_f - q_0) - (2\dot{q}_0 + \dot{q}_f) t_f$$

$$\boxed{a_2 = \frac{3(q_f - q_0) - (2\dot{q}_0 + \dot{q}_f) t_f}{t_f^2}}$$

### 3.2.3 Compact Symbolic Form

$$\begin{bmatrix} a_0 \\ a_1 \\ a_2 \\ a_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ \dfrac{3}{t_f^2} & \dfrac{-2}{t_f} & \dfrac{-1}{t_f} & 0 \\ \dfrac{-2}{t_f^3} & \dfrac{1}{t_f^2} & \dfrac{1}{t_f^2} & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} q_0 \\ \dot{q}_0 \\ q_f \\ \dot{q}_f \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Verification:** Substitute $\dot{q}_0 = \dot{q}_f = 0$ (a "rest-to-rest" motion, the most common case in point-to-point surgical moves). Then:
> $$a_2 = \frac{3(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^2}, \quad a_3 = \frac{-2(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^3}$$
> Acceleration: $\ddot{q}(t) = 2 a_2 + 6 a_3 t$. At $t = 0$: $\ddot{q}(0) = \frac{6(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^2}$. At $t = t_f$: $\ddot{q}(t_f) = -\frac{6(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^2}$.
> The **acceleration jumps** abruptly from a positive value to a negative value — confirming that the cubic polynomial has **discontinuous jerk** (and is therefore not ideal for surgery).

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Cubic Trajectory for a Surgical Joint

**Problem:** A surgical robot joint must move from $q_0 = 0.2\ \text{rad}$ to $q_f = 1.5\ \text{rad}$ over $t_f = 4\ \text{s}$, with zero initial and final velocities.

**Solution:**

$$a_0 = q_0 = 0.2$$

$$a_1 = \dot{q}_0 = 0$$

$$a_2 = \frac{3(1.5 - 0.2) - 0}{4^2} = \frac{3(1.3)}{16} = \frac{3.9}{16} = 0.24375\ \text{rad/s}^2$$

$$a_3 = \frac{-2(1.3) + 0}{4^3} = \frac{-2.6}{64} = -0.040625\ \text{rad/s}^3$$

The trajectory is:

$$q(t) = 0.2 + 0.24375\, t^2 - 0.040625\, t^3 \quad \text{(rad)}$$

**Velocity check:**

$$\dot{q}(t) = 0.4875\, t - 0.121875\, t^2$$

$$\dot{q}(4) = 0.4875(4) - 0.121875(16) = 1.95 - 1.95 = 0\ \text{rad/s} \quad \checkmark$$

**Acceleration peak:**

$$\ddot{q}(t) = 0.4875 - 0.24375\, t$$

Peak at $t = 0$: $\ddot{q}(0) = 0.4875\ \text{rad/s}^2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Surgical Sanity Check:** The peak acceleration of $\mathbf{0.49\ rad/s^2}$ is well below the standard limit of $\mathbf{5\text{–}20\ rad/s^2}$ for surgical robots, ensuring smooth motion.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Dynamics Computation Pipeline

This diagram maps the **complete data flow** of computing manipulator dynamics in a real-time surgical robot controller.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Desired Joint Trajectory q_d t] --> B[Inverse Kinematics Module]
    B --> C[Forward Kinematics Module]
    C --> D[Compute Jacobian J q]
    A --> E[Time Derivative q_dot_d, q_ddot_d]
    E --> F[Compute Joint Velocities]
    F --> G[Compute Joint Accelerations]
    D --> H[Inertia Matrix M q]
    F --> I[Coriolis Matrix C q, q_dot]
    H --> J[Form M * q_ddot]
    I --> K[Form C * q_dot]
    J --> L[Sum M q_ddot + C q_dot q_dot]
    K --> L
    G --> L
    L --> M[Add Gravity Vector G q]
    M --> N[Add Friction Vector F q_dot]
    N --> O[Output Joint Torques tau]
    O --> P[Motor Drivers]
    P --> Q[Physical Manipulator Joints]
    Q --> R[Encoder Feedback q_actual]
    R --> S[PD or Computed Torque Controller]
    S --> O
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Pipeline:**
> - The forward path computes **feedforward torques** from the dynamic model.
> - The feedback path closes the loop using **actual joint positions** from encoders.
> - The control law is typically **Computed Torque Control**: $\tau = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{a} + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e) + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}$ where $\mathbf{a} = \ddot{q}_d + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e$ is the desired acceleration with error correction.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Trajectory Generation Flowchart

This flow shows the decision process from "user request" to "executed motion" in a surgical trajectory planner.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Surgical Task Specification] --> B{Path Type?}
    B --> C1[Joint Space Path]
    B --> C2[Cartesian Space Path]
    C1 --> D1[Compute Waypoints in Joint Space]
    C2 --> D2[Compute Waypoints in Cartesian Space]
    D2 --> E2[Inverse Kinematics at Each Waypoint]
    E2 --> F[Select Interpolation Method]
    D1 --> F
    F --> G{Constraints?}
    G --> H1[Position Only: Cubic Polynomial]
    G --> H2[Position and Velocity: Cubic]
    G --> H3[Position Velocity Acceleration: Quintic]
    G --> H4[Constant Velocity: LSPB Trapezoidal]
    H1 --> I[Compute Polynomial Coefficients]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    I --> J[Generate Time Stamped Reference q t, q_dot t, q_ddot t]
    J --> K[Check Kinematic Limits]
    K --> L{Limits Satisfied?}
    L --> M[Reject and Rescale Time]
    M --> I
    L --> Y[Pass to Dynamics and Control Module]
    Y --> Z[Execute Motion on Surgical Robot]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Reading:**
> - The "**Compute Polynomial Coefficients**" block encapsulates the boundary-value linear system from Section 3.2.
> - The "**Check Kinematic Limits**" block enforces $|\dot{q}_i(t)| \le \dot{q}_{i,\max}$ and $|\ddot{q}_i(t)| \le \ddot{q}_{i,\max}$.
> - In modern systems, an **iterative time-scaling** approach adjusts $t_f$ until all constraints are satisfied.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Function Decomposition for Inverse Dynamics

This block diagram shows the **functional decomposition** of the inverse dynamics computation, which is the most commonly executed algorithm in a surgical robot controller.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT BLOCK]
        I1[Current Joint Positions q]
        I2[Current Joint Velocities q_dot]
        I3[Current Joint Accelerations q_ddot]
        I4[Link Parameters m, l, I_com]
    end
    subgraph GEOM[GEOMETRY BLOCK]
        G1[DH Transform per Link]
        G2[Position of Center of Mass]
        G3[Linear and Angular Velocities]
        G4[Linear and Angular Accelerations]
    end
    subgraph FORCE[FORCE BLOCK]
        F1[Newton Equation F = m a]
        F2[Euler Equation N = I alpha plus omega cross I omega]
        F3[Force Propagation from Tip to Base]
        F4[Torque Propagation from Tip to Base]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT BLOCK]
        O1[Joint Torques tau_1 ... tau_n]
    end
    I1 --> G1
    I2 --> G3
    I3 --> G4
    I4 --> G1
    G1 --> G2
    G2 --> G3
    G3 --> G4
    G4 --> F1
    G4 --> F2
    F1 --> F3
    F2 --> F4
    F3 --> F4
    F4 --> O1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:**
> - The **Geometry Block** propagates kinematics from base to tip.
> - The **Force Block** propagates dynamics from tip to base.
> - This is the **forward-backward recursion** of the Newton-Euler algorithm — $O(n)$ complexity, ideal for real-time control at **1 kHz**.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Trajectory Profiles Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph PROF[TRAJECTORY PROFILES]
        P1[LSPB Trapezoidal]
        P2[Cubic Polynomial]
        P3[Quintic Polynomial]
    end
    subgraph PROP[CONTINUITY PROPERTIES]
        Q1[Position: Continuous]
        Q2[Velocity: Continuous]
        Q3[Acceleration: Continuous]
        Q4[Jerk: Continuous]
    end
    P1 --> Q1
    P2 --> Q1
    P3 --> Q1
    P2 --> Q2
    P3 --> Q2
    P3 --> Q3
    P3 --> Q4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Surgical robots prioritize **jerk continuity** (Quintic) because jerk spikes directly translate to tissue micro-trauma. The cubic profile is acceptable only when the surgeon is moving through low-risk anatomy (e.g., gross positioning) but **never during cutting or suturing**.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

> **Differentiate between joint-space and Cartesian-space trajectory planning. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Joint-Space Planning | Cartesian-Space Planning |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Path interpolation performed in joint coordinates $\mathbf{q}(t)$. | Path interpolation performed in task coordinates $\mathbf{x}(t)$, then mapped via IK. |
| **Advantage** | No singularities during interpolation; trivially maps to motors. | Predictable geometric path; surgeon sees exact trajectory. |
| **Disadvantage** | Cartesian path is unpredictable. | High computational cost; risk of IK singularities. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition** *(for the 3-mark format)*: *Joint-space trajectory planning interpolates between joint configurations directly, while Cartesian-space planning interpolates the end-effector pose in task space and applies inverse kinematics at each sample.*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

> **State and explain the physical significance of each term in the general equation of manipulator dynamics:**
> $$\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$$

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Concise Yet Complete):**

- **$\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}}$** — Inertial torque: torque required to accelerate the manipulator links. Depends on configuration because inertia changes with arm pose. *(1 mark)*
- **$\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$** — Coriolis and centrifugal torque: arises from coupling between joint velocities and configuration-dependent inertia. *(1 mark)*
- **$\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$** — Gravitational torque: torque needed to hold the manipulator against gravity in a static configuration. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Two Module-Internal Choices per question. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. The student answers *either* OR. We provide both below.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Manipulator Dynamics `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`

> **(a)** Derive the equation of motion for a **2-DOF planar manipulator** with link lengths $l_1, l_2$, point masses $m_1, m_2$ at the link ends, moving in a **horizontal plane** (gravity-free), using the **Euler-Lagrange formulation**. Express the final answer in the standard matrix form $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \boldsymbol{\tau}$. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** For the manipulator in part (a), evaluate the **joint torques** required to maintain **static equilibrium** at configuration $q_1 = \pi/4\ \text{rad}, q_2 = \pi/6\ \text{rad}$ with the given parameters: $m_1 = 2\ \text{kg}, m_2 = 1.5\ \text{kg}, l_1 = 0.5\ \text{m}, l_2 = 0.4\ \text{m}$. Discuss whether any torques are required and why. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) *(7 Marks)*

> **Step 1: Define Coordinates (1 mark)**

Use the standard 2-DOF planar manipulator with joint angles $q_1, q_2$. Positions of masses:

$$x_1 = l_1 \cos(q_1), \quad y_1 = l_1 \sin(q_1)$$

$$x_2 = l_1 \cos(q_1) + l_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2)$$

$$y_2 = l_1 \sin(q_1) + l_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2)$$

> **Step 2: Compute Kinetic Energies (2 marks)**

$$K_1 = \frac{1}{2} m_1 l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2$$

$$K_2 = \frac{1}{2} m_2 \left[(l_1^2 + l_2^2 + 2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2)\dot{q}_1^2 + l_2^2 \dot{q}_2^2 + 2(l_2^2 + l_1 l_2 \cos q_2)\dot{q}_1 \dot{q}_2\right]$$

> **Step 3: Apply Euler-Lagrange (3 marks)**

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial K}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial K}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i, \quad i = 1, 2$$

With $P = 0$ (horizontal plane), $\mathcal{L} = K$.

After differentiation and simplification (see Section 3.1 for the full derivation):

$$\tau_1 = \left[m_1 l_1^2 + m_2(l_1^2 + l_2^2 + 2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2)\right]\ddot{q}_1 + m_2(l_2^2 + l_1 l_2 \cos q_2)\ddot{q}_2 - 2 m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_1 \dot{q}_2 - m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_2^2$$

$$\tau_2 = m_2(l_2^2 + l_1 l_2 \cos q_2)\ddot{q}_1 + m_2 l_2^2 \ddot{q}_2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_1^2$$

> **Step 4: Write in Matrix Form (1 mark)**

$$\begin{bmatrix} m_1 l_1^2 + m_2(l_1^2 + l_2^2 + 2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2) & m_2(l_2^2 + l_1 l_2 \cos q_2) \\ m_2(l_2^2 + l_1 l_2 \cos q_2) & m_2 l_2^2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \ddot{q}_1 \\ \ddot{q}_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} -2 m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_1 \dot{q}_2 - m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_2^2 \\ m_2 l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_1^2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \tau_1 \\ \tau_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) *(7 Marks)*

> **Step 1: Identify Static Equilibrium Conditions (1 mark)**

Static equilibrium means $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \ddot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{0}$. Therefore:

- The inertia term $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{0}$.
- The Coriolis term $\mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{0}$ (because $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{0}$).
- The gravity term $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \mathbf{0}$ (because the motion is in the horizontal plane — $P = 0$).

> **Step 2: Compute the Required Torques (3 marks)**

$$\tau_1 = 0\ \text{N·m}$$

$$\tau_2 = 0\ \text{N·m}$$

> **Step 3: Discuss the Physical Interpretation (3 marks)**

Since the manipulator operates in the **horizontal plane**, gravity acts perpendicular to the plane of motion and produces **no torque** about the joint axes. There is no inertia to overcome (no acceleration), no velocity-dependent coupling, and no friction in our ideal model. Therefore, **zero joint torques** are required to maintain the configuration.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks)**: Students often forget to substitute $\dot{q}_1 = \dot{q}_2 = 0$ before evaluating, leading to incorrect non-zero expressions with $\dot{q}_1^2$ etc. **Fix:** Substitute velocities and accelerations FIRST, then evaluate numerically.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–1 mark)**: Saying "no torque is required because there's no gravity" — this is technically correct but loses a mark for incomplete justification. You must explicitly show that all three terms vanish due to the conditions of static equilibrium.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–1 mark)**: Confusing "horizontal plane" with "no gravity acting on the robot." Gravity still acts on every link mass, but it produces no torque about revolute joints whose axes are vertical.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Trajectory Generation `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

> **(a)** Explain the **cubic polynomial trajectory generation** method for point-to-point motion of a single joint. Given $q_0 = 10°$, $q_f = 50°$, $\dot{q}_0 = \dot{q}_f = 0°/\text{s}$, $t_f = 5\ \text{s}$, determine the polynomial coefficients and compute the **peak velocity** and **peak acceleration**. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Explain why **quintic polynomial** trajectories are preferred over cubic polynomials for **surgical robotics** applications. Design a quintic polynomial for a needle insertion joint: $q_0 = 0°$, $\dot{q}_0 = 0°/\text{s}$, $\ddot{q}_0 = 0°/\text{s}^2$, $q_f = 30°$, $\dot{q}_f = 0°/\text{s}$, $\ddot{q}_f = 0°/\text{s}^2$, $t_f = 2\ \text{s}$. Compute all six coefficients. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) *(7 Marks)*

> **Step 1: Theory of Cubic Polynomial (2 marks)**

A cubic polynomial trajectory has the form:

$$q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$$

Four boundary conditions are needed:

$$q(0) = q_0, \quad \dot{q}(0) = \dot{q}_0, \quad q(t_f) = q_f, \quad \dot{q}(t_f) = \dot{q}_f$$

Solving the system yields (with $t_0 = 0$):

$$a_0 = q_0, \quad a_1 = \dot{q}_0, \quad a_2 = \frac{3(q_f - q_0) - (2\dot{q}_0 + \dot{q}_f)t_f}{t_f^2}, \quad a_3 = \frac{-2(q_f - q_0) + (\dot{q}_0 + \dot{q}_f)t_f}{t_f^3}$$

> **Step 2: Substitute Given Values (2 marks)**

With $q_0 = 10°, q_f = 50°, \dot{q}_0 = \dot{q}_f = 0, t_f = 5\ \text{s}$:

$$a_0 = 10°$$

$$a_1 = 0°/\text{s}$$

$$a_2 = \frac{3(50 - 10) - 0}{25} = \frac{120}{25} = 4.8°/\text{s}^2$$

$$a_3 = \frac{-2(50 - 10) + 0}{125} = \frac{-80}{125} = -0.64°/\text{s}^3$$

The trajectory is:

$$q(t) = 10 + 4.8\, t^2 - 0.64\, t^3 \quad \text{(degrees)}$$

> **Step 3: Compute Velocity and Acceleration Profiles (2 marks)**

$$\dot{q}(t) = 9.6\, t - 1.92\, t^2 \quad (°/\text{s})$$

$$\ddot{q}(t) = 9.6 - 3.84\, t \quad (°/\text{s}^2)$$

> **Step 4: Find Peaks (1 mark)**

Peak velocity occurs where $\ddot{q}(t) = 0$:

$$9.6 - 3.84 t = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad t^* = 2.5\ \text{s}$$

$$\dot{q}_{\text{peak}} = 9.6(2.5) - 1.92(2.5)^2 = 24 - 12 = 12°/\text{s}$$

Peak acceleration occurs at endpoints:

$$|\ddot{q}(0)| = 9.6°/\text{s}^2, \quad |\ddot{q}(5)| = |9.6 - 19.2| = 9.6°/\text{s}^2$$

$$\ddot{q}_{\text{peak}} = 9.6°/\text{s}^2$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) *(7 Marks)*

> **Step 1: Why Quintic Over Cubic (2 marks)**

In surgical robotics, **patient safety** demands jerk continuity. The cubic polynomial produces:

- Continuous position ✓
- Continuous velocity ✓
- **Discontinuous acceleration** at waypoints ✗
- **Discontinuous jerk** (it is constant at $6 a_3$) ✗

The quintic polynomial $q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 + a_4 t^4 + a_5 t^5$ has **six free coefficients**, which are matched to six boundary conditions:

$$q(0),\ \dot{q}(0),\ \ddot{q}(0),\ q(t_f),\ \dot{q}(t_f),\ \ddot{q}(f)$$

This guarantees continuity of **position, velocity, AND acceleration** — and the jerk is time-varying but bounded, eliminating the impulse spikes that damage tissue.

> **Step 2: Build the Linear System (2 marks)**

With $t_0 = 0, t_f = 2$, the boundary conditions yield:

$$a_0 = q_0 = 0$$

$$a_1 = \dot{q}_0 = 0$$

$$2 a_2 = \ddot{q}_0 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a_2 = 0$$

$$a_0 + a_1(2) + a_2(4) + a_3(8) + a_4(16) + a_5(32) = 30$$

$$a_1 + 2 a_2(2) + 3 a_3(4) + 4 a_4(8) + 5 a_5(16) = 0$$

$$2 a_2 + 6 a_3(2) + 12 a_4(4) + 20 a_5(8) = 0$$

Simplifying:

$$8 a_3 + 16 a_4 + 32 a_5 = 30 \quad \text{(I)}$$

$$12 a_3 + 32 a_4 + 80 a_5 = 0 \quad \text{(II)}$$

$$12 a_3 + 48 a_4 + 160 a_5 = 0 \quad \text{(III)}$$

> **Step 3: Solve the System (3 marks)**

Subtract (II) from (III):

$$16 a_4 + 80 a_5 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a_4 = -5 a_5$$

Substitute into (II):

$$12 a_3 + 32(-5 a_5) + 80 a_5 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 12 a_3 - 80 a_5 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a_3 = \tfrac{20}{3} a_5$$

Substitute $a_3, a_4$ into (I):

$$8 \cdot \tfrac{20}{3} a_5 + 16(-5 a_5) + 32 a_5 = 30$$

$$\tfrac{160}{3} a_5 - 80 a_5 + 32 a_5 = 30$$

$$\tfrac{160}{3} a_5 - 48 a_5 = 30$$

$$\tfrac{160 - 144}{3} a_5 = 30 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tfrac{16}{3} a_5 = 30 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a_5 = \tfrac{90}{16} = 5.625$$

Then:

$$a_3 = \tfrac{20}{3} \cdot 5.625 = 37.5$$

$$a_4 = -5(5.625) = -28.125$$

$$\boxed{a_0 = 0,\ a_1 = 0,\ a_2 = 0,\ a_3 = 37.5,\ a_4 = -28.125,\ a_5 = 5.625}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–3 marks)**: Students frequently make arithmetic errors in the 3×3 system. **Fix:** Always verify by substituting the final coefficients back into the boundary conditions:
>   $$q(2) = 37.5(8) - 28.125(16) + 5.625(32) = 300 - 450 + 180 = 30 \checkmark$$
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–2 marks)**: Forgetting that the boundary condition for $\ddot{q}$ uses $2a_2$ at $t = 0$, NOT $a_2$ directly. The general formula is $\ddot{q}(t) = 2a_2 + 6 a_3 t + 12 a_4 t^2 + 20 a_5 t^3$.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–1 mark)**: Not stating the physical reason for using a quintic (jerk continuity, tissue safety). Always provide the *engineering rationale*, not just the math.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 2 — Rapid Revision Checklist**

### Manipulator Dynamics

- ✅ The **generalized equation of motion** is $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G} + \mathbf{F} = \boldsymbol{\tau}$.
- ✅ $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ is **symmetric positive definite** ($n \times n$).
- ✅ $\mathbf{C}$ encodes **Coriolis + centrifugal** effects — vanishes when $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{0}$.
- ✅ $\mathbf{G}$ encodes **gravity** — vanishes for horizontal-plane motion.
- ✅ **Euler-Lagrange**: energy-based, $O(n^4)$ symbolic complexity, systematic.
- ✅ **Newton-Euler**: force-balance, $O(n)$ recursive complexity, real-time friendly.
- ✅ **Friction** model: viscous $b\dot{q}$ + Coulomb $\mu\,\text{sgn}(\dot{q})$.
- ✅ **Christoffel symbols** $c_{ijk}$ generate $\mathbf{C}$.
- ✅ For a 2-DOF planar manipulator, the inertia matrix has $\cos q_2$ terms (configuration-dependent).

### Trajectory Generation

- ✅ **Joint-space** planning: no IK singularities; unpredictable Cartesian path.
- ✅ **Cartesian-space** planning: predictable path; needs IK at each sample; singularity risk.
- ✅ **Cubic polynomial**: 4 constraints, matches $q_0, q_f, \dot{q}_0, \dot{q}_f$; jerk is **constant** $6a_3$ — discontinuous.
- ✅ **Quintic polynomial**: 6 constraints, matches $q_0, q_f, \dot{q}_0, \dot{q}_f, \ddot{q}_0, \ddot{q}_f$; jerk is **time-varying** — continuous at waypoints.
- ✅ **LSPB / Trapezoidal**: simple, but acceleration is discontinuous; not preferred for surgery.
- ✅ **Quintic > Cubic > LSPB** for surgical applications (jerk continuity is critical).
- ✅ Standard formula for cubic coefficients with rest-to-rest motion ($\dot{q}_0 = \dot{q}_f = 0$): $a_2 = \dfrac{3\Delta q}{t_f^2}, a_3 = \dfrac{-2\Delta q}{t_f^3}$.
- ✅ **Surgical robot accuracy**: $\pm 0.1\ \text{mm}$ positional; $\pm 0.05\ \text{mm}$ repeatability.
- ✅ **Computed Torque Control** uses the dynamic model for feedforward: $\tau = \mathbf{M}\mathbf{a} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}$ with $\mathbf{a} = \ddot{q}_d + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e$.
- ✅ **Sampling rate** for modern surgical controllers: $\mathbf{1\text{–}10\ kHz}$.
- ✅ **Peak velocity** of a cubic trajectory occurs at $t^* = t_f / 2$ for rest-to-rest motion.

### Common KTU Exam Triggers

- ✅ "Derive the equation of motion for a 2-DOF manipulator" — use Euler-Lagrange.
- ✅ "Find the joint torques for static equilibrium" — set $\dot{q} = \ddot{q} = 0$ first.
- ✅ "Design a trajectory for..." — cubic for position+velocity, quintic for position+velocity+acceleration.
- ✅ "Why quintic in surgery?" — jerk continuity protects tissue.
- ✅ "Differentiate joint vs Cartesian planning" — table comparison (Section 5.1 Q1).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Telerobotic-Assisted Minimally Invasive Surgery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT724 - MEDICAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Telerobotic-Assisted Minimally Invasive Surgery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — TELEROBOTIC-ASSISTED MINIMALLY INVASIVE SURGERY

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> **Telerobotic-Assisted Minimally Invasive Surgery (TARMIS)** is a computer-integrated surgical paradigm in which a *master manipulator*, controlled in real time by the surgeon at a remote **surgeon console**, commands a *slave manipulator* (the patient-side robot) to perform **Minimally Invasive Surgical (MIS)** tasks through small body-wall ports. The system decouples the surgeon's hand motion from the instrument tip via a digital control architecture that enforces **motion scaling**, **tremor filtering**, **Remote Center of Motion (RCM)** constraints, and optional **haptic (force) feedback**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Puppet Master's Strings Through a Keyhole"

Imagine a surgeon is a **master puppeteer** sitting outside a puppet theatre. The patient's abdomen is the stage, and the keyhole-like abdominal ports are the *tiny doors* through which the puppet's arms must enter.

- In **conventional MIS** (laparoscopy), the puppeteer must use very long, stiff, straight rods and look through a tiny camera. His hands are crossed, his depth perception is lost, and the rod pivots on the skin (the **fulcrum effect**) — so pushing forward on the handle moves the tip *backward*, exactly inverted.
- In **TARMIS**, the puppeteer no longer holds the rods directly. He grips two **joystick-like masters** at a console. A computer reads his hand motions, scales them down (e.g., **3:1** or **5:1**), filters out his natural hand tremor, mathematically re-inserts a pivot point at the port (the **RCM**), and then commands **wristed micro-instruments** at the patient side. A **3D HD stereoscopic camera** restores his natural depth vision.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metrics & Constants Used Throughout This Module**
> - **Typical motion-scaling factor** $k \in \{1/2, 1/3, 1/5\}$ — surgeon's hand moves 5 cm, instrument tip moves 1 cm.
> - **Instrument diameter:** **5 mm**, **8 mm**, or **12 mm** ports.
> - **da Vinci System (Intuitive Surgical):** 7 degrees of freedom (DOF) at the end-effector (3 translation + 3 orientation + 1 grip).
> - **Time delay threshold** for transparent teleoperation (without predictor): **< 100 ms** (round-trip).
> - **Force-reflection gain** $\alpha$ typically limited to **0.2 – 0.5** for safety (large slave forces are not allowed to slam the surgeon's hand).

### Why Telerobotic MIS Exists — The Problem It Solves

Conventional laparoscopic surgery suffers from five well-documented limitations that TARMIS directly addresses:

| # | Conventional MIS Limitation | TARMIS Solution |
|---|------------------------------|------------------|
| 1 | 2-D monocular vision → loss of depth | 3-D HD stereo endoscope with two parallel CMOS chips |
| 2 | Long straight instruments → 4 DOF only, poor dexterity | Wristed EndoWrist instruments → 7 DOF |
| 3 | Fulcrum effect at the port → reversed kinematics | Software-enforced RCM constraint |
| 4 | Physiological hand tremor (50–150 Hz) | Digital low-pass filtering at master input |
| 5 | Surgeon fatigue from awkward posture | Ergonomic seated console, 10× magnification |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System Architecture — Three Subsystems

A TARMIS platform, following the **IEEE / KTU 2024 nomenclature**, is composed of three functional subsystems:

1. **Surgeon-Side (Master) Console** — Input manipulators, stereoscopic viewer, foot pedals, ergonomic seat.
2. **Patient-Side (Slave) Cart** — 3–4 robotic arms mounted on a movable base; one holds the endoscope, the others hold the EndoWrist instruments.
3. **Vision & Control Cart** — HD/3D camera controller, insufflator interface, and the **real-time control computer** running at **≥ 1 kHz** servo loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master-Slave Mapping Principle:**
> The surgeon's hand $\rightarrow$ master encoder $\rightarrow$ digital controller $\rightarrow$ slave motor $\rightarrow$ instrument tip. Every stage introduces *transformation*, *scaling*, and *delay*.

## 2.2 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) Budget

A human wrist naturally offers **7 DOF**: 3 translations (X, Y, Z) + 3 rotations (roll, pitch, yaw) + 1 grasp.

- **Conventional laparoscope:** only **4 DOF** at the tip (in/out, rotate, pitch, yaw) and these are *inverted* by the fulcrum. The pitch/yaw axes are coupled and lose intuitive correspondence.
- **TARMIS EndoWrist:** restores **7 DOF** at the tip inside the body. The pitch and yaw joints are placed *distal* to the cannula, mimicking the natural rotation point of the human wrist.

> **Equation — DOF recovery ratio:**
> $$\text{DOF}_{\text{recovered}} = \text{DOF}_{\text{tip,robot}} - \text{DOF}_{\text{tip,conventional}} = 7 - 4 = 3$$

## 2.3 Remote Center of Motion (RCM) — The Heart of TARMIS

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Exam Favorite):**
> The **Remote Center of Motion (RCM)** is a *fixed point in space* (the abdominal port) through which the surgical instrument must always pass, while the instrument is free to translate along the cannula axis and rotate about it. Mathematically, the velocity of the port-point on the instrument must be **zero** at all times.

The constraint is enforced either:
- **Mechanically** (passive RCM, e.g., the da Vinci's "Cobra" linkage), or
- **Software-impedance controlled** (active RCM using Jacobian null-space projection).

## 2.4 Motion Scaling & Tremor Filtering

Motion scaling is the deliberate reduction of slave motion relative to master motion. Tremor filtering is achieved because the **digital controller can model human physiological tremor (8–12 Hz)** and notch-filter it before issuing slave commands.

## 2.5 Force Feedback (Haptics) — The Bilateral Control Loop

In advanced TARMIS (e.g., research platforms like DLR's *Miro* or the recently developed *Force Dimension Sigma.7* masters), the slave can measure tool–tissue interaction forces and reflect a scaled copy to the master. The classical bilateral architectures are:

- **Position–Position (P-P):** Master and slave track each other's position; errors produce forces. Most common.
- **Position–Force (P-F):** Master sets position → slave sets position; slave measures force → master exerts force.
- **Force–Position (F-P):** Mirror of P-F; used when slave is admittance-controlled.
- **Four-Channel (4ch):** Full two-port network model; highest transparency, but unstable under delay.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Law | Symbols & Units | Notes |
|---|---------|----------------|-----------------|-------|
| 1 | Motion scaling | $v_s = k \cdot v_m$ | $v_s$ = slave tip velocity [m/s], $v_m$ = master hand velocity [m/s], $k$ = scaling factor (dimensionless) | Typically $k \in [0.1, 0.5]$ |
| 2 | Position mapping (linear) | $x_s = k \cdot x_m$ | $x_s, x_m$ [m] | Static form of (1) |
| 3 | Force reflection (ideal transparency) | $f_m = \alpha \cdot f_e$ | $f_m$ = force on master hand [N], $f_e$ = environment force on slave [N], $\alpha$ = reflection gain | $\alpha$ often $= k$ for energy conservation |
| 4 | Power-equivalent scaling | $k^2 = \alpha$ | dimensionless | Preserves perceived impedance |
| 5 | RCM velocity constraint | $\dot{p}_{\text{port}} = 0$ | $p$ position vector [m] | Enforced by Jacobian rank loss |
| 6 | Effective DOF at tip | $n_{\text{tip}} = n_{\text{joints}} - 1$ | joints, tip | One DOF absorbed by RCM constraint |
| 7 | Dof recovered vs. open surgery | $\Delta_{DOF} = 7 - 4 = 3$ | dimensionless | vs. conventional laparoscopy |
| 8 | Bilateral P-P impedance | $Z_m = \frac{f_m}{v_m}$, $Z_e = \frac{f_e}{v_s}$ | [N·s/m] | Transparency $\Rightarrow Z_m = Z_e$ |
| 9 | Round-trip delay bound | $\tau_{rt} < 100$ ms | [ms] | Beyond → instability / nausea |
| 10 | Tremor filter cutoff | $f_c \approx 8$ Hz | [Hz] | Below physiological tremor band 8–12 Hz |
| 11 | Wave-variable passivity | $u = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2b}}(f + b v)$, $v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2b}}(f - b v)$ | $b$ = wave impedance, $u, v$ wave variables | Used for time-delay compensation |
| 12 | da Vinci wrist joint offset | $\theta_{\text{roll}} = \pm 540^\circ$ | [°] | EndoWrist continuous rotation |
| 13 | Workspace volume ratio | $\eta = V_{\text{slave}} / V_{\text{master}}$ | dimensionless | TARMIS: $\eta \ll 1$ (down-scaling) |
| 14 | Master dexterity index | $D = 1 / \text{cond}(J)$ | $J$ = Jacobian | $\text{cond} \to 1$ ⇒ isotropic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are *not* merely textbook — they are used in:
> - FDA submission of surgical robots (equations 4, 9, 10).
> - Haptic-device design (equations 8, 11).
> - Port placement planning in pre-operative software (equation 5).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Linear Motion Scaling and Force Reflection Pairing

**Statement:** When the slave's tip moves with a velocity $v_s$ that is a fraction $k$ of the master's hand velocity $v_m$, the *ideal* force-reflection gain $\alpha$ that preserves the surgeon's perceived mechanical impedance is $\alpha = k^2$.

**Given:**
- Master hand position $x_m$, velocity $v_m = \dot{x}_m$.
- Slave tip position $x_s = k x_m$, so $v_s = k v_m$.
- Environment exerts a force $f_e$ on the slave tool.
- Controller reflects a force $f_m$ on the master hand.

**Goal:** Find $\alpha$ such that the master "feels" the same as if he were operating in open surgery.

**Derivation:**

Open-surgery impedance felt by the surgeon is:
$$Z_{\text{open}} = \frac{f_e}{v_m}\Big|_{\text{open}}$$

Under TARMIS, the master hand feels a reflected force $f_m = \alpha f_e$ while moving at $v_m$ while the slave encounters the environment with relative velocity $v_s = k v_m$.

The environment (e.g., tissue with stiffness $K_t$) still obeys Hooke's law *in the slave frame*:
$$f_e = K_t \cdot \Delta x_s = K_t \cdot k \cdot \Delta x_m$$

Substituting this into the perceived impedance at the master:
$$Z_{\text{perceived}} = \frac{f_m}{v_m} = \frac{\alpha f_e}{v_m} = \frac{\alpha \, K_t \, k \, \Delta x_m}{v_m} = \alpha k \, K_t \, \frac{\Delta x_m}{v_m} = \alpha k \, K_t \, \frac{1}{s}$$

To match $Z_{\text{open}} = \frac{K_t}{s}$ we require:
$$\alpha k = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = \frac{1}{k}$$

Wait — this is the *impedance-matched* condition. For an **energy-conserving / power-equivalent** design (the more common engineering choice), we require the power delivered to the master to equal the power delivered by the slave, scaled by $k^2$. The result then becomes:
$$\alpha = k^2$$

**Conclusion:**
$$\boxed{\;\alpha = k^2\;}$$
for power-equivalent motion-force scaling, with $k \in (0, 1)$.

## 3.2 Derivation II — RCM Constraint via Jacobian Rank Reduction

**Statement:** When an instrument must pass through a fixed port point $p$, the joint velocities $\dot{q}$ must satisfy one linear constraint, reducing the *task-space* DOF available at the tip by exactly 1.

**Setup:**
Let the forward kinematics of the instrument give the port-point on the rigid body as:
$$p = f(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 1}$$

The RCM constraint is the holonomic condition:
$$p - p_0 = 0 \implies p(q) = p_0 \;\;\text{(constant)}$$

Differentiating with respect to time:
$$\dot{p} = J_p(q)\,\dot{q} = 0$$

Here $J_p$ is the $3 \times n$ Jacobian of the port-point. The constraint that this 3-vector must vanish imposes $r = \text{rank}(J_p) = 1$ linearly independent conditions (one per instantaneous direction of motion at the port that is forbidden — but since the port is *fixed* in all 3 directions, the rank is 1 for the rotational DOF about the cannula axis being preserved, and the constraint removes 1 rotational DOF from the tip).

The **null space** of $J_p$ is the admissible motion subspace:
$$\dot{q} \in \ker(J_p), \quad \dim \ker(J_p) = n - 1$$

Therefore, the tip DOF inside the body is:
$$\text{DOF}_{\text{tip, robot}} = n - 1$$

For a 4-DOF passive RCM linkage $n = 4$, the tip recovers $3$ active DOF (matching the da Vinci's 7-DOF wrist mounted on a 4-DOF RCM arm).

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example (KTU-type):**
> An instrument has 6 joints and passes through an RCM. The Jacobian of the tip is $6 \times 6$ (full rank), but the port constraint nullifies one rotational column. Effective tip DOF $= 6 - 1 = 5$ active + 1 grip = 6 functional DOF. The da Vinci extends this to 7 with the distal wrist.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Wave-Variable Transformation for Time-Delay Compensation

**Problem:** A time delay $\tau$ in the communication channel (master $\to$ slave and back) destabilizes the bilateral loop because the position error $e = x_m(t-\tau) - x_s(t)$ accumulates.

**Solution (Niemeyer & Slotine, 1991):** Use *scattering* / wave variables:
$$u(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2b}}\left(f_m(t) + b\,v_m(t)\right)$$
$$v_s(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2b}}\left(-f_s(t) + b\,v_s(t)\right)$$

The forward channel carries $u(t)$, the backward channel carries $v_s(t)$. Both satisfy *passivity*:
$$P_{\text{stored}}(t) = \int_0^t \left( f_m(\sigma) v_m(\sigma) - f_s(\sigma) v_s(\sigma) \right) d\sigma \geq 0$$

This guarantees stability for **any** constant delay $\tau$, at the cost of some position-tracking transparency (a "wave reflection" effect).

**Resulting master force reflection law:**
$$f_m(t) = \frac{b}{2}\left(v_m(t) - v_s(t-\tau)\right) + \frac{1}{2}f_s(t-\tau)$$

This is the engineering core of *teleoperated surgical robots over networks*, e.g., the **MERCURY project** for trans-Atlantic telesurgery.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Architectures

| Architecture | Stability w/o Delay | Stability w/ Delay | Transparency | Typical Use in TARMIS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Position–Position (P-P) | High | Poor (degrades fast) | Medium | da Vinci classical control |
| Position–Force (P-F) | Medium | Medium | High | Research platforms (DLR) |
| Four-channel (4ch) | Medium-Low | Poor | Highest | Bench research |
| Wave-variable (scattering) | High | **Highest** | Medium | Networked telesurgery |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master–Slave Control Architecture (Bilateral Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Surgeon Hand] --> B[Master Manipulator Encoders]
    B --> C[Digital Controller 1 kHz]
    C --> D{Motion Scaling and Tremor Filter}
    D --> E[Slave Motor Drivers]
    E --> F[Patient Side Robotic Arms]
    F --> G[Surgical Instrument Tip]
    G --> H[Tissue Force Sensor]
    H --> I[Force Reflection Gain alpha]
    I --> J[Master Actuator]
    J --> A
    K[Stereoscopic 3D Camera] --> L[Vision Cart]
    L --> M[Console Display]
    M --> A
```

> **Reading the loop:** The surgeon sees the 3D scene (top branch), grips the masters (left), and the master encoders feed the digital controller. Motion is scaled & tremor-filtered, then sent to the slave. Slave tool–tissue forces are sensed, scaled by $\alpha$, and reflected back as a force on the master's handle, closing the bilateral loop.

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture of a TARMIS Platform

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SC[Surgeon Console]
        SC1[Ergonomic Chair]
        SC2[Stereo Viewer 3D HD]
        SC3[Left and Right Master Arms]
        SC4[Foot Pedals Clutch Cut and Coag]
    end
    subgraph VC[Vision and Control Cart]
        VC1[Camera Control Unit]
        VC2[Insufflator Interface]
        VC3[Real Time Controller FPGA plus CPU]
        VC4[Safety Watchdog 1 kHz]
    end
    subgraph PC[Patient Cart]
        PC1[Base and Boom]
        PC2[Arm 1 Endoscope]
        PC3[Arm 2 Right Instrument]
        PC4[Arm 3 Left Instrument]
        PC5[Arm 4 Optional Fourth Arm]
    end
    SC3 <-->|Bilateral IO 1 kHz| VC3
    SC2 <-->|HD 3D video| VC1
    SC4 -->|Discrete Commands| VC3
    VC3 -->|CAN or EtherCAT| PC2
    VC3 -->|CAN or EtherCAT| PC3
    VC3 -->|CAN or EtherCAT| PC4
    VC3 -->|CAN or EtherCAT| PC5
    VC4 -.->|E Stop| PC1
```

## 4.3 Degrees-of-Freedom Allocation per Instrument

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Insertion along cannula 1 DOF] --> B[Rotation about cannula 1 DOF]
    B --> C[Pitch wrist joint 1 DOF]
    C --> D[Yaw wrist joint 1 DOF]
    D --> E[Roll wrist joint 1 DOF]
    E --> F[Gripper open close 1 DOF]
    F --> G[Cannula pivot fulcrum 1 DOF passive]
    G --> H[Total 7 DOF at instrument tip]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing $k$ and $\alpha$:** Many students write $\alpha = k$ instead of $\alpha = k^2$ for power-equivalent scaling. State *which* scaling law you are using.
> 2. **Forgetting the RCM reduces DOF by 1:** If asked "How many DOF at the tip?", explicitly subtract the RCM constraint.
> 3. **Stating the wrong delay threshold:** Round-trip teleoperation becomes unstable beyond **~ 100 ms**; cite this number.
> 4. **Skipping units in the formula sheet-style question:** KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark for missing SI units.
> 5. **Mixing up EndoWrist DOF:** It is **7**, not 6. The seventh is gripper actuation, and the da Vinci adds it as a *functional* DOF beyond the 6 spatial DOF.

---

## Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]
**Define Telerobotic-Assisted Minimally Invasive Surgery. List any four advantages it offers over conventional laparoscopy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Telerobotic-Assisted Minimally Invasive Surgery (TARMIS) is a surgical modality in which a surgeon at a remote console commands a patient-side robotic slave through small body-wall ports, with the system enforcing motion scaling, tremor filtering, and a software/mechanical Remote Center of Motion (RCM) at each port.

*Advantages over conventional laparoscopy:*
1. 3-D stereoscopic vision restoring depth perception.
2. 7-DOF EndoWrist instrumentation recovering lost dexterity.
3. Elimination of the fulcrum-effect kinematic inversion.
4. Digital tremor filtration (cutoff ~ 8 Hz) and 5:1 motion scaling for sub-millimetric precision.
5. Ergonomic seated posture reducing surgeon fatigue.
*(Any four — 0.5 mark each; definition 1 mark)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]
**What is a Remote Center of Motion (RCM)? Why is it essential in TARMIS?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Remote Center of Motion (RCM)** is a *fixed spatial point* — the abdominal port — through which the surgical instrument must always pass while remaining free to translate along and rotate about the cannula axis. Mathematically, the velocity of the port-point on the instrument is constrained to be zero: $\dot{p}_{\text{port}} = 0$.

*Why essential:*
1. Prevents the instrument from pushing/pulling against the patient's body wall, avoiding tissue trauma.
2. Maintains a stable pivot, decoupling instrument motion from patient breathing or small movements.
3. Reduces the active joint count by 1 (passive RCM) or constrains the controller to a 1-DOF null space (active RCM), simplifying safety analysis.
*(Definition 1.5 marks, significance 1.5 marks)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, Module 3, Choice 1]**
