**(a)** With the aid of a block diagram, describe the **functional architecture** of a Telerobotic-Assisted Minimally Invasive Surgery system. Clearly identify the surgeon console, vision cart, patient cart, and the bilateral control loop. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **force-reflection gain** $\alpha$ that preserves the surgeon's perceived mechanical impedance when the **motion-scaling factor is $k$**, under a power-equivalent (energy-conserving) design. State clearly the assumption of energy/power preservation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Functional Architecture (7 marks):**

A TARMIS system has three coupled subsystems (3 marks for naming, 2 marks for diagram, 2 marks for explanation).

1. **Surgeon Console** — ergonomic seated workstation housing two **master manipulators** (typically 7-DOF gimbaled arms), a **stereoscopic 3-D HD viewer**, and a **foot-pedal array** for instrument clutch, electrocautery, and camera control. The surgeon's hand motion is read by encoders at the master joints at 1 kHz.
2. **Vision & Control Cart** — contains the **camera control unit (CCU)**, the **illumination source**, the **insufflator interface**, and the **real-time control computer** (multi-core CPU + FPGA) running the bilateral control law and the 1 kHz safety watchdog.
3. **Patient Cart** — three to four robotic arms mounted on a movable base. One arm carries a **12 mm stereo endoscope**; the others carry **8 mm EndoWrist instruments** with distal wrists. Each arm incorporates a **passive or active RCM** mechanism.

**Bilateral control loop** (2 marks): The forward path scales and filters the master command before issuing it to the slave; the backward path senses tool–tissue force, multiplies by $\alpha$, and reflects it to the master actuator. The foot pedal provides discrete set-point overrides.

*Block diagram (refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid architecture).*

**(b) Derivation of $\alpha = k^2$ (7 marks):**

Let:
- Master hand position $x_m$, velocity $v_m$.
- Slave tip position $x_s = k x_m$ → $v_s = k v_m$.
- Environment (tissue) exerts force $f_e$ on the slave.
- Controller exerts reflected force $f_m$ on the master.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Open-surgery impedance felt by the surgeon:
$$Z_{\text{open}} = \frac{f_e}{v_m}\Big|_{\text{open}}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** In TARMIS, tissue interaction in the slave frame is governed by the local stiffness $K_t$:
$$f_e = K_t \cdot \Delta x_s = K_t \cdot k \cdot \Delta x_m$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** The impedance *perceived* at the master is:
$$Z_{\text{master}} = \frac{f_m}{v_m} = \frac{\alpha f_e}{v_m} = \frac{\alpha K_t k \Delta x_m}{v_m} = \alpha k \cdot Z_{\text{open}}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Power-equivalent / energy-conserving design requires the power delivered to the master to equal the power extracted from the slave scaled by $k^2$. This is achieved by enforcing the relation between force and motion scaling as:
$$\alpha = k^2$$

Then $Z_{\text{master}} = k^3 \cdot Z_{\text{open}}$. For *impedance matching* (not power conservation), the alternative condition is $\alpha k = 1 \Rightarrow \alpha = 1/k$, which is the *transparent* but *non-passive* condition and not preferred for safety.

> **Final boxed result:** $\boxed{\alpha = k^2}$ for power-equivalent TARMIS scaling.

---

### Question B — (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, Module 3, Choice 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **Remote Center of Motion (RCM)** constraint mathematically. Show that enforcing RCM reduces the effective DOF of an $n$-joint instrument by exactly 1. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **limitations of conventional laparoscopy** and explain, with reference to the **da Vinci Surgical System**, how TARMIS overcomes each limitation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Mathematical RCM Constraint (7 marks):**

**Statement (1 mark):** Let the port be a fixed point $p_0 \in \mathbb{R}^3$. The forward kinematics gives the port-point on the instrument as a function of joint variables $q \in \mathbb{R}^n$:
$$p = f(q) \in \mathbb{R}^3$$

**Holonomic RCM constraint (1 mark):**
$$p(q) = p_0 \;\;\text{(constant in time)}$$

**Differentiating (1 mark):**
$$\dot{p} = J_p(q)\dot{q} = 0_{3\times 1}$$
where $J_p = \frac{\partial f}{\partial q}$ is the $3 \times n$ Jacobian of the port-point.

**Rank argument (2 marks):** For a non-degenerate RCM, the cannula axis is the only instantaneous direction in which the port-point is *not* free to move; equivalently, the row space of $J_p$ has rank 1. Thus the constraint $J_p \dot q = 0$ imposes **one** linearly independent scalar condition on $\dot q$.

**Null-space and DOF reduction (2 marks):** The admissible joint velocities lie in the null space of $J_p$:
$$\dot q \in \ker(J_p), \qquad \dim \ker(J_p) = n - \text{rank}(J_p) = n - 1$$

Therefore the **effective task-space DOF at the instrument tip inside the body** is $n-1$.

*Numerical illustration:* For the da Vinci's 4-DOF passive RCM arm $n = 4$, the tip has $3$ active DOF inside the body. Combined with the **4-DOF EndoWrist wrist** (pitch, yaw, roll, grip) distal to the cannula, the total functional DOF reaches $3 + 4 = 7$.

**(b) Limitations of Conventional Laparoscopy and TARMIS Overcomes Them (7 marks):**

| # | Limitation of Conventional Laparoscopy | TARMIS (da Vinci) Solution | Marks |
|---|----------------------------------------|----------------------------|-------|
| 1 | 2-D monocular camera → loss of depth perception | **12 mm stereoscopic endoscope** with two parallel HD CMOS sensors viewed through a 3-D headset, restoring natural depth | 1.5 |
| 2 | Rigid, non-articulating instruments → only 4 DOF and poor dexterity | **EndoWrist instruments** with distal pitch, yaw, and roll joints → **7 DOF** at the tip | 1.5 |
| 3 | Fulcrum effect at port → handle and tip motion are inverted | Software-implemented **RCM** restores intuitive 1:1 directional mapping | 1 |
| 4 | Amplification of physiological hand tremor (8–12 Hz) | Digital **low-pass filter** at master input with cutoff ~ 8 Hz | 1 |
| 5 | Surgeon's hands are crossed and elevated, causing fatigue | **Ergonomic seated console** with masters positioned at natural hand height | 1 |
| 6 | Camera held by an assistant → unstable view | **Surgeon-controlled endoscope** with foot-pedal clutching | 1 |

*(Allocate marks proportionally; full marks require naming the da Vinci-specific components: EndoWrist, stereo endoscope, surgeon console, RCM linkage.)*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 — TARMIS)**

- **TARMIS = Master + Slave + Digital Controller + Vision System**, communicating over a (possibly delayed) channel.
- **Three subsystems:** Surgeon Console, Vision & Control Cart, Patient Cart.
- **Conventional MIS = 4 DOF**; **TARMIS = 7 DOF** at the instrument tip.
- **Motion scaling:** $x_s = k x_m$, with $k \in [1/5,\, 1/2]$.
- **Force-reflection gain (power-equivalent):** $\alpha = k^2$.
- **Impedance-matching (alternative):** $\alpha k = 1$.
- **RCM constraint:** $\dot p_{\text{port}} = 0$, reduces effective DOF by 1.
- **Round-trip delay bound for stable teleoperation:** $\tau_{rt} < 100$ ms (without predictor).
- **Tremor filter cutoff:** ~ 8 Hz (below physiological tremor band 8–12 Hz).
- **Bilateral architectures:** P–P, P–F, F–P, Four-channel, Wave-variable (best for delay).
- **da Vinci EndoWrist:** 7 DOF including gripper; 8 mm and 12 mm instrument diameters; ±540° continuous roll.
- **Wave variables (Niemeyer–Slotine):** $u, v$ transforms guarantee **passivity** under arbitrary delay.
- **Surgical advantages of TARMIS:** smaller incisions, less blood loss, shorter hospital stay, lower infection rate, faster recovery.
- **Engineering challenges:** sterility of drapes, time-delay stability, force-reflection safety ($\alpha \le 0.5$ in practice), and the *open-surgery haptic gap* — most commercial TARMIS still have **no haptic feedback** to the surgeon (a hot KTU viva question).
- **Examples of TARMIS platforms (cite in answers):** *da Vinci Xi* (Intuitive Surgical), *Hugo RAS* (Medtronic), *Versius* (CMR Surgical), *Miro* and *MiroSurge* (DLR), *Senhance* (Asensus), *MicroHand S* (Chinese platform).
- **Safety watchdog runs at ≥ 1 kHz** with hardware-enforced E-stop independent of the main CPU.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Design Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT724 - MEDICAL ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Robot Design Concepts # Robot Design Concepts in Medical Robotics ## 1. Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Robot Design Concepts** in medical robotics refer to the integrated engineering methodology used to architect, dimension, and configure a robotic manipulator intended for clinical, surgical, rehabilitation, or assistive applications. It encompasses the selection of **kinematic structure**, **degrees of freedom (DOF)**, **actuation topology**, **workspace geometry**, **mechanical transmission**, **stiffness and load-bearing capacity**, and **biocompatibility/sterilization compliance** such that the resulting system satisfies the safety, precision, and ergonomic constraints mandated by the operating room or patient-care environment. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, robot design is treated as the *convergence* of three engineering sub-disciplines: 1. **Kinematic Design** – topology, link lengths, joint types (revolute/prismatic/spherical). 2. **Mechanical Design** – materials, structural rigidity, backlash, fatigue life. 3. **Task-Driven Design** – matching the robot's performance envelope (resolution, payload, repeatability) to the medical procedure (MIS surgery, radiosurgery, biopsy, rehabilitation). --- ## 2. Intuitive Overview – "The Surgical Assistant Analogy" > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** Imagine a senior cardiac surgeon tasked with suturing a 1 mm vessel inside a beating heart. He cannot hold his hand perfectly still — his hand trembles by ~50–100 µm. He also cannot see the vessel directly. To overcome this, he trains a surgical apprentice and gives him a pair of **tremor-free, motion-scaled, scaled-vision instruments**. The apprentice is the **medical robot**, and the way you "design" the apprentice (tall/short, strong/weak, 2 arms or 4 arms, how the wrists bend, how the gripper closes) is the **Robot Design Concept**. A well-designed medical robot is essentially the surgeon's **geometric and force-amplifying extension**. Every design choice — the length of an arm, the angle of a wrist joint, the placement of motors — exists to either: - Bring the surgical tip exactly where the surgeon wants it (kinematic design), - Hold it there without shaking (stiffness design), - Or do so safely inside a sterile field (sterilization/biocompatibility design). ## 3. Core Design Metrics Used in Medical Robotics | Metric | Typical Surgical Robot Value | Engineering Meaning | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** | **6 to 7** | Independent motions of the end-effector | | **Payload** | **2 – 5 N** | Force the robot can exert on tissue | | **Position Resolution** | **< 50 µm** | Smallest controllable displacement | | **Repeatability** | **± 0.05 mm** | Ability to return to a taught pose | | **Workspace Volume** | **0.001 – 0.01 m³** | Reachable region in the patient | | **Stiffness** | **> 10 N/mm** | Resistance to deflection under load | | **Mass of Moving Structure** | **< 5 kg** | Affects inertia and safety | | **Backlash** | **< 0.1°** | Lost motion in gears/joints | # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet ## 1. The Robot Design Workflow (Engineering Logic Steps) A medical robot is **not** designed randomly. It follows a structured, iterative loop: 1. **Task Analysis** – Identify the surgical/clinical task (e.g., laparoscopic suturing, neurosurgical biopsy). 2. **Workspace Definition** – Compute the 3D region the end-effector must reach inside the patient. 3. **DOF Allocation** – Decide how many joints the robot needs (3 for positioning + 3 for orientation → typical 6-DOF). 4. **Kinematic Structure Selection** – Choose between serial, parallel, or hybrid architectures. 5. **Link Length Optimization** – Use inverse kinematics and dexterity indices to size each link. 6. **Actuation & Transmission** – Decide on motors (DC servo, brushless, pneumatic muscle) and drivetrain (harmonic drive, cable, direct drive). 7. **Stiffness & Deflection Analysis** – Use finite element or analytical beam models to check tip deflection. 8. **Sterilization & Material Compliance** – Select autoclavable materials (stainless steel 316L, titanium, PEEK). 9. **Safety & Redundancy** – Add backups (dual encoders, watchdog timers, mechanical brakes). > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" behind Step 3 (DOF Allocation):** A rigid body in 3D space has exactly **6 DOF** (3 translations + 3 rotations). Therefore, a fully general-purpose medical manipulator must have **at least 6 DOF**. If a port is used (e.g., in laparoscopic surgery), the port itself removes 2 DOF, so the robot can compensate with only 4 additional DOF outside the body — this is the **Remote Center of Motion (RCM)** concept central to the da Vinci system. --- ## 2. KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula Name | Equation | Application in Robot Design | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Grübler's Mobility Formula** | $M \;=\; d\,(n - g - 1) \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{g} f_i$ | Computes DOF of a planar/spatial mechanism | | **Position Resolution** | $\Delta x \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta q \cdot L}{n_{red}}$ | Smallest end-effector step per encoder tick | | **Mechanical Advantage** | $MA \;=\; \dfrac{F_{out}}{F_{in}} \;=\; \dfrac{d_{in}}{d_{out}}$ | Force amplification via gear/cable | | **Joint Stiffness Relation** | $k_{joint} \;=\; \dfrac{G \cdot J}{L}$ | Torsional rigidity of a transmission shaft | | **Cantilever Beam Tip Deflection** | $\delta_{tip} \;=\; \dfrac{F \cdot L^{3}}{3\,E\,I}$ | Maximum tip sag of a robot link | | **Workspace Volume (Spherical Approx.)** | $V_{ws} \;=\; \dfrac{4}{3}\,\pi\,(L_{max} - L_{min})^{3}$ | Reach envelope of the manipulator | | **Jacobian Velocity Mapping** | $\dot{X} \;=\; J(q)\,\dot{q}$ | Joint-to-Cartesian velocity transformation | | **Force Transmission (Static)** | $\tau \;=\; J^{T}\,F$ | Joint torque required for a given end-effector force | | **Singularity Condition** | $\det(J) \;=\; 0$ | Identifies loss of DOF (design must avoid this in surgical workspace) | | **Hertzian Contact Stress** | $\sigma_{max} \;=\; \dfrac{3\,F}{2\,\pi\,a^{2}}$ | Stress at gear-tooth or ball-bearing contact | | **Repeatability (Statistical)** | $R \;=\; k\,\sigma_{pos}$ | k = 3 corresponds to 99.7% confidence (3σ rule) | | **Dextrous Workspace Fraction** | $W_{dex} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{dexterous}}{V_{reachable}}$ | Quality metric; ideally > 0.5 | > **Notation Legend:** $M$ = mobility (DOF), $d$ = degrees of freedom of the unconstrained space (2 planar, 3 spatial), $n$ = number of links, $g$ = number of joints, $f_i$ = DOF of $i^{th}$ joint, $L$ = link length, $E$ = Young's modulus, $I$ = area moment of inertia, $J$ = Jacobian matrix, $q$ = joint variable, $X$ = Cartesian variable, $\sigma$ = standard deviation. --- ## 3. Engineering Real-World Utility - **Surgical Precision:** The **tip deflection formula** $\delta_{tip} = FL^{3}/(3EI)$ is why surgical robot links are short, thick, and made of high-$E$ materials (carbon fiber, Ti-6Al-4V) — even a 10 mm long link deflects far less than a 100 mm one, for the same force. - **da Vinci Patient-Side Cart:** Uses a **4-bar parallelogram linkage** to achieve the RCM. Grübler's formula applied to this linkage gives $M = 1$ — exactly what is needed: one rotational DOF pivoting about the trocar. - **NeuroArm (Canada):** Designed with **isometric (force-equalizing) mechanisms** so that the surgeon feels realistic haptic feedback during tumor resection. - **Haptic Surgical Robots:** Use the **$\tau = J^{T} F$** relationship in reverse to display force to the surgeon — this is the *impedance/admittance* control foundation. - **Rehabilitation Robots (Lokomat):** Designed with **series-elastic actuators (SEA)** to provide safe, compliant interaction with the patient's limbs. # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## Derivation 1: Degrees of Freedom via Grübler's Formula > [!IMPORTANT] > Grübler's formula is a **topological** count of how a kinematic chain can move. It assumes **rigid links**, **ideal joints**, and **no redundant constraints**. **Step 1 – Identify the dimension of the ambient space.** If the robot operates in a plane: $d = 2$. If it operates in 3D space: $d = 3$. $$ d = \begin{cases} 2, & \text{planar mechanism} \\ 3, & \text{spatial mechanism} \end{cases} $$ **Step 2 – Count the total number of rigid links (n).** Each link is a rigid body. The ground (fixed base) is **not** counted as a link in some texts but **is** counted in Grübler's original form. Using the inclusive convention: $$ n \;\text{includes the ground link} $$ **Step 3 – Count the number of joints (g).** Each revolute (R) joint contributes $f = 1$. Each prismatic (P) joint contributes $f = 1$. A spherical (S) joint contributes $f = 3$. A universal (U) joint contributes $f = 2$. **Step 4 – Apply the formula.** $$ M \;=\; d\,(n - g - 1) \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{g} f_i $$ **Step 5 – Worked Example: 6-DOF Anthropomorphic Surgical Arm.** - $d = 3$ (spatial) - $n = 7$ (1 base + 6 moving links) - $g = 6$ (six revolute joints) - $f_i = 1$ for all $i$ $$ M \;=\; 3\,(7 - 6 - 1) \;+\; (1+1+1+1+1+1) $$ $$ M \;=\; 3\,(0) \;+\; 6 \;=\; 6 \text{ DOF} $$ > **Conversion Logic:** The term $d(n-g-1)$ accounts for the *net unconstrained* degrees of the assembled chain in free space. The summation $\sum f_i$ adds back the constraints each joint relaxes. The result is the **true mobility** of the robot. --- ## Derivation 2: Tip Deflection of a Surgical Robot Link **Step 1 – Model the link as a cantilever beam** with one end fixed (at the joint actuator housing) and the other end free (carrying the payload $F$). **Step 2 – Apply the Euler-Bernoulli beam theory equation:** $$ E\,I\,\frac{d^{4}y}{dx^{4}} \;=\; w(x) $$ where $w(x)$ is the distributed load. For a point load $F$ at the tip, $w(x) = 0$ for $0 \le x < L$ and $w(L) = F$. **Step 3 – Integrate four times with the cantilever boundary conditions** at $x = 0$: $$ y(0) = 0, \quad \frac{dy}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x=0} = 0 $$ The general solution after two integrations gives bending moment: $$ M(x) \;=\; E\,I\,\frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} \;=\; -F\,(L - x) $$ **Step 4 – Integrate twice more to obtain deflection:** $$ \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} \;=\; -\frac{F\,(L - x)}{E\,I} $$ Integrating from $x$ to $L$ (using $dy/dx \vert_{x=0} = 0$): $$ \frac{dy}{dx} \;=\; -\frac{F}{E\,I}\left(Lx - \frac{x^{2}}{2}\right) + C_{1}, \quad C_{1} = 0 $$ Integrating again (using $y(0) = 0$): $$ y(x) \;=\; -\frac{F}{E\,I}\left(\frac{Lx^{2}}{2} - \frac{x^{3}}{6}\right) $$ **Step 5 – Evaluate at the tip $x = L$:** $$ \delta_{tip} \;=\; y(L) \;=\; -\frac{F}{E\,I}\left(\frac{L^{3}}{2} - \frac{L^{3}}{6}\right) $$ $$ \delta_{tip} \;=\; -\frac{F}{E\,I}\left(\frac{3L^{3} - L^{3}}{6}\right) \;=\; -\frac{F\,L^{3}}{3\,E\,I} $$ The negative sign indicates downward deflection. Taking magnitude: $$ \boxed{\;\delta_{tip} \;=\; \frac{F\,L^{3}}{3\,E\,I}\;} $$ **Step 6 – Numerical Check (da Vinci Instrument Shaft):** - $F = 2$ N (tissue contact force) - $L = 0.4$ m (long instrument) - $E = 110$ GPa (titanium) - $I = \pi d^{4}/64$ for $d = 8$ mm $$ I \;=\; \frac{\pi (8 \times 10^{-3})^{4}}{64} \;=\; 2.01 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}^{4} $$ $$ \delta_{tip} \;=\; \frac{(2)\,(0.4)^{3}}{3\,(110 \times 10^{9})\,(2.01 \times 10^{-10})} $$ $$ \delta_{tip} \;=\; \frac{0.128}{66.33} \;\approx\; 1.93 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \;=\; 1.93 \text{ mm} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This 1.93 mm deflection is **unacceptable** for microsurgery. Therefore, surgical robots are designed with **short, thick links** *or* **parallel kinematic structures** (e.g., the Delta robot in the Sphinx XT system) to keep $L$ small and $I$ large. --- ## Derivation 3: Workspace Volume of a Spherical-Coordinate Manipulator **Step 1 – Define the link-length constraint.** For a spherical-coordinate robot, the radial reach $r$ lies between $r_{min}$ and $r_{max}$: $$ r_{min} \le r \le r_{max} $$ **Step 2 – The polar (vertical) angle $\phi$ and azimuthal angle $\theta$** also have bounds, but for a *fully spherical* idealized design, $\phi \in [0, \pi]$ and $\theta \in [0, 2\pi]$. **Step 3 – Integrate in spherical coordinates:** $$ V_{ws} \;=\; \int_{0}^{2\pi}\!\int_{0}^{\pi}\!\int_{r_{min}}^{r_{max}} r^{2}\,\sin\phi\,dr\,d\phi\,d\theta $$ **Step 4 – Evaluate the radial integral:** $$ \int_{r_{min}}^{r_{max}} r^{2}\,dr \;=\; \frac{1}{3}\left(r_{max}^{3} - r_{min}^{3}\right) $$ **Step 5 – Evaluate the angular integrals:** $$ \int_{0}^{\pi} \sin\phi\,d\phi \;=\; 2 $$ $$ \int_{0}^{2\pi} d\theta \;=\; 2\pi $$ **Step 6 – Combine:** $$ V_{ws} \;=\; (2\pi)(2)\,\cdot\,\frac{1}{3}\left(r_{max}^{3} - r_{min}^{3}\right) $$ $$ \boxed{\;V_{ws} \;=\; \frac{4\pi}{3}\left(r_{max}^{3} - r_{min}^{3}\right)\;} $$ > **Conversion Logic:** This is the volume of a spherical **shell** — the region between two concentric spheres. The result $(4\pi/3)\,(r_{max}^{3} - r_{min}^{3})$ is intuitive: it is the difference of the volumes of two full spheres of radii $r_{max}$ and $r_{min}$. --- ## Derivation 4: Singularity Condition from the Jacobian **Step 1 – The Jacobian $J(q)$** is the matrix mapping joint velocities $\dot{q}$ to Cartesian end-effector velocities $\dot{X}$: $$ \dot{X} \;=\; J(q)\,\dot{q} $$ **Step 2 – For a 2-link planar arm** with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$ and joint angles $q_1$ and $q_2$: $$ x \;=\; L_1\cos q_1 \;+\; L_2\cos(q_1 + q_2) $$ $$ y \;=\; L_1\sin q_1 \;+\; L_2\sin(q_1 + q_2) $$ **Step 3 – Differentiate with respect to time:** $$ \dot{x} \;=\; -L_1\sin q_1\,\dot{q}_1 \;-\; L_2\sin(q_1+q_2)\,(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2) $$ $$ \dot{y} \;=\; L_1\cos q_1\,\dot{q}_1 \;+\; L_2\cos(q_1+q_2)\,(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2) $$ **Step 4 – Assemble the Jacobian matrix:** $$ J(q) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} -L_1\sin q_1 - L_2\sin(q_1+q_2) & -L_2\sin(q_1+q_2) \\ L_1\cos q_1 + L_2\cos(q_1+q_2) & L_2\cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 5 – Compute the determinant:** $$ \det(J) \;=\; \left(-L_1\sin q_1 - L_2\sin(q_1+q_2)\right)\left(L_2\cos(q_1+q_2)\right) - \left(-L_2\sin(q_1+q_2)\right)\left(L_1\cos q_1 + L_2\cos(q_1+q_2)\right) $$ After expansion and cancellation: $$ \det(J) \;=\; L_1\,L_2\,\sin q_2 $$ **Step 6 – Set the determinant to zero to find the singular configuration:** $$ \det(J) \;=\; 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \sin q_2 \;=\; 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad q_2 \;=\; 0 \text{ or } \pi $$ > **Engineering Meaning:** When $q_2 = 0$, the arm is **fully stretched out**; when $q_2 = \pi$, the arm is **folded back on itself**. In both cases, the manipulator loses one degree of freedom and cannot move in certain Cartesian directions. Surgical robot designers place a **software singularity-avoidance envelope** around these poses to prevent the robot from entering them. # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## Diagram 1: Robot Design Workflow (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Task Analysis
Identify Surgical Procedure] --> B[Workspace Definition
Compute Reach Envelope] B --> C[DOF Allocation
Decide 6 or 7 Joints] C --> D[Kinematic Structure
Serial vs Parallel vs Hybrid] D --> E[Link Length Sizing
Use Inverse Kinematics] E --> F[Actuation Selection
DC Servo / BLDC / Pneumatic] F --> G[Stiffness Analysis
Euler-Bernoulli Check] G --> H[Material Selection
Ti-6Al-4V / SS 316L / PEEK] H --> I[Sterilization Design
Autoclave Compatibility] I --> J[Safety Layer
Dual Encoder / Watchdog / Brake] J --> K[Prototype Fabrication] K --> L{Validation Tests} L -->|Pass| M[Clinical Trial] L -->|Fail| E ``` > **Reading the Diagram:** Each block is a design phase. The arrow from `L → E` is the iterative feedback loop — if validation fails (e.g., tip deflection too high, repeatability out of spec), the designer returns to the link-sizing stage and re-iterates. --- ## Diagram 2: Kinematic Chain of a 6-DOF Surgical Arm ```mermaid flowchart LR BASE[Base Frame
Fixed to Patient Side Cart] --> J1[Joint 1
Revolute Z] J1 --> L1[Link 1
Shoulder Yaw] L1 --> J2[Joint 2
Revolute Y] J2 --> L2[Link 2
Upper Arm] L2 --> J3[Joint 3
Revolute Y] J3 --> L3[Link 3
Forearm] L3 --> J4[Joint 4
Revolute Y] J4 --> L4[Link 4
Wrist Pitch 1] L4 --> J5[Joint 5
Revolute Z] J5 --> L5[Link 5
Wrist Pitch 2] L5 --> J6[Joint 6
Revolute Y] J6 --> TOOL[End Effector
Grasper / Scalpel / Camera] ``` > **Reading the Diagram:** This is a classic **anthropomorphic (RRRRRR)** kinematic chain similar to the da Vinci Patient-Side Manipulator. The first three revolute joints (J1, J2, J3) handle **positioning** of the wrist; the last three (J4, J5, J6) handle **orientation** of the tool — this is the *spherical-wrist* design principle. --- ## Diagram 3: Cantilever Beam Model for Tip Deflection ```mermaid flowchart LR WALL[Fixed Wall
Joint Housing] -->|Length L| BEAM[Surgical Link
Cross-Section I] BEAM -->|Load F| TIP[End Effector
Deflection delta] BEAM -.->|E Young Modulus| MATERIAL[(Material Property)] MATERIAL -.-> BEAM ``` > **Reading the Diagram:** Maps the analytical variables of the deflection equation $\delta_{tip} = F L^{3} / (3 E I)$ to their physical interpretations in the robot structure. Increasing $E$ or $I$ decreases deflection; increasing $F$ or $L$ increases it. --- ## Diagram 4: RCM (Remote Center of Motion) Principle ```mermaid flowchart TB TROCAR[Trocar / Port
Fixed Entry Point on Patient] -->|Pivot Point| P1[Parallelogram Link 1] P1 --> P2[Parallelogram Link 2] P2 --> INSTR[Instrument Tip
Inserts Through Trocar] P2 --> MANIP[Manipulator Handle
Outside Patient Body] MANIP -.->|Constrained Motion| P1 MANIP -.->|Constrained Motion| P2 ``` > **Reading the Diagram:** The two parallelogram linkages force the instrument tip to **always pass through the trocar**, creating a virtual pivot at the patient's skin. The surgeon can move the handle freely in any direction, but the tool shaft pivots — never pushes — against the incision. # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **List and briefly explain any THREE design considerations specific to medical (surgical) robots that distinguish them from industrial robots.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Sterilization Compatibility:** Medical robots must withstand repeated autoclaving (134 °C, 30 psi steam) or Sterrad plasma sterilization. Materials like stainless steel 316L, titanium, and PEEK are preferred over aluminum or standard plastics. 2. **Biocompatibility:** Any part of the robot that may contact the patient (e.g., instrument shaft, forceps) must use ISO 10993-compliant materials to avoid cytotoxic, allergic, or inflammatory reactions. 3. **Backlash & Reversal Error:** Surgical robots require backlash of < 0.1° to avoid "jitter" when the surgeon changes direction of motion. Industrial robots tolerate backlash 5–10× higher. 4. **Workspace Constrained by Trocar:** Unlike industrial robots with open workspaces, surgical robots must operate through a small entry port, necessitating the **Remote Center of Motion (RCM)** kinematic constraint. 5. **Safety & Redundancy:** Dual encoders, mechanical brakes, and force-torque limiters are mandatory. An industrial robot that fails is a financial loss; a surgical robot that fails is a life-threatening event. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define "dexterous workspace" of a robot. Why is it important in surgical robot design?** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** The **dexterous workspace** of a robotic manipulator is the set of all points in the reachable workspace at which the end-effector can be arbitrarily oriented — i.e., every orientation of the tool is achievable at that point. **Importance in Surgical Robotics:** - **Orientation flexibility** is critical: a laparoscopic grasper may need to be vertical for tissue retraction, tilted 30° for suturing, and horizontal for clipping a vessel — all at the *same* incision point. - A surgical robot designed without a sufficient dexterous workspace will be limited in the **types of procedures** it can perform, even if its reachable workspace is large. - A useful design metric is the **dexterous-to-reachable workspace ratio** $W_{dex}/W_{reach}$; a value > 0.5 is considered good practice. - A spherical wrist (concentrating the last 3 joints at a single point) maximizes this ratio. --- ## Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Internal Choice:** Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks **Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]:** With the help of a suitable diagram, explain the **kinematic structure of a 6-DOF surgical manipulator** used in laparoscopic surgery. Discuss why 6 DOF are required and how the last 3 joints form a spherical wrist. **Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]:** A surgical robot link is modeled as a cantilever beam of length $L = 0.3$ m, circular cross-section of diameter $d = 10$ mm, and material titanium alloy with $E = 110$ GPa. The end-effector applies a force $F = 1.5$ N perpendicular to the link. Calculate the **tip deflection** and comment on whether it is acceptable for microsurgery. **Model Solution:** #### (a) Kinematic Structure Explanation [7 marks] **Diagram Description (2 marks):** A 6-DOF surgical manipulator consists of: - **Base → Joint 1 (R, vertical axis)** – Rotates the entire arm about the vertical axis (yaw). - **Link 1 → Joint 2 (R, horizontal axis)** – Shoulder pitch. - **Link 2 → Joint 3 (R, horizontal axis)** – Elbow pitch. - **Link 3 → Joint 4 (R, longitudinal axis)** – Wrist roll. - **Link 4 → Joint 5 (R, pitch axis)** – Wrist pitch. - **Link 5 → Joint 6 (R, yaw axis)** – Tool yaw. **Why 6 DOF (3 marks):** A rigid body in 3D space has 6 independent DOF — 3 translations ($x, y, z$) and 3 rotations (roll, pitch, yaw). For a surgical tool to reach a point *and* align at any orientation, 6 DOF are mathematically necessary and sufficient. Fewer than 6 DOF restrict the surgeon's ability to approach anatomy from arbitrary angles; more than 6 (redundancy) can be added for obstacle avoidance and dexterity optimization. **Spherical Wrist Concept (2 marks):** The last three joints (J4, J5, J6) have **intersecting axes** at a single point (the wrist center). This is called a **spherical wrist**. Its benefits: - Kinematics **decouple** — position of the wrist is set by joints 1–3; orientation is set by joints 4–6. - Inverse kinematics becomes **analytically tractable** (closed-form solutions exist). - The **dexterous workspace** is maximized. #### (b) Tip Deflection Calculation [7 marks] **Given:** - $L = 0.3$ m - $d = 10$ mm = $0.01$ m - $E = 110$ GPa = $110 \times 10^{9}$ Pa - $F = 1.5$ N **Step 1 — Compute the area moment of inertia for a circular cross-section:** $$ I \;=\; \frac{\pi\,d^{4}}{64} \;=\; \frac{\pi\,(0.01)^{4}}{64} $$ $$ I \;=\; \frac{\pi \times 10^{-8}}{64} \;=\; 4.909 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}^{4} $$ **Step 2 — Apply the cantilever tip-deflection formula:** $$ \delta_{tip} \;=\; \frac{F\,L^{3}}{3\,E\,I} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute values:** $$ \delta_{tip} \;=\; \frac{1.5 \times (0.3)^{3}}{3 \times (110 \times 10^{9}) \times (4.909 \times 10^{-10})} $$ $$ \delta_{tip} \;=\; \frac{1.5 \times 0.027}{3 \times 53.999} $$ $$ \delta_{tip} \;=\; \frac{0.0405}{161.997} $$ $$ \delta_{tip} \;\approx\; 2.50 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \;=\; 0.25 \text{ mm} \;=\; 250 \text{ µm} $$ **Step 4 — Comment on acceptability (2 marks):** Microsurgery typically requires positional accuracy better than **100 µm** (0.1 mm). The calculated 250 µm deflection is **2.5× higher** than this threshold. This link alone would be **unacceptable for microsurgery** without further design improvements, such as: - Reducing link length $L$ (deflection scales as $L^3$). - Increasing diameter $d$ (deflection scales as $1/d^4$). - Switching to a higher-$E$ material (e.g., carbon fiber, $E \approx 200$ GPa). - Using a **parallel kinematic structure** to share the load across multiple struts. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Many students **forget to convert mm to m** in the diameter or the GPa to Pa. The cross-section diameter of 10 mm is $10 \times 10^{-3}$ m, **not** 0.01 m **is correct**, but the fourth power $(0.01)^4$ is the most common error — ensure you compute $10^{-8}$, not $10^{-4}$. A single unit error gives a wildly wrong answer worth zero credit. --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks **Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]:** Using **Grübler's formula**, derive the degrees of freedom of (i) a 4-bar parallelogram linkage used for RCM in the da Vinci system, and (ii) a Stewart-Gough parallel platform used in radiosurgery robots. **Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **stiffness design considerations** for surgical robot links. Why are short links, large cross-sections, and high-modulus materials preferred? Support your answer with the relevant equation. **Model Solution:** #### (a) Grübler's Formula Applications [7 marks] **Recall Grübler's Formula (1 mark):** $$ M \;=\; d\,(n - g - 1) \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{g} f_i $$ **(i) 4-Bar Parallelogram Linkage (3 marks):** - Operates in a plane: $d = 2$ - Links: $n = 4$ (ground + 3 moving bars) - Joints: $g = 4$ (all revolute, $f_i = 1$ for each) $$ M \;=\; 2\,(4 - 4 - 1) \;+\; (1+1+1+1) $$ $$ M \;=\; 2\,(-1) \;+\; 4 \;=\; -2 + 4 \;=\; 2 \text{ DOF} $$ > **Correction Note:** A *true* parallelogram has a *kinematic constraint* that makes it equivalent to a **1-DOF mechanism** (the diagonal link constrains the figure). In the da Vinci implementation, the parallelogram is *isostatic* (exactly constrained), and only **1 DOF** is actuated — pure rotation about the trocar. The Grübler calculation must account for the **redundant constraint** introduced by the parallel side-equal link lengths, which removes 1 DOF: > > $$ > M_{effective} \;=\; 2\,(4 - 4 - 1) + 4 - 1 \;=\; 1 \text{ DOF} > $$ **(ii) Stewart-Gough Platform (3 marks):** - Spatial mechanism: $d = 3$ - Links: $n = 14$ (1 base + 1 platform + 6 upper struts + 6 lower struts) — *depending on counting convention* - Joints: $g = 6$ spherical (each $f = 3$) + $6$ universal (each $f = 2$) — using the U-S-S form, $g = 6$ U-joints + $6$ S-joints = 12 joints Using a common compact form (treating each leg as one strut with 2 joints): $$ n = 8 \text{ (base + 6 struts + platform)}, \quad g = 12 \text{ (6 U + 6 S)} $$ $$ M \;=\; 3\,(8 - 12 - 1) \;+\; (6\times 2 + 6\times 3) $$ $$ M \;=\; 3\,(-5) \;+\; (12 + 18) \;=\; -15 + 30 \;=\; 6 \text{ DOF} $$ This is the **6-DOF** motion of the platform (3 translations + 3 rotations) — exactly what is needed for radiosurgery. #### (b) Stiffness Design Considerations [7 marks] **Core Equation (2 marks):** $$ \delta_{tip} \;=\; \frac{F\,L^{3}}{3\,E\,I} $$ **Discussion (5 marks):** 1. **Short links ($L$):** Deflection scales with the **cube** of the length. Halving $L$ reduces deflection by **8×**. Hence, surgical robots are designed with compact, short arms (e.g., the da Vinci instruments are < 0.4 m long) even at the cost of reach. 2. **Large cross-sections ($I$):** For a circular cross-section, $I = \pi d^{4}/64$, so $I$ scales as the **fourth power** of the diameter. Doubling the diameter increases $I$ by **16×**, reducing deflection by the same factor. Hollow tubes are used to increase $I$ without adding excessive mass. 3. **High Young's modulus ($E$):** Materials with high $E$ resist elastic deformation. Comparison: - Aluminum 6061: $E \approx 69$ GPa - Titanium Ti-6Al-4V: $E \approx 110$ GPa - Carbon fiber (PBO): $E \approx 200$ GPa - Steel 316L: $E \approx 200$ GPa 4. **Material Fatigue and Creep:** Surgical robots undergo thousands of sterilization cycles and millions of motion cycles. The material must retain stiffness and strength over time. 5. **Joint Stiffness vs. Link Stiffness:** A robot is **only as stiff as its weakest joint**. Harmonic drives have high reduction ratios but their torsional stiffness is finite; this is included in the overall robot stiffness matrix $K_{robot}$. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall (Part a-i):** Many students apply Grübler's formula mechanically to the parallelogram and get $M = 2$, then fail to explain that the **equal-side constraint** reduces the effective DOF to 1. This is a **3-mark conceptual loss** in KTU valuation. Always state the assumptions of Grübler's formula and check if redundant constraints are present. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Robot Design Concepts** - ✅ A **medical robot** is designed for *task, patient, and sterility*, not just for accuracy. - ✅ A **6-DOF** manipulator is the minimum to position **and orient** an end-effector in 3D space. - ✅ **Grübler's formula:** $M = d(n - g - 1) + \sum f_i$ — count links, joints, and DOF per joint carefully. - ✅ The **last 3 joints** of a surgical arm should form a **spherical wrist** (decoupled position/orientation kinematics). - ✅ The **RCM (Remote Center of Motion)** uses a parallelogram linkage to pivot the tool about a trocar — only 1 DOF is required at the incision. - ✅ **Tip deflection** $\delta_{tip} = F L^{3} / (3 E I)$ — scales as $L^{3}$ and $1/d^{4}$. - ✅ **Workspace** can be *reachable* (the robot can position the wrist) or *dexterous* (the robot can also orient the tool arbitrarily). - ✅ **Singularity** occurs when $\det(J) = 0$ — robot loses 1 or more DOF; must be avoided in the surgical workspace. - ✅ **Force transmission** $\tau = J^{T} F$ links joint torques to end-effector forces; used in haptic feedback design. - ✅ **Stewart-Gough platforms** give 6 DOF with high stiffness — used in CyberKnife, TruPoint, and spinal surgery robots. - ✅ **Sterilization** dictates material choice: 316L stainless steel, Ti-6Al-4V, PEEK — never aluminum or polymer gears. - ✅ **Series-Elastic Actuators (SEAs)** are used in rehabilitation robots (Lokomat, Armeo) for safe patient interaction. - ✅ **Cable-driven transmissions** reduce moving mass but introduce compliance; **harmonic drives** offer high ratios in compact form. - ✅ **Redundancy** (7 DOF) is added in some designs (e.g., MAKO RIO for knee surgery) to avoid joint limits and singularities in vivo. - ✅ **Safety standards** for medical robots: **ISO 13482**, **IEC 80601-2-77**, and **ISO 14971** (risk management).

topics.title: Robot-Assisted Image-Guided Interventions: CT and Ultrasound
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT724 - MEDICAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Robot-Assisted Image-Guided Interventions: CT and Ultrasound

# Module 3 — Robot-Assisted Image-Guided Interventions: CT and Ultrasound

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Image-Guided Intervention (IGI):** A clinical procedure in which pre-operative and/or intra-operative medical images (CT, MRI, Ultrasound, Fluoroscopy, PET) are used in conjunction with a spatial tracking / localization system to plan, navigate, and execute a diagnostic or therapeutic intervention with sub-millimetric precision.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robot-Assisted Image-Guided Intervention (R-IGI):** A sub-class of IGI in which a **medical robot** (serial, parallel, or continuum) is registered to the imaging coordinate frame so that a surgical tool, needle, or probe steered by the robot coincides with a target visible in the image volume — replacing freehand manipulation with **automated, image-locked motion**.

**The two imaging modalities emphasized in this module:**

| Modality | Full Form | Imaging Principle | Robotic Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CT** | Computed Tomography | X-ray attenuation reconstruction (Hounsfield Units) | Percutaneous needle biopsy, radio-frequency ablation, spinal pedicle screw placement |
| **US** | Ultrasound | High-frequency acoustic pulse-echo (Piezoelectric) | Real-time needle guidance, brachytherapy seed placement, focused-ultrasound ablation |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "GPS for the surgeon":** Imagine you are driving a car to a destination hidden behind a wall. You cannot see the destination, but a satellite (CT/US scanner) takes a "picture" of the wall and labels the hidden spot. A robot is your car, equipped with a steering system that talks to the satellite. You feed the destination coordinates from the picture into the robot, and it drives the needle exactly to the hidden point — through the wall — without the surgeon ever needing to see the target directly.

**Why CT vs. Ultrasound?**

- **CT = "Pre-planned, high-accuracy, ionizing":** Provides a 3D, geometrically accurate map of bone and soft tissue. The "map" is taken *before* the procedure; the robot follows it. Excellent for **rigid anatomies** (spine, skull, pelvis).
- **Ultrasound = "Real-time, non-ionizing, soft-tissue":** Provides a continuous, live stream of images. The robot (or its US probe) tracks moving anatomy in real time. Ideal for **soft, deformable organs** (liver, kidney, prostate, thyroid).

> [!TIP]
> **Clinical Pearl:** CT-guided systems are dominated by *bone-anchored* registration (rigid), whereas US-guided systems lean on *real-time visual servoing* and *deformable models*.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants (KTU High-Yield)

- **Sub-millimetric accuracy target:** **0.5 mm – 2.0 mm** for most R-IGI procedures.
- **CT slice thickness:** typically **0.5 mm – 1.0 mm** (high-resolution protocols).
- **US probe frequency:** **2 MHz – 15 MHz** (clinical diagnostic range).
- **Speed of sound in soft tissue (c):** **1540 m/s** (used for time-of-flight depth calculation in US).

$$ d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2} $$

where $d$ is the depth of the reflector, $c$ is the speed of sound, and $t$ is the round-trip time-of-flight.

- **Hounsfield Unit (HU) scale:** Water = **0 HU**, Air = **−1000 HU**, Dense bone ≈ **+1000 HU**.

---

### 1.4 The Three Coordinate Frames in Every R-IGI

> [!NOTE]
> **THE FUNDAMENTAL REGISTRATION PROBLEM:** Every R-IGI system must solve the equation:
>
> $$\mathbf{T}_{\text{robot}}^{\text{image}} \cdot \mathbf{p}_{\text{robot}} = \mathbf{p}_{\text{image}}$$
>
> The robot knows points in *its* base frame; the image knows points in *its* pixel frame. We must find the single rigid transform $\mathbf{T}$ that maps one to the other.

The three frames:
1. **Image frame** $\{I\}$ — voxel coordinates $(u, v, w)$ from CT or US.
2. **Robot base frame** $\{R\}$ — Cartesian $(x, y, z)$ of the robot.
3. **Tool / Tracker frame** $\{T\}$ — attached to the end-effector or probe.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Layers of a Robot-Assisted IGI System

A complete R-IGI pipeline has **five operational layers**, each with its own accuracy budget:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Layer 1 — Image Acquisition:** Acquire CT volume or live US stream.
> **Layer 2 — Segmentation & Planning:** Segment target (lesion) and critical structures; define entry point and trajectory.
> **Layer 3 — Registration:** Compute the rigid transform between image frame $\{I\}$ and robot frame $\{R\}$.
> **Layer 4 — Tracking:** Real-time pose of tool $\Rightarrow$ update display.
> **Layer 5 — Robot Control:** Closed-loop motion of end-effector to align with planned trajectory.

---

### 2.2 Registration: The Heart of Image-Guided Robotics

**Registration** = finding the geometric transformation that aligns two coordinate systems (image-to-patient, or image-to-robot).

Two principal registration families used with CT and US:

| Type | Method | Used With | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Point-based (Paired-point)** | Fiducials, bone-anchored markers | CT | Simple, fast, high accuracy in rigid anatomy | Invasive, requires identifiable landmarks |
| **Surface-based (Iterative Closest Point)** | Surface matching of segmented anatomy | CT, US | No fiducials needed | Slower, needs pre-segmentation |
| **Image-based (Intensity)** | Mutual information maximization | CT–MRI fusion | No markers | Computationally heavy |
| **Vision-based (Visual Servoing)** | Real-time feature tracking in US | US | Real-time, no ionising | Sensitive to image quality |

---

### 2.3 The Hand-Eye Calibration Problem

The **Hand-Eye equation** describes the relationship between the camera (or US probe), the robot end-effector, and the external world:

$$ \mathbf{A}\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{B} $$

where:
- $\mathbf{A}$ = robot end-effector motion: $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{T}_{\text{ee},2}^{-1} \mathbf{T}_{\text{ee},1}$
- $\mathbf{B}$ = camera motion observed in world: $\mathbf{B} = \mathbf{T}_{\text{cam},2} \mathbf{T}_{\text{cam},1}^{-1}$
- $\mathbf{X}$ = unknown hand-eye transform we seek: $\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{T}_{\text{ee}}^{\text{cam}}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for CT/US:** The US probe is a "camera." Its pose relative to the robot end-effector must be known to overlay the live US image onto the robot's coordinate system.

---

### 2.4 CT-Guided Robotic Systems — Deep Analysis

#### 2.4.1 Imaging Chain
1. Patient lies on the CT table (or in a CT-compatible robot like the **MedTech Rosa** or **Brainlab Cirq**).
2. Pre-procedural CT scan (typically **120 kVp, 100–250 mAs**, slice thickness **0.5–1.0 mm**).
3. Fiducial markers (skin-adhesive or bone-anchored) are visible in the scan.
4. The robot's base is rigidly fixed to the CT table or to a stereotactic frame.

#### 2.4.2 Registration Workflow (CT)
1. **Fiducial Localization:** Identify ≥ 3 non-collinear fiducials in CT $\{(u_i, v_i, w_i)\}$.
2. **Probe Localization:** Touch each fiducial with the robot's tool; record robot coordinates $\{(x_i, y_i, z_i)\}$.
3. **Solve rigid transform** (see Section 3 for derivation).
4. **Verify** with a "check fiducial" — residual error must be < **1.0 mm**.

#### 2.4.3 Geometric Accuracy Equation (Fitzpatrick)

The expected **Target Registration Error (TRE)** at a target point $\mathbf{p}$ given $N$ fiducials and fiducial localization error $\text{FLE}$:

$$ \text{TRE}^2(\mathbf{p}) \approx \frac{\text{FLE}^2}{N} \left( 1 + \frac{1}{3} \sum_{k=1}^{3} \frac{d_k^2}{f_k^2} \right) $$

where $d_k$ is the distance from target to principal axis $k$, and $f_k^2$ is the variance of fiducials along axis $k$.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Hook:** KTU often asks: *"Why is placing fiducials *close* to the target critical?"* — Answer: TRE grows with $d_k^2$; the further the target is from the fiducial cluster, the larger the registration error.

---

### 2.5 Ultrasound-Guided Robotic Systems — Deep Analysis

#### 2.5.1 Imaging Chain
- A piezoelectric transducer emits pulses at $f$ = **2–15 MHz**; the echoes return at time $t$.
- The beamformer produces a 2D B-mode image (sector, linear, or convex footprint).
- Frame rate: **30–60 fps** (real-time).

#### 2.5.2 Why US Is Harder for Robots

| Challenge | Why It Matters | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Speckle noise** | Reduces feature-tracking reliability | Image preprocessing, beamforming |
| **Shadowing** | Hides structures behind bone/air | Compound imaging, fusion with CT/MRI |
| **Limited field of view** | Cannot see whole organ in one frame | Probe sweeping / 3D US reconstruction |
| **Probe pressure deformation** | Anatomy shifts under contact | Force-controlled probe holders |
| **Air gap** | Loss of acoustic coupling | Gel standoff pads |

#### 2.5.3 Two Operational Modes

1. **Tele-operated US robot (e.g., MELODY, Advinus):** Robot holds the probe; clinician guides it from a console. Used in **tele-echography** (remote rural diagnosis).
2. **US-visual-servoing (closed-loop):** Robot extracts a feature (e.g., vessel cross-section) from US, computes the Jacobian in the image plane, and moves the probe to keep the feature centered. Formally:

$$ \mathbf{v}_{\text{tool}} = \lambda \, \mathbf{J}^{+}(\mathbf{q}) \, \mathbf{e} $$

where $\mathbf{e}$ is the image-feature error, $\mathbf{J}^{+}$ is the image Jacobian pseudo-inverse, and $\lambda$ is a scalar gain.

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are EXAM-RELEVANT for KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 3.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | US depth | $d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | $d$ = depth (m), $c$ = **1540 m/s**, $t$ = time-of-flight (s) | Round-trip echo |
| 2 | Hounsfield Unit | $HU = 1000 \cdot \frac{\mu - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$ | $\mu$ = linear attenuation coeff. | Water = 0, Air = −1000 |
| 3 | 4×4 Homogeneous transform | $\mathbf{T} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} & \mathbf{p} \\ \mathbf{0}^{T} & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $\mathbf{R} \in SO(3)$, $\mathbf{p} \in \mathbb{R}^3$ | Robot ↔ image mapping |
| 4 | Rigid-body point map | $\mathbf{p}' = \mathbf{R}\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{t}$ | $\mathbf{R}$ rotation, $\mathbf{t}$ translation | 6 DOF |
| 5 | Image-to-robot transform | $\mathbf{T}_{\text{img}}^{\text{robot}} = \arg\min_{\mathbf{R},\mathbf{t}} \sum_i \vert \mathbf{R}\mathbf{p}_i^{\text{img}} + \mathbf{t} - \mathbf{p}_i^{\text{robot}} \vert^2$ | Least-squares | Solved by SVD |
| 6 | Hand-Eye | $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{B}$ | $\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{T}_{\text{ee}}^{\text{cam}}$ | Two-pose solution; multiple-pose uses dual quaternion |
| 7 | FLE–TRE (Fitzpatrick) | $\text{TRE}^2 \approx \frac{\text{FLE}^2}{N}\left(1 + \frac{1}{3}\sum \frac{d_k^2}{f_k^2}\right)$ | $d_k$ target-to-fiducial distance | Increases with target–fiducial distance |
| 8 | FRE (Fiducial Reg. Error) | $\text{FRE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert \mathbf{R}\mathbf{f}_i + \mathbf{t} - \mathbf{g}_i \vert^2}$ | $\mathbf{f}_i$ image fiducials, $\mathbf{g}_i$ robot fiducials | Should be < 1 mm clinically |
| 9 | Image Jacobian | $\dot{\mathbf{s}} = \mathbf{J}_{\text{img}}(\mathbf{q}) \dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | $\mathbf{s}$ = image features, $\mathbf{q}$ = joint angles | Visual servoing core |
| 10 | Acoustic impedance | $Z = \rho c$ | $\rho$ density, $c$ sound speed | Mismatch causes reflection |
| 11 | Axial resolution US | $\delta_a = \frac{c}{2 \Delta f}$ | $\Delta f$ = transducer bandwidth | Smaller = better |
| 12 | Frame-to-frame transform | $\mathbf{T}_{i}^{\,j} = \mathbf{T}_{i}^{-1}\mathbf{T}_{j}$ | Used in FLE estimation | |

---

### 2.7 Why These Equations Matter — Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rigid body transform (Eq. 4):** Lets the surgeon "see" a needle trajectory on the CT image, even though the robot moves in 3D Cartesian space.
- **Fitzpatrick TRE (Eq. 7):** Allows **pre-operative planning** of fiducial placement to *guarantee* sub-mm accuracy at the target — a regulatory requirement.
- **Hand-Eye (Eq. 6):** The foundation of every surgical robot that overlays live imaging (US, endoscope) on pre-op data.
- **Image Jacobian (Eq. 9):** The control law that makes a US probe *autonomously* follow a moving vessel or tumor.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rigid Body Transformation (Closed Form)

**Problem statement:** Given two corresponding point sets $\{\mathbf{p}_i\}_{i=1}^{N}$ in image space and $\{\mathbf{q}_i\}_{i=1}^{N}$ in robot space, find $\mathbf{R}$ and $\mathbf{t}$ such that $\mathbf{q}_i = \mathbf{R}\mathbf{p}_i + \mathbf{t}$.

**Step 1 — Define the cost function:**

$$
J(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t}) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left\| \mathbf{q}_i - (\mathbf{R}\mathbf{p}_i + \mathbf{t}) \right\|^2
$$

We seek $\arg\min_{\mathbf{R},\mathbf{t}} J(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t})$ subject to $\mathbf{R}^T\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I}$, $\det(\mathbf{R}) = +1$.

**Step 2 — Solve for $\mathbf{t}$ first** by taking $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{t}} = 0$:

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{t}} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( \mathbf{q}_i - \mathbf{R}\mathbf{p}_i - \mathbf{t} \right) = \mathbf{0}
$$

Solving:

$$
\mathbf{t} = \bar{\mathbf{q}} - \mathbf{R}\bar{\mathbf{p}}
$$

where $\bar{\mathbf{p}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbf{p}_i$ and $\bar{\mathbf{q}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbf{q}_i$ are the centroids.

**Step 3 — Substitute $\mathbf{t}$ back into $J$ and use the cross-covariance matrix:**

$$
\mathbf{H} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\mathbf{p}_i - \bar{\mathbf{p}})(\mathbf{q}_i - \bar{\mathbf{q}})^T
$$

The optimization reduces to:

$$
\mathbf{R}^{*} = \arg\max_{\mathbf{R}} \; \text{trace}(\mathbf{R}\mathbf{H}) \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbf{R}^T\mathbf{R}=\mathbf{I}
$$

**Step 4 — Singular Value Decomposition (Arun's method):**

Compute SVD of $\mathbf{H}$:

$$
\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{U} \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^T
$$

Then the optimal rotation is:

$$
\mathbf{R}^{*} = \mathbf{V} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \det(\mathbf{V}\mathbf{U}^T) \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{U}^T
$$

The diagonal correction matrix guarantees $\det(\mathbf{R}^{*}) = +1$ (proper rotation, no reflection).

**Step 5 — Translation (final):**

$$
\mathbf{t}^{*} = \bar{\mathbf{q}} - \mathbf{R}^{*}\bar{\mathbf{p}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **This 5-step procedure is the de-facto standard for CT-to-robot registration using skin or bone fiducials — exam favorite.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Fitzpatrick's TRE Formula (Simplified)

**Starting point:** After rigid registration, the residual error at a target point is statistically related to FLE.

**Step 1 — Model FLE as isotropic Gaussian noise** with variance $\sigma^2$ per axis:

$$
\text{FLE} = \sigma
$$

**Step 2 — Define the fiducial centroid** $\bar{\mathbf{f}}$ and the target $\mathbf{r}$:

**Step 3 — Express TRE as the expected squared distance** between the true target and the registered target:

$$
\text{TRE}^2(\mathbf{r}) = E\!\left[ \left\| \mathbf{R}(\mathbf{r} - \bar{\mathbf{f}}) - (\mathbf{r}' - \bar{\mathbf{f}}') \right\|^2 \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Diagonalize the fiducial covariance** by rotating into the principal axes of the fiducial distribution:

Let $f_k^2$ be the variance of fiducials along principal axis $k$.

**Step 5 — Derive the scalar form:**

$$
\text{TRE}^2(\mathbf{r}) \approx \frac{\text{FLE}^2}{N} \left[ 1 + \frac{1}{3} \sum_{k=1}^{3} \frac{d_k^2(\mathbf{r})}{f_k^2} \right]
$$

where $d_k(\mathbf{r})$ is the distance from the target to the $k$-th principal axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** If the target is at the centroid of the fiducials, $d_k = 0$ and TRE reduces to its minimum $\frac{\text{FLE}}{\sqrt{N}}$. The closer the target is to the fiducial cluster centroid, the better the accuracy.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — US Probe-to-Robot Calibration (Pivot Style)

**Setup:** The US probe is mounted on the robot's end-effector. A Z-fiducial (a cross-wire or point phantom) lies in a water bath.

**Step 1 — Identify the fiducial point $\mathbf{p}_{\text{US}}$ in the US image** (in pixel coords).

**Step 2 — Define two coordinate frames:**
- $\{P\}$ = US probe frame (origin at the imaging plane corner).
- $\{R\}$ = Robot base frame.

**Step 3 — At calibration, the probe is positioned so the fiducial is centered in the US image at $(u_0, v_0, 0)$**. The robot reports the probe tip pose $\mathbf{T}_{R}^{P}$.

**Step 4 — Compute the rigid transform** $\mathbf{T}_{R}^{\text{US-image}} = \mathbf{T}_{R}^{P} \cdot \mathbf{T}_{P}^{\text{image}}$ by the same Arun SVD as in §3.1, using the robot's positions and the image's observed pixel coordinates of the phantom cross.

**Step 5 — Validation:** Re-acquire a phantom and verify that the **reprojection error** (pixel discrepancy) is below **1 pixel**.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (CT Registration)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A CT image has 4 fiducials at image-space coordinates $\mathbf{p}_i$ (in mm, scaled from voxels). A robot probe touches the same fiducials, giving $\mathbf{q}_i$ in robot base coordinates. Find the registration.

**Given (synthetic):**

$$
\mathbf{p} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 10 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 10 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 10 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{q} = \begin{bmatrix} 100 & 50 & 20 \\ 110 & 52 & 22 \\ 99 & 60 & 21 \\ 101 & 49 & 30 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 1 — Centroids:**

$$
\bar{\mathbf{p}} = \frac{1}{4}\begin{bmatrix} 10 \\ 10 \\ 10 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.5 \\ 2.5 \\ 2.5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\bar{\mathbf{q}} = \begin{bmatrix} 102.5 \\ 52.75 \\ 23.25 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Centered coordinates:**

$$
\tilde{\mathbf{p}} = \begin{bmatrix} -2.5 & -2.5 & -2.5 \\ 7.5 & -2.5 & -2.5 \\ -2.5 & 7.5 & -2.5 \\ -2.5 & -2.5 & 7.5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\tilde{\mathbf{q}} = \begin{bmatrix} -2.5 & -2.75 & -3.25 \\ 7.5 & -0.75 & -1.25 \\ -3.5 & 7.25 & -2.25 \\ -1.5 & -3.75 & 6.75 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Cross-covariance:**

$$
\mathbf{H} = \tilde{\mathbf{p}}^T \tilde{\mathbf{q}}
$$

Carrying out the 3×3 matrix multiplication:

$$
\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} 81.25 & 25 & 0 \\ 0 & 81.25 & 25 \\ 25 & 0 & 81.25 \end{bmatrix}
$$

(approximate, for illustrative purposes)

**Step 4 — SVD of $\mathbf{H}$** (computed numerically): assume $\mathbf{H} \approx \mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^T$ with $\mathbf{\Sigma} = \text{diag}(94.6, 94.6, 54.5)$ and $\mathbf{U} = \mathbf{V} \approx \mathbf{I}$ (near-orthogonal case).

**Step 5 — Compute $\mathbf{R}^*$:**

Since $\det(\mathbf{V}\mathbf{U}^T) \approx +1$, the correction matrix is the identity.

$$
\mathbf{R}^{*} = \mathbf{V}\mathbf{U}^T \approx \mathbf{I}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute $\mathbf{t}^*$:**

$$
\mathbf{t}^{*} = \bar{\mathbf{q}} - \mathbf{R}^{*}\bar{\mathbf{p}} = \begin{bmatrix} 102.5 \\ 52.75 \\ 23.25 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 2.5 \\ 2.5 \\ 2.5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 100 \\ 50.25 \\ 20.75 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 7 — Verification (FRE):**

$$
\text{FRE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{4}\sum_{i=1}^{4} \left\| \mathbf{R}^{*}\mathbf{p}_i + \mathbf{t}^{*} - \mathbf{q}_i \right\|^2} \approx 0.7 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam takeaway:** FRE = 0.7 mm is clinically acceptable for CT-guided interventions (target < 1 mm). Then estimate TRE from Fitzpatrick's formula for any pre-planned target.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — CT vs. US Comparison

| Parameter | CT-Guided R-IGI | US-Guided R-IGI |
|---|---|---|
| Imaging modality | Ionising X-ray | Non-ionising acoustic |
| Spatial resolution | **0.5–1.0 mm** (3D) | **0.5–2.0 mm** (2D) |
| Temporal resolution | Static (pre-op) | Real-time (30–60 fps) |
| Best for | Rigid anatomy (spine, skull) | Soft, mobile tissue (liver, kidney) |
| Registration | Point-based, surface, bone-anchored | Visual servoing, probe-tracked |
| Typical robot | Rosa, Mazor, ExcelsiusGPS | MELODY, KUKA-based research |
| Radiation to staff | Yes (CT, fluoroscopy) | None |
| Deformation handling | Implicit (assumed rigid) | Explicit (deformable models) |
| Cost | High (CT room) | Moderate (US cart) |
| Image-to-patient coupling | Fiducials / frame | Probe contact force |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an R-IGI System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[CT or US Scanner] --> B[Image Volume / Live Stream]
    B --> C[Segmentation and Planning Module]
    C --> D[Target and Trajectory Definition]
    D --> E[Registration Module]
    E --> F[Fiducial or Feature Detection]
    F --> G[Compute Rigid Transform T_img_robot]
    G --> H[Robot Controller]
    H --> I[Surgical Robot End Effector]
    I --> J[Needle or Probe]
    J --> K[Patient Anatomy]
    B --> L[Image Display Overlay]
    G --> L
    L --> M[Surgeon Console]
    M --> H
    K --> N[Intra op US Probe Optional]
    N --> E
    I --> O[Position Encoder Feedback]
    O --> H
```

**Interpretation:** The block diagram shows a closed-loop architecture — images flow from the scanner into a planning module, the registration module aligns them with the robot's coordinate frame using fiducials or visual features, the robot controller drives the end-effector to the planned trajectory, and real-time encoder feedback (plus optional intra-op US) closes the loop. The surgeon supervises via a console with overlaid image guidance.

---

### 4.2 Registration Workflow (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step1: Acquire Pre op CT] --> S2[Step2: Place Fiducials on Patient]
    S2 --> S3[Step3: Acquire Intra op CT]
    S3 --> S4[Step4: Localize Fiducials in Image Space]
    S4 --> S5[Step5: Touch Fiducials with Robot Tool]
    S5 --> S6[Step6: Collect Robot Coordinates]
    S6 --> S7[Step7: Compute Rigid Transform via SVD]
    S7 --> S8[Step8: Compute FRE]
    S8 --> S9{FRE less than 1 mm?}
    S9 -->|Yes| S10[Step9: Register and Proceed]
    S9 -->|No| S2
    S10 --> S11[Step10: Plan Trajectory]
    S11 --> S12[Step11: Execute Robot Motion]
    S12 --> S13[Step12: Verify with Check Fiducial]
```

---

### 4.3 US Visual Servoing Loop (Closed-Loop Control)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[US Probe on Robot EE] --> ACQ[Acquire US Frame]
    ACQ --> FE[Extract Image Feature e]
    FE --> COMP[Compare with Desired Feature e_star]
    COMP --> ERR[Compute Error delta e]
    ERR --> JAC[Compute Image Jacobian J]
    JAC --> INV[Compute Joint Velocity q_dot via Pseudoinverse J_plus]
    INV --> CTRL[Robot Low Level Controller]
    CTRL --> P
    COMP --> DISP[Display Error to Surgeon]
```

**Interpretation:** This is a classical **image-based visual servoing (IBVS)** loop. The probe is mounted on the robot; the US image is the only feedback. A feature (e.g., centroid of a target lesion) is extracted, compared to the desired feature, and the robot's joint velocities are computed via the pseudoinverse of the image Jacobian — moving the probe to keep the feature centered.

---

### 4.4 CT vs. US Decision Tree (Clinical Selection)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Target Lesion Identified] --> R{Anatomy Rigid?}
    R -->|Yes, spine, skull, pelvis| S{Visibility on CT?}
    R -->|No, soft, mobile organ| T[Choose US Guided R IGI]
    S -->|Yes, bone / contrast| U[Choose CT Guided R IGI]
    S -->|Poor soft tissue contrast| V[Consider CT MRI Fusion]
    U --> X[Apply Point based Registration]
    T --> Y[Apply Visual Servoing]
    V --> Z[Apply Mutual Information Registration]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Image-Guided Intervention (IGI). List any two clinical applications of robot-assisted CT-guided interventions.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Image-Guided Intervention (IGI):** A clinical procedure that uses pre-operative and/or intra-operative medical images (CT, MRI, US, fluoroscopy) together with a spatial tracking or localization system to plan, navigate, and execute a diagnostic or therapeutic intervention with high geometric accuracy (sub-millimetre).

**Two clinical applications of robot-assisted CT-guided interventions (1 mark each):**

1. **Percutaneous needle biopsy of pulmonary or hepatic lesions** — the robot aligns a needle along a pre-planned trajectory computed from the CT volume, avoiding ribs, vessels, and critical organs.
2. **Spinal pedicle screw placement** — robot (e.g., Mazor, ExcelsiusGPS) drills screw channels in vertebrae using CT-derived trajectories, reducing breach rates compared to freehand surgery.

*Other acceptable answers: RF/microwave ablation of liver tumours, stereotactic neurosurgery, O-arm–guided trauma fixation.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is meant by Fiducial Registration Error (FRE)? Write its mathematical expression.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **FRE (Fiducial Registration Error):** The root-mean-square (RMS) residual distance between corresponding fiducial points in the two coordinate frames *after* a rigid-body registration has been applied. It is the most common measure of registration *fidelity* (not target accuracy).

**Mathematical expression (2 marks):**

$$
\text{FRE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left\| \mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{f}_i + \mathbf{t} - \mathbf{g}_i \right\|^2}
$$

where $\mathbf{f}_i$ are the fiducial coordinates in the image frame, $\mathbf{g}_i$ are the corresponding coordinates in the robot (or patient) frame, $\mathbf{R}$ and $\mathbf{t}$ are the rotation and translation from the registration, and $N$ is the number of fiducials.

*Acceptable clinical threshold: FRE < 1.0 mm for CT-guided interventions.*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a robot-assisted CT-guided intervention system**. Describe the role of each block and the chain of coordinate transformations from image to robot. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **rigid-body point registration** that maps $N$ image-space fiducials $\mathbf{p}_i$ to robot-space coordinates $\mathbf{q}_i$. Use the SVD (Arun) method. Show the closed-form solution. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Functional architecture (7 marks):**

A robot-assisted CT-guided intervention system consists of the following blocks (refer Section 4.1 diagram):

| Block | Function | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| 1. CT Scanner | Acquires 3D image volume of patient anatomy with fiducials visible | 1 |
| 2. Image Processing & Segmentation | Isolates target lesion, vessels, bones, entry/exit points | 1 |
| 3. Trajectory Planner | Computes optimal linear/curved path from skin entry to target | 1 |
| 4. Registration Module | Fiducial localization + Arun SVD to compute $\mathbf{T}_{\text{img}}^{\text{robot}}$ | 1.5 |
| 5. Robot Controller | Inverse kinematics, safety constraints, motion execution | 1 |
| 6. End-effector / Needle Driver | Holds and advances the surgical tool | 0.5 |
| 7. Tracking & Display Overlay | Real-time display of tool position on CT slices for surgeon | 1 |

**Coordinate transformation chain:**

$$
\mathbf{p}_{\text{robot}} = \mathbf{T}_{\text{robot}}^{\text{base}} \cdot \mathbf{T}_{\text{tip}}^{\text{tool}} \cdot \mathbf{p}_{\text{tip}}
$$

$$
\mathbf{p}_{\text{image}} = \mathbf{T}_{\text{img}}^{\text{robot}} \cdot \mathbf{p}_{\text{robot}}
$$

Registration computes $\mathbf{T}_{\text{img}}^{\text{robot}}$ via Arun's method (next part).

---

**(b) Derivation of rigid-body point registration (7 marks):**

Full derivation as in **§3.1** of these notes.

1. **Cost function** $J(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t}) = \frac{1}{2} \sum \left\| \mathbf{q}_i - \mathbf{R}\mathbf{p}_i - \mathbf{t} \right\|^2$ — 1 mark
2. **Optimal translation** $\mathbf{t} = \bar{\mathbf{q}} - \mathbf{R}\bar{\mathbf{p}}$ — 1 mark
3. **Cross-covariance** $\mathbf{H} = \sum (\mathbf{p}_i - \bar{\mathbf{p}})(\mathbf{q}_i - \bar{\mathbf{q}})^T$ — 1 mark
4. **Reduced problem** $\mathbf{R}^{*} = \arg\max_{\mathbf{R}} \text{trace}(\mathbf{R}\mathbf{H})$ s.t. $\mathbf{R}^T\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I}$ — 1 mark
5. **SVD** $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^T$, $\mathbf{R}^{*} = \mathbf{V}\,\text{diag}(1,1,\det(\mathbf{V}\mathbf{U}^T))\,\mathbf{U}^T$ — 2 marks
6. **Final translation** $\mathbf{t}^{*} = \bar{\mathbf{q}} - \mathbf{R}^{*}\bar{\mathbf{p}}$ — 1 mark

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Compare **CT-guided** and **Ultrasound-guided** robot-assisted interventions under the heads: imaging principle, registration method, real-time capability, radiation, suitable anatomy, and clinical examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With suitable diagrams, describe the **image-based visual servoing (IBVS)** control loop used in US-guided robotic systems. Derive the **image Jacobian** relationship and explain how the joint velocity is computed. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison table (7 marks — 1 mark per row + 1 mark for concluding clinical example):**

| Parameter | CT-Guided R-IGI | US-Guided R-IGI |
|---|---|---|
| Imaging principle | X-ray attenuation, Hounsfield reconstruction | Acoustic pulse-echo at 2–15 MHz |
| Registration | Point-based / surface / bone-anchored (rigid) | Visual servoing, probe-tracked (real-time) |
| Real-time | No — pre-op static volume | Yes — 30–60 fps B-mode stream |
| Radiation | Yes (ionising) | None (non-ionising) |
| Anatomy | Rigid (spine, skull, pelvis) | Soft, mobile (liver, kidney, prostate) |
| Example | Mazor spine robot, Rosa neurosurgery | MELODY tele-echography, BRACOS prostate biopsy |

---

**(b) IBVS loop (7 marks):**

**Diagram:** Refer §4.3 of these notes (US visual servoing loop block diagram) — 2 marks.

**Derivation of image Jacobian (5 marks):**

Let a 3D point $\mathbf{X} = (X, Y, Z)$ in the camera/probe frame project to image coordinates:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u &= f \frac{X}{Z} \\
v &= f \frac{Y}{Z}
\end{aligned}
$$

Differentiating with respect to time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{u} &= f \frac{\dot{X} Z - X \dot{Z}}{Z^2} = \frac{f}{Z} \dot{X} - \frac{u}{Z} \dot{Z} \\
\dot{v} &= f \frac{\dot{Y} Z - Y \dot{Z}}{Z^2} = \frac{f}{Z} \dot{Y} - \frac{v}{Z} \dot{Z}
\end{aligned}
$$

Stacking $\dot{\mathbf{s}} = [\dot{u}, \dot{v}]^T$ and $\mathbf{v}_c = [\dot{X}, \dot{Y}, \dot{Z}]^T$:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{s}} = \mathbf{J}_{\text{img}}(\mathbf{X}, Z) \, \mathbf{v}_c
$$

$$
\mathbf{J}_{\text{img}} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{f}{Z} & 0 & -\frac{u}{Z} \\ 0 & \frac{f}{Z} & -\frac{v}{Z} \end{bmatrix}
$$

The end-effector twist $\mathbf{v}_c$ is mapped to joint velocities via the robot Jacobian $\mathbf{J}_q$:

$$
\mathbf{v}_c = \mathbf{J}_q(\mathbf{q}) \, \dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

Combining:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{s}} = \underbrace{\mathbf{J}_{\text{img}}(\mathbf{X}, Z) \, \mathbf{J}_q(\mathbf{q})}_{\mathbf{J}_{\text{total}}(\mathbf{q})} \dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

The control law to drive the feature error $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{s} - \mathbf{s}^{*}$ to zero is:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} = -\lambda \, \mathbf{J}_{\text{total}}^{+}(\mathbf{q}) \, \mathbf{e}
$$

where $\lambda > 0$ is the control gain and $\mathbf{J}_{\text{total}}^{+}$ is the Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Students often confuse the **image Jacobian** (camera/probe frame) with the **robot Jacobian** (joint frame). Clearly label which frame you are differentiating in.
> - The pseudoinverse $\mathbf{J}^{+}$ is used because the system is **under-actuated** (2 image features cannot control 6 Cartesian DOFs). You need ≥ 3 features for full 6-DOF control.
> - **Don't forget** the depth $Z$ in the image Jacobian — it is the *current* depth of the target, not the desired depth. Re-estimate it from the US image at every frame.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — CT and US Image-Guided Interventions**

- ☐ **IGI** = pre-op + intra-op imaging + tracking + execution with sub-mm accuracy.
- ☐ **R-IGI** adds a robot end-effector that is registered to the image frame.
- ☐ **Three coordinate frames** in every R-IGI: image $\{I\}$, robot $\{R\}$, tool $\{T\}$.
- ☐ **Core equation** of registration: $\mathbf{T}_{\text{img}}^{\text{robot}} \cdot \mathbf{p}_{\text{img}} = \mathbf{p}_{\text{robot}}$.
- ☐ **Two registration families:** point-based (rigid, fiducials) and image-based (deformable, mutual information / visual servoing).
- ☐ **Arun SVD method** (5 steps) is the standard solution for rigid-body point registration — must be remembered.
- ☐ **FRE** = RMS residual after registration; **TRE** = expected error at the actual target.
- ☐ **Fitzpatrick's formula** — TRE grows as target moves away from fiducial centroid; place fiducials *close to the target*.
- ☐ **Hand-Eye equation** $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{B}$ — solved by dual-quaternion or SVD.
- ☐ **US speed of sound** in soft tissue: $c = 1540$ m/s; depth: $d = ct/2$.
- ☐ **HU scale**: water = 0, air = −1000, bone ≈ +1000.
- ☐ **Image Jacobian** for IBVS: $\dot{\mathbf{s}} = \mathbf{J}_{\text{img}}(\mathbf{X}, Z) \mathbf{J}_q(\mathbf{q}) \dot{\mathbf{q}}$.
- ☐ **CT is best for rigid anatomy** (spine, skull); **US is best for soft mobile tissue** (liver, kidney).
- ☐ **CT = ionising + 3D static**; **US = non-ionising + real-time 2D**.
- ☐ **Real-time US robotics** uses IBVS or tele-operated probe holders (MELODY).
- ☐ **Clinical accuracy benchmark:** < 1 mm TRE for spinal, < 2 mm for abdominal interventions.
- ☐ **Fiducial placement strategy:** minimum 3 non-collinear points, surrounding the target, as close as possible to the centroid of the target volume.
- ☐ **FLE ≈ σ** (Gaussian isotropic); **TRE depends on FLE, N, and target-to-fiducial distance**.
- ☐ **Validation** is mandatory: a *check fiducial* not used in registration must show < 1 mm residual.
- ☐ **Safety layer:** Robot must have a force/torque limit and emergency stop, since it operates near critical anatomy.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 3 notes — Robot-Assisted Image-Guided Interventions: CT and Ultrasound.**
> These notes cover the full KTU 2024 Scheme learning outcomes for OEBRT724 Module 3, including definitions, derivations, registration theory, visual servoing, and exam-style practice questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Other Applications: Robotic Catheters for Heart Electrophysiology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT724 - MEDICAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Other Applications: Robotic Catheters for Heart Electrophysiology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Robotic Catheter Systems for Heart Electrophysiology (EP)** are master–slave teleoperated or image-guided mechatronic devices designed to navigate, position, and manipulate flexible catheter instruments within the cardiac chambers to diagnose and treat cardiac arrhythmias (irregular heartbeats). These systems provide enhanced **dexterity, stability, precision, and force feedback** that surpass the manual capabilities of a cardiologist manipulating a catheter by hand at the femoral or subclavian access site.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A *Robotic Catheter System* is a continuum/articulated manipulator inserted percutaneously into the vascular system and steered under physician command to perform endocardial mapping (recording electrical activity) and catheter ablation (delivering RF/cryothermal energy to create conduction-blocking lesions) for treating arrhythmias such as Atrial Fibrillation (AF), Atrial Flutter, AVNRT, and Ventricular Tachycardia.

### 1.1.1 Clinical Workflow Context

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Electrophysiology (EP) Study | Diagnostic mapping of the heart's electrical conduction system |
| Catheter Ablation | Therapeutic destruction of arrhythmia-causing tissue using RF, cryo, or laser energy |
| Atrial Fibrillation (AF) | Most common sustained arrhythmia; characterized by chaotic atrial electrical activity |
| Pulmonary Vein Isolation (PVI) | Gold-standard ablation target for paroxysmal AF |
| Lesion | A controlled scar created by ablation to block abnormal electrical pathways |
| Steerable Sheath | An outer robotic guide that articulates the catheter tip |
| CARTO / EnSite | 3D electroanatomical mapping systems providing GPS-like navigation of the heart |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** A typical manual ablation procedure exposes the cardiologist to **~30 minutes of fluoroscopy (X-ray)** per case and suffers from **2–5 mm catheter tip drift** due to cardiac and respiratory motion. Robotic systems reduce fluoroscopy to <3 minutes for the physician and provide **sub-millimeter positional repeatability**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The GPS-Guided Wire Through the Heart"

Imagine you need to thread a long, flexible wire from your leg all the way up into a moving, beating heart — and once inside, you must touch 20–30 specific tiny spots on the heart wall in a precise pattern to cauterize them. Doing this by hand is like trying to paint a delicate miniature while standing in a vibrating boat.

A **robotic catheter system** is essentially a **precision joystick + game-engine GPS** for this exact problem:

- The **master console** is the steering wheel — the cardiologist sits comfortably, away from radiation, viewing a 3D map of the heart.
- The **slave catheter** is the smart wire — it has tiny pull-wires inside that bend the tip, just like the tendons in your finger bend your fingertip.
- The **3D mapping system** (CARTO/EnSite) is the GPS — it color-codes the heart's electrical activity, showing exactly which spots are "broken" and need treatment.
- The **force sensor** is the fingertip sense — it tells the doctor if they are pushing too hard (risk of perforation) or too softly (incomplete lesion).

> [!TIP]
> **Memorable Mnemonic for KTU:** **"S-M-M-F-S"** — The five pillars of a robotic EP catheter system are **Steerability, Mapping, Master–Slave, Force-feedback, and Safety** (collision detection, sterile barrier, speed limiting).

## 1.3 Historical and Commercial Milestones

| Year | Milestone |
|---|---|
| 1999 | Catheter Robotics **Amigo** — first FDA-cleared remote catheter system |
| 2003 | Stereotaxis **Niobe** — magnetic navigation introduced |
| 2007 | Hansen Medical **Sensei X** with **Artisan** catheter — first widely adopted robotic EP platform |
| 2011 | Force-sensing catheters (TactiCath, SmartTouch) introduced |
| 2019 | Stereotaxis Genesis — fully robotic magnetic EP system with 0.1 T magnets |
| 2022 | Multi-catheter magnetic workflows with **Centripetal** and **Emerald** catheters |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two dominant technical philosophies are:
> 1. **Mechanical (Pull-Wire) Steering** — *Hansen Sensei, Amigo* — uses tendon-driven articulation.
> 2. **Magnetic Field Steering** — *Stereotaxis* — uses external magnets to orient a soft, magnet-tipped catheter.

## 1.4 Clinical Indications (Why EP Catheters Are Used)

- **Atrial Fibrillation (AF)** — most common; affects ~2% of the global population
- **Atrial Flutter** — macro-reentrant tachycardia
- **AVNRT (AV Nodal Re-entrant Tachycardia)** — common in young adults
- **Ventricular Tachycardia (VT)** — life-threatening; often post-infarct
- **Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) syndrome** — accessory pathway ablation
- **Typical Procedure Time:** 2–4 hours manual → 1.5–2.5 hours robotic
- **Fluoroscopy Reduction:** ~90% for the operating physician

## 1.5 Key Design Requirements (KTU High-Yield)

| Requirement | Target Value | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Tip Diameter | 7 Fr (~2.3 mm) | Miniaturization of actuators |
| Working Length | 900–1100 mm | Long tendon routing, friction compensation |
| Bending Angle | ≥ 180° in two planes | Tendon routing through multiple joints |
| Tip Force | 10–40 g (ablative range) | Force sensing resolution ~1 g |
| Position Accuracy | < 1 mm in 3D space | Sensor fusion (EM + impedance) |
| Update Rate | 100–200 Hz | Real-time closed-loop control |
| Degrees of Freedom | 4–7 DOF | Continuum kinematics required |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Robotic Catheter System

A robotic EP catheter system consists of four tightly coupled subsystems:

### 2.1.1 Mechanical Subsystem (Slave Side)
- **Steerable Sheath** — outer 12 Fr sheath with 4 pull-wires for 4-DOF bending (pitch, yaw, roll, insertion)
- **Inner Catheter** — 7 Fr mapping/ablation catheter with tip electrode and saline irrigation
