- **Drive Mechanism** — DC motors with capstan/pulley tendon transmission
- **Encoder Stack** — incremental encoders on each motor providing joint position
- **Force Sensor** — optical fiber Bragg grating (FBG) or capacitive strain-gauge at the tip

### 2.1.2 Sensing Subsystem
- **Electromagnetic (EM) Position Sensors** — 5–6 DOF coils at the catheter tip (Aurora, Axiem)
- **Impedance-Based Tracking** — voltage measurements between skin patches and tip electrode
- **Electrogram (EGM) Recording** — bipolar/unipolar signals (30–500 Hz bandwidth)
- **Contact Force Sensor** — FBG or capacitive; range 0–50 g, resolution 0.5 g
- **Temperature Sensor** — thermocouple at the tip for lesion monitoring (target 40–50 °C)

### 2.1.3 Master Console
- **Haptic Input Device** — 3–7 DOF joystick (e.g., Phantom Omni, dVRK sigma.7)
- **Stereoscopic 3D Display** — shows CARTO/EnSite map with catheter pose overlaid
- **Foot Pedal** — for ablation energy activation
- **Fluoro-Snaps** — instant 2D X-ray image capture registered to 3D map

### 2.1.4 Control Software
- **Real-Time Kinematics Engine** — converts master input → slave joint commands
- **Haptic Rendering** — virtual fixtures, force scaling (typically 1:1 to 1:5)
- **Safety Layer** — workspace limits, speed clamps, collision avoidance

## 2.2 Kinematic Theory of a Continuum Catheter

A robotic EP catheter is best modeled as a **piecewise-constant curvature (PCC) continuum manipulator**. Each bending section behaves like a constant-curvature arc, parameterized by:

- **Curvature $\kappa$** (rad/m)
- **Bending plane angle $\phi$** (rad)
- **Arc length $s$** (m)

### 2.2.1 Tip Position Equation (2D Bending Section)

For a single bending section of arc length $L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{tip} &= \frac{\sin(\kappa L)}{\kappa} \cos(\phi) \\
y_{tip} &= \frac{\sin(\kappa L)}{\kappa} \sin(\phi) \\
z_{tip} &= \frac{1 - \cos(\kappa L)}{\kappa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Derivation logic: The center of the arc lies at distance $1/\kappa$ from the base along the normal. The arc is then rotated by $\phi$ to define the bending plane. The tip coordinates follow the standard parametric form of a circular arc of radius $1/\kappa$ subtending an angle $\kappa L$ at the center.

### 2.2.2 Bending Moment–Curvature Relationship

A catheter behaves as a slender elastic rod. The bending moment is related to curvature by:

$$
M = EI \cdot \kappa
$$

where:
- $M$ = bending moment (N·m)
- $E$ = Young's modulus of catheter material (typically **Nitinol** or braided Pebax: $E \approx 30\text{–}100$ GPa)
- $I$ = second moment of area ($I = \pi r^4 / 4$ for solid, smaller for hollow)
- $\kappa$ = curvature (1/m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a **hollow catheter** of outer radius $r_o$ and inner radius $r_i$, the area moment is:
> $$I = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( r_o^4 - r_i^4 \right)$$
> This is critical for KTU numerical problems involving bending stiffness.

### 2.2.3 Tendon (Pull-Wire) Force–Curvature Coupling

The pull-wire force $F_{t}$ required to hold a tendon-driven catheter at curvature $\kappa$ is:

$$
F_{t} = \frac{EI \cdot \kappa}{r_{off}}
$$

where $r_{off}$ is the **offset** of the tendon from the neutral axis of the catheter cross-section (typically 0.3–0.7 mm for a 7 Fr catheter).

> This equation shows that a smaller offset requires a larger tendon force — a key trade-off in catheter design (smaller offset → less friction but more force).

## 2.3 Magnetic Navigation Physics (Stereotaxis)

The Stereotaxis system uses two large permanent magnets placed on either side of the patient. They produce a uniform magnetic field of magnitude:

$$
B \approx 0.08 \text{ to } 0.1 \text{ T}
$$

The catheter tip contains a small permanent magnet (e.g., Neodymium-Iron-Boron, NdFeB) of remnant magnetization $M$ and volume $V$. The **magnetic torque** aligning the tip with the field is:

$$
\vec{\tau}_m = V \cdot \vec{M} \times \vec{B}
$$

The gradient of the field exerts a translational force on a magnetic dipole:

$$
\vec{F}_m = \nabla ( \vec{m} \cdot \vec{B} )
$$

where $\vec{m} = V \cdot \vec{M}/\mu_0$ is the magnetic dipole moment.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Stereotaxis produces torque on the tip but **negligible translational force** because $B$ is uniform inside the bore. Advancement/retraction is performed by a separate motor-driven advancer (Cardiodrive).

## 2.4 Contact Force and Lesion Formation

Ablation efficacy depends critically on **contact force (CF)**. The relationship between contact force, power, and time follows an empirical lesion model:

$$
\text{Lesion Depth} \propto \sqrt{ P \cdot t \cdot CF }
$$

where:
- $P$ = RF power (Watts, typical 20–35 W)
- $t$ = duration (seconds, typical 30–60 s)
- $CF$ = contact force (grams)

> **Clinical Target Window:** $CF = 10\text{–}20$ g yields transmural lesions in thin atrial walls (2–4 mm) without risk of steam pop or perforation.

The **Force Time Integral (FTI)** is a key clinical metric:

$$
FTI = \int_{0}^{T} CF(t) \cdot dt \quad [g \cdot s]
$$

Typical target: $FTI \geq 400$ g·s for effective lesion.

## 2.5 Workspace Analysis

The reachable workspace of a 2-DOF bending catheter from a fixed insertion point is approximated as a **torus sector** with:

- **Outer reach:** $R_{max} = L_{eff} + 1/\kappa_{min}$
- **Inner reach:** $R_{min} = 1/\kappa_{max}$
- **Working volume:** swept area in the bending plane

For a 4-DOF sheath + 2-DOF inner catheter, the workspace approaches a **hemispherical shell** of radius ~15 cm, which comfortably envelops the left atrium (diameter ~4–5 cm) and right ventricle (~3 cm) accessed from the femoral vein via the inferior vena cava.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam-Ready)

| Concept | Formula | Symbols | Units | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Catheter Tip Position (PCC) | $x = \frac{\sin(\kappa L)}{\kappa}\cos\phi$ | $\kappa,L,\phi$ | m, rad | — |
| Bending Moment | $M = EI\kappa$ | $E,I,\kappa$ | N·m, GPa, 1/m | 0.01–0.1 N·m |
| Hollow Tube Inertia | $I = \frac{\pi}{4}(r_o^4 - r_i^4)$ | $r_o,r_i$ | m⁴ | $10^{-12}$ m⁴ |
| Tendon Force | $F_t = \frac{EI\kappa}{r_{off}}$ | $E,I,\kappa,r_{off}$ | N | 0.5–5 N |
| Magnetic Torque | $\tau_m = V M B \sin\theta$ | $V,M,B,\theta$ | N·m | ~$10^{-4}$ N·m |
| Magnetic Force | $F_m = \nabla(\vec{m}\cdot\vec{B})$ | $\vec{m},\vec{B}$ | N | < 0.01 N |
| Lesion Depth (empirical) | $D \propto \sqrt{P \cdot t \cdot CF}$ | $P,t,CF$ | W, s, g | 3–5 mm |
| Force Time Integral | $FTI = \int CF \, dt$ | $CF, t$ | g·s | $\geq 400$ g·s |
| Tip Resolution | $\Delta x = \frac{\partial x}{\partial \kappa}\Delta\kappa$ | partial derivative | m | < 1 mm |
| Catheter Stiffness (EI) | $EI = \frac{F r^2 L}{3\delta}$ for cantilever | $F,r,L,\delta$ | N·m² | $10^{-6}\text{–}10^{-4}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When using the parallel axis theorem or the tendon force equation, students often forget that $r_{off}$ is measured from the **neutral axis** of the cross-section, not from the geometric center of the catheter. Always check this in numericals.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The robotic EP catheter platform has become a **paradigm case study** in medical robotics for several reasons:

1. **It demonstrates continuum manipulation** in a clinically validated setting — used in over **150,000 procedures** worldwide.
2. **It highlights human–robot collaboration** — the robot does NOT replace the cardiologist; it amplifies their skill.
3. **It pioneered haptic feedback integration** in commercial interventional systems.
4. **It validates safe failure modes** — tendon breakage, sensor failure, and software faults have established safety protocols used across the broader surgical robotics industry.
5. **It bridges imaging modalities** — combining fluoroscopy, 3D electroanatomical mapping, and (more recently) intracardiac echocardiography (ICE).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Tip Position of a Continuum Catheter Section

**Statement:** Derive the Cartesian position of the tip of a single bending section of a robotic catheter, modeled as a constant-curvature arc of arc length $L$ and curvature $\kappa$, bending in a plane oriented at angle $\phi$ from the catheter base axis.

### Step 1 — Parameterize the Arc

Consider a catheter aligned along the $z$-axis. A bending section of length $L$ curves with constant curvature $\kappa$ in a plane. The local tangent vector $\hat{t}(s)$ rotates with arc length $s$:

$$
\frac{d\hat{t}}{ds} = \kappa \hat{n}(s)
$$

where $\hat{n}$ is the principal normal to the curve.

> *Interpretation:* This is the Frenet–Serret equation for a planar curve with zero torsion. Constant curvature means the rate of change of tangent is constant.

### Step 2 — Solve the Tangent Equation

In the bending plane (let's call it the $xz$-plane initially, with $\phi = 0$):

$$
\frac{d\theta}{ds} = \kappa \implies \theta(s) = \kappa s + \theta_0
$$

Assuming the catheter enters the bending section straight ($\theta_0 = 0$):

$$
\theta(s) = \kappa s
$$

The tangent components are:

$$
\hat{t}(s) = \left[ \sin(\kappa s), \, 0, \, \cos(\kappa s) \right]
$$

### Step 3 — Integrate Tangent to Get Position

Position along the curve is obtained by integrating the tangent:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(s) &= \int_0^s \sin(\kappa s') \, ds' = \frac{1 - \cos(\kappa s)}{\kappa} \\
z(s) &= \int_0^s \cos(\kappa s') \, ds' = \frac{\sin(\kappa s)}{\kappa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Interpretation:* $z(s)$ is the projection along the base axis; $x(s)$ is the lateral deflection. At $s = L$ (the tip), this gives the unrotated tip position.

### Step 4 — Evaluate at the Tip

Setting $s = L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(L) &= \frac{1 - \cos(\kappa L)}{\kappa} \\
z(L) &= \frac{\sin(\kappa L)}{\kappa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* These can be rewritten using the standard constant-curvature tip-position convention by swapping the roles of $x$ and $z$. We choose the convention where the $x$-axis is in-plane and $z$ is along the base, but the common published convention is:

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
x_{tip} &= \frac{\sin(\kappa L)}{\kappa} \cos(\phi) \\
y_{tip} &= \frac{\sin(\kappa L)}{\kappa} \sin(\phi) \\
z_{tip} &= \frac{1 - \cos(\kappa L)}{\kappa}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

> *Justification of the rotated form:* We rotate the 2D tip position by angle $\phi$ about the base $z$-axis. The radial distance is $r = \frac{\sin(\kappa L)}{\kappa}$ and axial distance is $h = \frac{1 - \cos(\kappa L)}{\kappa}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Tendon Force Required to Bend a Catheter

**Statement:** A 7 Fr catheter (outer radius $r_o = 1.17$ mm, inner radius $r_i = 0.85$ mm) is made of Nitinol-braided Pebax with effective $E = 50$ GPa. It is bent to a curvature of $\kappa = 10$ m⁻¹ using a tendon offset $r_{off} = 0.5$ mm. Find the tendon force required.

### Step 1 — Calculate Area Moment of Inertia

$$
I = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(r_o^4 - r_i^4\right)
$$

Substituting values (in meters):

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_o^4 &= (1.17 \times 10^{-3})^4 = 1.874 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^4 \\
r_i^4 &= (0.85 \times 10^{-3})^4 = 0.521 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^4 \\
I &= \frac{\pi}{4}(1.874 - 0.521) \times 10^{-12} \\
I &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times 1.353 \times 10^{-12} \\
I &= 1.063 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Bending Moment

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= EI\kappa \\
M &= (50 \times 10^9)(1.063 \times 10^{-12})(10) \\
M &= 50 \times 10^9 \times 1.063 \times 10^{-11} \\
M &= 0.5315 \text{ N·m} \\
M &\approx 0.53 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Interpretation:* This is the internal moment the catheter must develop at its base to hold the prescribed curvature.

### Step 3 — Calculate Tendon Force Using Moment Equilibrium

Taking moments about the neutral axis, the tendon force $F_t$ at offset $r_{off}$ must balance $M$:

$$
F_t \cdot r_{off} = M \implies F_t = \frac{M}{r_{off}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t &= \frac{0.5315}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}} \\
F_t &= 1063 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Sanity check:* This is unrealistically high! Real systems use much lower $\kappa$ or much larger $r_{off}$, OR the catheter is much more flexible. For example, a true EP catheter with $EI \approx 5 \times 10^{-6}$ N·m² would need only ~0.05 N·m, giving $F_t \approx 0.1$ N (achievable with small DC motors).

### Step 4 — Engineering Reality Check

Commercial catheters use **braided composite structures** that lower effective $EI$ by orders of magnitude. Typical real-world $EI$ for an Artisan catheter:

$$
EI \approx 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ N·m}^2
$$

This is the value used in the Hansen Sensei system. The lesson: **braided shafts trade absolute stiffness for controllable compliance**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Workspace Volume of a 2-Section Catheter

**Statement:** A catheter has two bending sections, each of arc length $L = 5$ cm, each bendable to $\kappa_{max} = 20$ m⁻¹. Find the reachable workspace volume assuming spherical-sector approximation.

### Step 1 — Maximum Lateral Reach

For a single section at maximum curvature:

$$
R_{max} = \frac{\sin(\kappa L)}{\kappa}\bigg\vert_{\kappa = \kappa_{max}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\kappa_{max} L &= 20 \times 0.05 = 1.0 \text{ rad} \\
R_{max} &= \frac{\sin(1.0)}{20} = \frac{0.8415}{20} = 0.0421 \text{ m} \approx 4.2 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Maximum Axial Reach (when $\kappa = 0$)

$$
z_{max} = L = 5 \text{ cm}
$$

### Step 3 — Workspace of a 2-Section Catheter (coaxial bending)

With two independent bending sections, the total effective length is $2L = 10$ cm. The tip can reach a hemisphere of radius approximately:

$$
R_{hemi} \approx 2 L = 10 \text{ cm}
$$

The **workspace volume** (hemispherical shell, treating the heart as a target region):

$$
V = \frac{2}{3} \pi \left( R_{out}^3 - R_{in}^3 \right)
$$

With $R_{out} = 10$ cm and $R_{in} = 1$ cm (the minimum reachable distance given stiffness):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \frac{2}{3} \pi (1000 - 1) \text{ cm}^3 \\
V &\approx 2093 \text{ cm}^3 \\
V &\approx 2.1 \text{ liters}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Interpretation:* This 2.1 L workspace easily contains the entire left atrium (volume ~60 mL) and both ventricles, validating the design choice of 2-bending-section catheters.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Force Time Integral (FTI) for Lesion Quality

**Statement:** A contact force sensor records the following measurements during a 30-second RF ablation: 8 g, 12 g, 15 g, 14 g, 12 g, 10 g, 8 g, 7 g, 5 g, 4 g. The RF power is constant at 30 W. Compute the FTI and decide if the lesion is adequate.

### Step 1 — Compute the Time-Averaged Force

Using a 3-second sampling interval:

$$
\bar{CF} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} CF_i
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum CF_i &= 8+12+15+14+12+10+8+7+5+4 = 95 \text{ g} \\
\bar{CF} &= 95 / 10 = 9.5 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute FTI

$$
\begin{aligned}
FTI &= \bar{CF} \times T \\
FTI &= 9.5 \times 30 \\
FTI &= 285 \text{ g·s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compare to Clinical Threshold

Target: $FTI \geq 400$ g·s for an effective transmural lesion.

**Result:** 285 g·s < 400 g·s → **Inadequate lesion**. The cardiologist should either increase contact force or extend the ablation duration to:

$$
T_{required} = \frac{400}{9.5} \approx 42 \text{ s}
$$

> *Clinical decision rule:* A FTI < 200 g·s guarantees an ineffective lesion. A FTI between 200–400 g·s is borderline. A FTI > 400 g·s is considered durable.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Pull-Wire vs Magnetic Systems

| Parameter | Hansen Sensei (Pull-Wire) | Stereotaxis (Magnetic) |
|---|---|---|
| Tip Actuation | Tendon-driven, mechanical | Field-aligned, contactless |
| Catheter Stiffness | Required (must transmit force) | Very soft (no internal mechanics) |
| Tip Force Resolution | ~1 g (direct sensing) | Inferred from model (~3–5 g) |
| Mechanical Failure Modes | Tendon breakage, hysteresis | Magnet failure (extremely rare) |
| Workspace | Limited by mechanical stops | Limited only by magnet geometry |
| Procedure Time | Comparable | Comparable (slightly longer early cases) |
| Cost | ~$1M | ~$3M (incl. magnet room) |
| Open vs Closed Architecture | Closed (proprietary Artisan) | Open (any magnetic catheter) |
| Tactile Feedback | Direct (force sensor) | Indirect (catheter is floppy) |
| Patient Anesthesia | Standard | Sometimes deep sedation needed |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Definition of Robotic Catheter Ablation
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Style]**

**Q: Define Robotic Catheter System for cardiac Electrophysiology. List any two clinical advantages over manual ablation.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Robotic Catheter System** is a master–slave teleoperated mechatronic device that allows a cardiologist to remotely navigate a flexible catheter inside the heart chambers for diagnostic mapping and therapeutic ablation of cardiac arrhythmias.

**Two Clinical Advantages:**
1. **Significant reduction in physician fluoroscopy exposure** — from ~30 minutes (manual) to <3 minutes (robotic) per case, reducing long-term cancer risk for the operator.
2. **Sub-millimeter positional stability and force consistency** — robotic systems hold the catheter tip steady during the 30–60 s ablation period, eliminating drift caused by cardiac and respiratory motion, leading to more durable lesions.

*(Any other valid points like reduced operator fatigue, 3D map integration, remote operation are acceptable.)*

---

### Question 2: Contact Force in Catheter Ablation
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]**

**Q: What is the significance of contact force (CF) in catheter ablation? State the clinical target window for CF and the FTI threshold for an effective lesion.**

**Model Answer:**

**Significance:** Contact force is the **key parameter governing lesion quality and safety** during RF ablation. Insufficient force leads to ineffective, non-transmural lesions and arrhythmia recurrence. Excessive force (>40 g) risks tissue perforation, steam pop, and collateral damage to adjacent structures (e.g., esophagus during AF ablation).

**Clinical Target Window:** $CF = 10\text{–}20$ g for atrial wall ablation; $CF = 8\text{–}15$ g for ventricular ablation.

**FTI Threshold:** A Force Time Integral of $FTI \geq 400$ g·s is considered necessary for a durable transmural lesion.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A: Comprehensive Design of a Robotic EP Catheter

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, 14 Marks]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: A 7 Fr robotic ablation catheter has outer radius 1.17 mm, inner radius 0.85 mm, and is made of Nitinol-braided Pebax with effective Young's modulus E = 50 GPa. It must reach a target 4 cm away from its base at a bending angle of 90°. The tendon offset is 0.5 mm. Calculate:**
**(i) The required curvature $\kappa$**
**(ii) The required tendon force $F_t$**
**(iii) The expected FTI if the contact force is 12 g for 45 s of ablation**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Curvature Calculation:**

For 90° bending ($\kappa L = \pi/2$) of a catheter section of length $L = 4$ cm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\kappa L &= \frac{\pi}{2} \\
\kappa \times 0.04 &= 1.5708 \\
\kappa &= 39.27 \text{ m}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* A 90° bend in arc length 4 cm corresponds to traversing $\pi/2$ radians of arc. Solving for $\kappa$ gives the curvature.

**Part (ii) — Tendon Force:**

Area moment of inertia:

$$
I = \frac{\pi}{4}\left( r_o^4 - r_i^4 \right)
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{\pi}{4}\left( (1.17)^4 - (0.85)^4 \right) \times 10^{-12} \\
I &= \frac{\pi}{4}(1.874 - 0.521) \times 10^{-12} \\
I &= 1.063 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

Bending moment:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= EI\kappa = (50 \times 10^9)(1.063 \times 10^{-12})(39.27) \\
M &= 2.087 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Tendon force:

$$
F_t = \frac{M}{r_{off}} = \frac{2.087 \times 10^{-3}}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.17 \text{ N}
$$

> *Verification:* This force is well within the capability of a small DC motor (typical stall force ~10 N), confirming the design is feasible.

**Part (iii) — Force Time Integral:**

$$
FTI = CF \times T = 12 \times 45 = 540 \text{ g·s}
$$

> *Conclusion:* 540 g·s > 400 g·s → **Effective transmural lesion expected**. The procedure can proceed with this protocol.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Compare the two major robotic catheter philosophies — tendon-driven (Hansen Sensei) vs magnetic-navigation (Stereotaxis). Discuss at least 5 parameters with engineering trade-offs.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Hansen Sensei (Tendon) | Stereotaxis (Magnetic) |
|---|---|---|
| **Tip Actuation** | Mechanical pull-wires; rigid catheter | Field-aligned soft catheter; magnet in tip |
| **Tactile Feedback** | Direct force sensing at tip | Indirect (inferred from catheter model) |
| **Catheter Stiffness** | Higher EI (must transmit tendon force) | Very low EI (no internal mechanics needed) |
| **Tip Resolution** | 1–2 mm (encoder-limited) | <1 mm (analog field control) |
| **Mechanical Failure** | Tendon wear, hysteresis over thousands of cycles | Magnet fatigue (rare); no moving parts in catheter |
| **Setup Time** | Faster (cart-based) | Longer (shielded room, magnet calibration) |
| **Cost** | ~$1M USD | ~$3M USD |
| **Mapping Integration** | Proprietary (CARTO) | Open (any magnetic catheter, any mapping) |
| **Procedure Cost** | Lower per case | Higher per case |
| **Operator Learning Curve** | Shorter (joystick-like) | Longer (3D vector reasoning) |

**Trade-off Summary:** Tendon systems offer better haptics and lower cost; magnetic systems offer superior tip precision, patient safety (no mechanical contact force), and access to hard-to-reach anatomy.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students often confuse **magnetic force** with **magnetic torque**. The Stereotaxis system produces **torque** to orient the tip — the **translational force is negligible** inside the bore. Do not write "magnets pull the catheter forward" — they only align its orientation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** When asked for catheter workspace, students often give only the maximum reach. Examiners expect both the **outer reach** and the **inner reach** (limited by minimum curvature / maximum stiffness). Forgetting the inner boundary loses 2 marks.

---

### Question B: Catheter Kinematics and EP Procedure Analysis (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, 14 Marks]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: A robotic EP catheter bending section has arc length L = 3 cm and is bent to a curvature of $\kappa = 25$ m⁻¹ in a plane oriented at $\phi = 60°$ from the base axis. Calculate the 3D Cartesian tip position using the Piecewise Constant Curvature (PCC) model.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $\kappa L$:**

$$
\kappa L = 25 \times 0.03 = 0.75 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Radial Distance:**

$$
r = \frac{\sin(\kappa L)}{\kappa} = \frac{\sin(0.75)}{25} = \frac{0.6816}{25} = 0.02726 \text{ m} = 2.726 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Axial Distance:**

$$
z = \frac{1 - \cos(\kappa L)}{\kappa} = \frac{1 - \cos(0.75)}{25} = \frac{1 - 0.7317}{25} = \frac{0.2683}{25} = 0.01073 \text{ m} = 1.073 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 4 — Rotate by $\phi = 60°$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{tip} &= r \cos(\phi) = 0.02726 \times \cos(60°) = 0.02726 \times 0.5 = 0.01363 \text{ m} = 1.363 \text{ cm} \\
y_{tip} &= r \sin(\phi) = 0.02726 \times \sin(60°) = 0.02726 \times 0.8660 = 0.02361 \text{ m} = 2.361 \text{ cm} \\
z_{tip} &= 0.01073 \text{ m} = 1.073 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Tip Position:**

$$
\boxed{ (x, y, z) = (1.363, \, 2.361, \, 1.073) \text{ cm} }
$$

> *Sanity check:* The tip lies within a 2.726 cm radius from the base and is 1.073 cm forward — consistent with a 75%-of-quarter-circle arc in a tilted plane.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Describe the clinical workflow of a robotic catheter ablation for Atrial Fibrillation. Include the roles of the 3D electroanatomical mapping system, the contact force sensor, and the FTI threshold.**

**Model Answer:**

The clinical workflow consists of **seven sequential stages:**

1. **Vascular Access and Sheath Insertion:** The patient is placed under general anesthesia. A 12 Fr steerable sheath is introduced via the right femoral vein and advanced to the inferior vena cava, right atrium, and (after transseptal puncture) into the left atrium.

2. **Catheter Registration with 3D Map:** The ablation catheter (7 Fr) is inserted through the sheath. Its tip is touched to anatomical landmarks (e.g., coronary sinus, mitral annulus) to register the EM-tracked catheter position with the pre-acquired CT/MRI of the left atrium in the CARTO or EnSite system.

3. **Electroanatomical Mapping Phase:** The cardiologist navigates the catheter to ~50–100 points on the left atrial wall. At each point, the system records:
   - 3D position (from EM sensor)
   - Local bipolar electrogram (electrical timing)
   - Unipolar electrogram (morphology)
   - Contact force
   
   The system reconstructs a color-coded **activation map** showing the wavefront propagation in red (early) → blue (late).

4. **Identification of Arrhythmogenic Foci:** For AF, the standard target is **pulmonary vein isolation (PVI)** — circumferential lesions around the four pulmonary vein ostia. For persistent AF, additional linear lesions and rotor ablation may be added.

5. **Robotic Navigation to Target Sites:** Using the master console joystick, the cardiologist drives the catheter to each target point on the 3D map. The 4-DOF steerable sheath (Hansen) or magnetic field vector (Stereotaxis) articulates the tip.

6. **Contact Force Verification and RF Delivery:** Before ablation, the contact force sensor must read **10–20 g**. The foot pedal is pressed to deliver **30 W RF power for 30–60 s per point**. Real-time parameters displayed:
   - CF curve
   - Temperature
   - Impedance drop
   - FTI accumulating

7. **Lesion Verification and Closure:** After each point, the system checks $FTI \geq 400$ g·s. After all points, the cardiologist verifies **bidirectional electrical block** across each lesion line using pacing maneuvers. The catheter is withdrawn, vascular access closed, and the patient is monitored in the EP lab for 30 minutes.

**Role of the 3D Mapping System (CARTO/EnSite):** Acts as a **GPS for the heart**, providing:
- Real-time catheter position relative to anatomy
- Color-coded activation maps
- Ablation tags at each treated point
- Lesion contiguity index
- Reduced fluoroscopy requirement

**Role of the Contact Force Sensor:** Provides **direct quantitative feedback** of tip–tissue interaction:
- Prevents perforation (>40 g is dangerous)
- Ensures lesion efficacy (<5 g is ineffective)
- Replaces the "tactile feel" lost in robotic teleoperation
- Enables FTI calculation

**Role of the FTI Threshold:** $FTI \geq 400$ g·s is the **clinical decision rule** for declaring a lesion durable. Sub-threshold FTI correlates with arrhythmia recurrence at 6–12 month follow-up.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #3:** When describing EP workflow, students often skip the **transseptal puncture** step. This is a critical access maneuver and omitting it costs 1–2 marks. Examiners expect a complete flow from vascular access to verification.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #4:** In numerical problems on catheter bending, students forget to **convert mm to m** before substitution. A radius of 1.17 mm must be written as $1.17 \times 10^{-3}$ m, otherwise the final force will be off by a factor of $10^9$. Always write units at every step.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### Core Concepts
- [ ] Robotic EP catheter = **master–slave teleoperated system** for arrhythmia treatment
- [ ] Two main philosophies: **Tendon-driven (Hansen)** and **Magnetic (Stereotaxis)**
- [ ] Clinical gold standard: **Pulmonary Vein Isolation (PVI)** for Atrial Fibrillation
- [ ] Contact Force target: **10–20 g** for atrial ablation
- [ ] FTI threshold for durable lesion: **≥ 400 g·s**
- [ ] Magnetic field of Stereotaxis: **~0.1 T**, produces **torque only** (not translational force)

### Key Formulas to Memorize
- [ ] Tip position (PCC): $x = \frac{\sin(\kappa L)}{\kappa}\cos\phi$, etc.
- [ ] Bending moment: $M = EI\kappa$
- [ ] Hollow tube inertia: $I = \frac{\pi}{4}(r_o^4 - r_i^4)$
- [ ] Tendon force: $F_t = \frac{EI\kappa}{r_{off}}$
- [ ] Magnetic torque: $\tau = VMB\sin\theta$
- [ ] Lesion depth: $D \propto \sqrt{P \cdot t \cdot CF}$
- [ ] FTI: $\int CF \, dt$

### Hardware & Components
- [ ] Catheter sizes: **7 Fr inner (2.3 mm)**, **12 Fr outer sheath**
- [ ] Catheter materials: **Nitinol-braided Pebax**
- [ ] Force sensors: **FBG (Fiber Bragg Grating)** or **capacitive**
- [ ] Position sensors: **5–6 DOF EM coils** (Axiem, Aurora)
- [ ] Mapping systems: **CARTO 3** (Biosense Webster), **EnSite Precision** (Abbott)

### Workflow & Clinical Steps
- [ ] **7-stage EP workflow:** Access → Registration → Mapping → Identification → Navigation → Ablation → Verification
- [ ] **Transseptal puncture** is required for left atrial access
- [ ] **Bidirectional block** confirms successful PVI
- [ ] **Fluoroscopy reduction:** 90% for the operator

### Engineering Trade-offs (Frequently Asked)
- [ ] Smaller tendon offset → less friction but **higher tendon force** required
- [ ] Stiffer catheter (higher EI) → better force transmission but **larger workspace limit**
- [ ] Higher contact force → better lesion but **perforation risk**
- [ ] More bending sections → **larger workspace** but **more complex tendon routing**
- [ ] Magnetic vs mechanical: **magnetic = soft catheter, no haptics**; **mechanical = stiff catheter, haptics possible**

### Numerical Pitfalls to Avoid
- [ ] **Unit conversion** — always mm → m, g → kg if using SI base
- [ ] **Offset $r_{off}$** is from the **neutral axis**, not the geometric center
- [ ] **FTI > 200 g·s ≠ adequate** — must be ≥ 400 g·s for durability
- [ ] **Magnetic system cannot translate** the catheter — only orient it

### Quick Mnemonics
- [ ] **"S-M-M-F-S"** — Steerability, Mapping, Master–Slave, Force-feedback, Safety
- [ ] **"4DOF Sheath + 2DOF Catheter = Heart Hemisphere Workspace"**
- [ ] **"PCC = Piecewise Constant Curvature"** — the standard catheter kinematic model
- [ ] **"FTI = Force × Time"** — simplest clinical decision metric

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw a **labeled block diagram** of the robotic catheter system in any 7+ mark answer. Examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for clean, well-labeled system diagrams showing the master console, control computer, slave manipulator, sensor feedback loop, and the patient/heart interface. Even a hand-drawn schematic in pencil is acceptable and earns full credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Humanoids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT724 - MEDICAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Humanoids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Humanoid Robot (KTU Standard Definition):**
> A **humanoid robot** is an autonomous robotic system whose overall **anthropomorphic body structure** mimics the human body plan, possessing a **head, torso, two arms, and two legs**, equipped with a network of **proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensors**, and driven by **multi-degree-of-freedom (multi-DOF) actuators** that enable human-like locomotion, manipulation, and social interaction within unstructured environments.

In the context of **Medical Robotics (OEBRT724)**, humanoids are specialised robotic platforms engineered to operate in **clinical, rehabilitation, surgical-assistance, and elder-care** environments where human-robot physical proximity, gesture-based communication, and human-like dexterity provide tangible therapeutic and operational advantages.

### 1.2 Key Distinguishing Features of a Humanoid

A robot qualifies as a *humanoid* under the KTU classification only if it satisfies the following three structural criteria:

1. **Anthropomorphic Morphology** – bilateral symmetry, head, trunk, two arms, two legs.
2. **Bipedal Locomotion** – capability for two-legged walking (static or dynamic).
3. **Dexterous Manipulation** – multi-fingered end-effectors emulating human hand function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Distinction Box:**
> - A **Humanoid ≠ Android ≠ Bipedal Robot ≠ Human-Robot Interaction (HRI) System**.
> - *Bipedal robots* focus only on walking stability.
> - *Androids* emphasise outer skin/appearance replication.
> - *Humanoids* integrate morphology, bipedalism, manipulation, and cognition.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Hospital Colleague":**
> Imagine a **nurse in a hospital** who can walk down corridors, climb stairs to reach a ward, pick up a tray of medicine with two hands, gently hand it to a patient, and respond to verbal cues. A **humanoid robot is the engineered, programmable, non-biological counterpart** of this nurse. Just as the nurse has eyes (cameras), ears (microphones), a brain (CPU/AI processor), muscles (actuators), and joints (mechanical linkages), the humanoid replicates these subsystems in electromechanical form. The key engineering challenge is replicating *human-like grace* without *human fragility* — i.e., robots that never get tired, are sterile, and operate with millimetre precision.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics for Humanoid Evaluation

A KTU 2024 student must be familiar with the following benchmark metrics (often asked in Part A):

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Degrees of Freedom | **DOF** | **20 – 60+** | Determines flexibility of motion |
| Total Height | $h$ | **1.2 m – 1.8 m** | Optimises human-environment reach |
| Walking Speed | $v$ | **0.5 – 2.5 km/h** | Acceptance for clinical ambulation |
| Payload | $P$ | **0.5 – 7 kg per arm** | Manipulation capacity |
| Battery Autonomy | $T_b$ | **30 min – 4 h** | Operational endurance |
| Self-Weight | $W$ | **30 – 130 kg** | Stability vs. mobility trade-off |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — The "Magic Number" 12 + 7 + 6 + 1 = 26 DOF:**
> A textbook humanoid typically has:
> - **2 eyes/neck** = 2 DOF
> - **2 shoulders** × 3 + **2 elbows** × 1 + **2 wrists** × 2 = 10 DOF (arms)
> - **1 waist** × 2 = 2 DOF (torso)
> - **2 hips** × 3 + **2 knees** × 1 + **2 ankles** × 2 = 12 DOF (legs)
> - **Total ≈ 26 DOF** is the canonical reference.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Anatomical Architecture of a Humanoid

A humanoid is decomposed into **five primary subsystems** (the KTU-recommended modular framework):

### (a) **Mechanical Skeleton (Kinematic Chain)**
- Composed of rigid links connected by **revolute (R)** and **prismatic (P)** joints.
- Kinematically equivalent to a **serial chain manipulator per limb**, combined into a **tree-structured parallel mechanism** for the whole body.
- Each limb's pose is described by **Denavit–Hartenberg (D-H) parameters**.

### (b) **Actuation Subsystem**
| Actuator Type | Principle | Application | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DC Servo Motor** | Electromagnetic | Most commercial humanoids (NAO, Pepper) | Limited torque density |
| **Harmonic Drive + Motor** | Gear reduction | High-precision joints (ASIMO) | Expensive |
| **Series Elastic Actuator (SEA)** | Spring + Motor | Safe HRI, rehabilitation (HAL) | Complex control |
| **Pneumatic Muscle (PAM)** | Compressed air | Soft, compliant motion | Bulky compressor |
| **Hydraulic Cylinder** | Fluid pressure | Heavy-payload (Atlas, Boston Dynamics) | Leakage risk |

### (c) **Sensory Subsystem**
> [!NOTE]
> KTU classification divides humanoid sensors into:
> - **Proprioceptive (Internal)**: Encoders, IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit), joint torque sensors, force/torque at feet.
> - **Exteroceptive (External)**: RGB-D cameras, LiDAR, ultrasonic, microphones, tactile skin sensors.

### (d) **Control & Computing Subsystem**
Hierarchical architecture:
1. **High-level (Cognitive)**: Task planning, AI inference (e.g., gesture recognition, voice intent).
2. **Mid-level (Behavioural)**: Gait generation, ZMP-based walking, balance recovery.
3. **Low-level (Reactive)**: Joint servo loops at **1 kHz** (current, velocity, position).

### (e) **Power Subsystem**
Typically **Li-ion battery** with **48 V DC bus** for motors, regulated down to **5 V / 3.3 V** for logic.

---

## 2.2 Bipedal Locomotion and the Zero Moment Point (ZMP)

The single most important theoretical concept in humanoid robotics (and a KTU favourite) is the **Zero Moment Point (ZMP)** introduced by **Miomir Vukobratović (1972)**.

### 2.2.1 Definition of ZMP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ZMP Definition:**
> The **Zero Moment Point** is the point on the ground plane about which the **net moment of the active forces (inertial + gravitational)** generated by the robot's motion is **zero**. For a stable, dynamically-balanced walk, this point must lie **inside the support polygon** formed by the foot (or feet) in contact with the ground.

### 2.2.2 The Support Polygon

- **Single-support phase**: Support polygon = convex hull of the standing foot.
- **Double-support phase**: Support polygon = convex hull of *both* feet (a quadrilateral).

If the ZMP exits this polygon → the robot **tips over** (kinematically inadmissible).

### 2.2.3 The Linearised Cart-Table Model (used by KTU)

The humanoid's centre of mass (CoM) is modelled as a **cart of mass $m$** moving on a **frictionless table** with leg of length $L$ acting as a prismatic constraint. The 2D ZMP coordinates are:

$$
x_{zmp} = x_{com} - \frac{z_{com} \cdot \ddot{x}_{com}}{g + \ddot{z}_{com}}
$$

$$
y_{zmp} = y_{com} - \frac{z_{com} \cdot \ddot{y}_{com}}{g + \ddot{z}_{com}}
$$

Where:
- $(x_{com}, y_{com}, z_{com})$ = CoM position.
- $(\ddot{x}_{com}, \ddot{y}_{com}, \ddot{z}_{com})$ = CoM accelerations.
- $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$ = gravitational acceleration.

For the typical case where $\ddot{z}_{com} \approx 0$ (CoM height constant during walking), this simplifies to the **high-yield KTU form**:

$$
x_{zmp} = x_{com} - \frac{z_{com} \cdot \ddot{x}_{com}}{g}
$$

---

## 2.3 Forward Kinematics vs. Inverse Kinematics in Humanoid Limbs

| Operation | Goal | Typical Method | Difficulty |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Forward Kinematics (FK)** | Given joint angles $\theta$, find end-effector pose $T$ | Homogeneous transformation matrix multiplication | Trivial |
| **Inverse Kinematics (IK)** | Given desired end-effector pose $T$, find joint angles $\theta$ | Analytical (closed-form) or numerical (Jacobian pseudo-inverse) | Hard (multiple solutions, singularities) |

For a humanoid leg, the **forward kinematic chain** is:
$$
T_{foot}^{world} = T_{pelvis}^{world} \cdot T_{hip}^{pelvis} \cdot T_{knee}^{hip} \cdot T_{ankle}^{knee} \cdot T_{foot}^{ankle}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** FK is used in **motion-playback systems** for humanoid gaits (e.g., ASIMO's pre-recorded walk). IK is used in **real-time footstep planning** when the robot must reach a target position on uneven terrain or place a foot on a stair.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Quick-Reference Table)

> [!NOTE]
> Memorise this table. It is the single highest-yield compilation for Module 3 humanoids.

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ZMP (linearised) | $x_{zmp} = x_{com} - \dfrac{z_{com} \,\ddot{x}_{com}}{g + \ddot{z}_{com}}$ | metres, m/s² |
| 2 | ZMP stability | $x_{zmp} \in [x_{heel}, x_{toe}]$ | m |
| 3 | CoM height approx. for adult humanoid | $z_{com} \approx 1.0 \,\text{m}$ | m |
| 4 | Walking speed (typical) | $v \le \sqrt{g \cdot L \cdot \mu}$ | m/s, $\mu$ = friction |
| 5 | Static stability margin | $S = \min_{i} \left[ \text{dist}(ZMP, \text{edge}_i) \right]$ | m |
| 6 | Number of joints in humanoid | $N_j = 2N_{arm} + 2N_{leg} + N_{torso} + N_{head}$ | — |
| 7 | Total DOF (kinematic) | $\text{DOF} = 6(N-1) - \sum_i (6 - f_i)$ where $f_i$ = DOF of joint $i$ | Gruebler's eq. |
| 8 | Leg Jacobian determinant | $\det(J) \neq 0$ for non-singular | — |
| 9 | Stability (tilt angle) | $\theta_{tilt} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{a_{com}}{g}\right)$ | rad |
| 10 | Battery energy | $E = V \cdot I \cdot t$ | J |

---

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Rehabilitation robotics** (e.g., **HAL – Hybrid Assistive Limb, Cyberdyne, Japan**): Humanoid exoskeleton suits restore gait to paraplegic patients by reading **bioelectric signals (EMG)** from the skin surface.
- **Surgical assistance** (e.g., da Vinci research platforms with humanoid kinematic arms): Provides intuitive 7-DOF instrument handling.
- **Elder care and ASD therapy** (e.g., **NAO, Pepper, QTrobot**): Humanoids use expressive faces and gestures to deliver repetitive therapy to children with autism spectrum disorder.
- **Pharmaceutical logistics in hospitals** (e.g., **HSR, Toyota**): Autonomous bipedal delivery of drugs in wards.
- **Telepresence** for remote specialist consultation in rural clinics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the ZMP Equation (Linear Inverted Pendulum + Cart-Table Model)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this derivation is mandatory:**
> KTU 2024 ESE questions frequently test the **2-mark derivation of the ZMP relation** under the heading *"Derive the relationship between ZMP and CoM for a bipedal robot."* The following is the exact board-examination-ready derivation.

### Step 1 — System Setup

We model the humanoid as a single rigid body of total mass $m$ concentrated at its **Centre of Mass (CoM)** located at $(x_{com}, y_{com}, z_{com})$. The robot is supported on a horizontal ground plane. The reaction force from the ground acts at the **ZMP**, denoted $(x_{zmp}, y_{zmp}, 0)$.

### Step 2 — Net Force Balance (Newton's Second Law)

The sum of gravity and inertial forces equals the ground reaction:
$$
\vec{F}_{ground} = m\,\vec{a}_{com} - m\,g\,\hat{z}
$$

In scalar form (2D, x-direction):
$$
F_{x} = m\,\ddot{x}_{com}
$$

### Step 3 — Net Moment about the ZMP

The ZMP is defined as the point where the **net moment of all active forces vanishes**. Taking moments about the ZMP:

$$
\sum M_{zmp} = 0
$$

The active forces produce moments about the ZMP from:
1. **Gravity** acting at the CoM: moment arm = $(x_{com} - x_{zmp})$ in x-direction.
2. **Inertial force** at the CoM: effective horizontal moment = $-m\,\ddot{x}_{com} \cdot z_{com}$ (since the inertial force is horizontal but acts at height $z_{com}$).

### Step 4 — Equation Setup

$$
m\,g\,(x_{com} - x_{zmp}) - m\,\ddot{x}_{com} \cdot z_{com} = 0
$$

### Step 5 — Algebraic Rearrangement

$$
m\,g\,(x_{com} - x_{zmp}) = m\,\ddot{x}_{com} \cdot z_{com}
$$

Dividing both sides by $m$:

$$
g\,(x_{com} - x_{zmp}) = \ddot{x}_{com} \cdot z_{com}
$$

Solving for $x_{zmp}$:

$$
x_{com} - x_{zmp} = \frac{\ddot{x}_{com} \cdot z_{com}}{g}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,x_{zmp} = x_{com} - \frac{z_{com} \cdot \ddot{x}_{com}}{g}\,}
$$

### Step 6 — 3D Generalisation

Including vertical acceleration of the CoM (during squatting, climbing stairs):

$$
\boxed{\,x_{zmp} = x_{com} - \frac{z_{com} \cdot \ddot{x}_{com}}{g + \ddot{z}_{com}}\,\quad ; \quad y_{zmp} = y_{com} - \frac{z_{com} \cdot \ddot{y}_{com}}{g + \ddot{z}_{com}}\,}
$$

### Step 7 — Stability Criterion

For the humanoid to remain upright (no tipping):
$$
x_{zmp} \in [x_{support,\,min}, \quad x_{support,\,max}]
$$

In the single-support phase:
$$
x_{zmp} \in [x_{heel}, \quad x_{toe}]
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Numerical sanity check (KTU 2022 type question):**
> A humanoid has $z_{com} = 1.0$ m, $x_{com} = 0.5$ m, $\ddot{x}_{com} = 0.5$ m/s². Find $x_{zmp}$.
> 
> $x_{zmp} = 0.5 - \frac{1.0 \times 0.5}{9.81} = 0.5 - 0.05097 = 0.449$ m ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Gruebler-Kutzbach Criterion for DOF of a Humanoid Leg

### Step 1 — Gruebler's Formula (Planar)

For a planar mechanism with $n$ rigid links, $j_1$ single-DOF joints, $j_2$ two-DOF joints:

$$
\text{DOF}_{planar} = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

### Step 2 — Application to a Humanoid Leg

A typical humanoid leg (hip → knee → ankle → foot) has:
- $n = 4$ links (thigh, shin, foot, plus ground as a virtual link)
- Hip: 3-DOF ball joint (treated as 3 revolute joints = $j_1 = 3$)
- Knee: 1 revolute (j₁ += 1)
- Ankle: 2 revolute (j₁ += 2)

Substituting:
$$
\text{DOF}_{leg,\,planar} = 3(4 - 1) - 2(6) - 0 = 9 - 12 = -3
$$

> **Note:** The negative result indicates the leg is a **closed kinematic chain** (constrained by ground contact), not a free linkage.

### Step 3 — Spatial Correction

In 3D, for a humanoid with multiple limbs treated as a tree:
$$
\text{DOF}_{spatial} = 6(N - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{j} \left(6 - f_i\right)
$$

Where:
- $N$ = number of rigid bodies (including ground).
- $f_i$ = DOF of joint $i$.
- For a humanoid with **26 joints**, of which 24 are 1-DOF and 2 are 2-DOF:

$$
\text{DOF} = 6(N - 1) - \left[ 24(6 - 1) + 2(6 - 2) \right]
$$

This is a 2-step algebraic verification useful for ESE short-answers.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Exam-Ready Summary)

| Humanoid | Height (m) | DOF | Primary Medical Use | Locomotion |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ASIMO (Honda)** | 1.30 | 57 | R&D platform for gait rehab | Bipedal, dynamic walk |
| **NAO (SoftBank)** | 0.58 | 25 | ASD therapy, paediatric engagement | Bipedal, static-stable |
| **Pepper (SoftBank)** | 1.20 | 20 | Elder companion, patient reception | Wheeled base |
| **HAL (Cyberdyne)** | 1.60 | — | Wearable exoskeleton, paraplegic rehab | Exoskeleton over legs |
| **Atlas (Boston Dynamics)** | 1.80 | 28 | R&D for emergency hospital logistics | Hydraulic bipedal |
| **REEM-C (PAL Robotics)** | 1.65 | 22 | Hospital guide, R&D | Bipedal |
| **HRP-4 (AIST, Japan)** | 1.51 | 34 | R&D, nursing assistance | Bipedal |
| **ARMAR-III (KIT)** | 1.50 | 43 | Kitchen/rehab R&D | Wheeled base |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Insight:** The KTU examiner often asks *"Compare any two humanoids used in medical applications."* Use the table above to construct a 7-mark answer.

## 4.2 Kinematic Architecture of a Humanoid Leg

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pelvis Base Link] --> B[Hip Yaw 1 DOF]
    B --> C[Hip Roll 1 DOF]
    C --> D[Hip Pitch 1 DOF]

```

> A single leg in KTU canonical form: **6 DOF** matching the human hip-knee-ankle joint set. Total for both legs = **12 DOF**.

## 4.3 ZMP Stability Concept Visualised

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Robot CoM at height z_com] --> B[Project CoM onto ground]
    B --> C{Apply Inertial Force}

    E -->|Yes| F[ZMP found]
    E -->|No| G[Shift candidate point]
    G --> D
    F --> H{ZMP inside Foot Support Polygon?}

    H -->|No| J[UNSTABLE: Humanoid tips over]

```
