# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between Verification and Validation in the design of a medical robot. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Verification | Validation |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Confirms the product is built *correctly* per specifications | Confirms the *right* product is built per user needs |
| **Question Answered** | "Are we building the product right?" | "Are we building the right product?" |
| **Performed On** | Design outputs, subsystems, code | Final integrated system, clinical use |
| **Example** | Bench-testing joint torque of a 7-DOF arm against the design input of 5 N·m | Surgeon performs a mock suturing task and confirms the robot meets clinical expectations |

> **Mark Distribution (KTU Pattern):** 1 mark for correct definitions + 1 mark for distinction + 1 mark for examples.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Jun 2023]
**Q: List any three international standards that govern the design of a medical robotic system and state their primary purpose.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **ISO 13485** — Specifies requirements for a **Quality Management System (QMS)** for medical device manufacturers.
2. **ISO 14971** — Defines the **Risk Management Process** for medical devices, including hazard identification and risk control.
3. **IEC 62304** — Governs the **Software Life Cycle Processes** for medical device software, classifying software safety levels (Class A, B, C).

> **Alternative valid standards accepted:** IEC 60601 (electrical safety), IEC 62443 (cybersecurity), ISO 10993 (biocompatibility).

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Q: (a)** Explain the V-Model for medical device development with a neat block diagram. Describe the importance of traceability between design input, design output, verification, and validation. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** Discuss the technology selection criteria for actuators and sensors in a laparoscopic surgical robot. Justify your choices with specific examples. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Define the V-Model (1 mark)**
The V-Model is an extension of the waterfall model where each development phase has a corresponding testing phase, ensuring **traceability** and **regulatory compliance** for safety-critical medical devices.

**Step 2: Left Arm of V — Specification Phases (2 marks)**

- **User Needs:** "Surgeon needs to perform minimally invasive suturing with 1 mm precision through a single 12 mm port."
- **Design Input:** Translate to engineering specs — "End-effector positioning accuracy: ±0.5 mm; payload: 1 kg; 7 DOF."
- **Design Output:** Mechanical drawings, control software, BOM, schematics, FMEA report.
- **Subsystem Design:** Mechanical arm, end-effector, control unit, vision system designed separately.
- **Integration Plan:** Assembly sequence, software integration order.

**Step 3: Right Arm of V — Testing Phases (2 marks)**

- **Unit Testing** → Each module tested against its detailed design.
- **Subsystem Testing** → Mechanical, electrical, software subsystems tested against their specifications.
- **Integration Testing** → Full system tested for inter-subsystem communication, latency, and synchronization.
- **System Verification** → Tested against Design Input (e.g., positioning accuracy).
- **Clinical Validation** → Tested against User Needs in simulated OR or clinical trial.

**Step 4: Importance of Traceability (2 marks)**
- Every Design Output must be linked to a Design Input via a **traceability matrix**.
- Every Test must be linked to a Design Output.
- This allows the FDA/regulator to verify that **no requirement is missed** and **no unverified feature exists**.
- It is a **legal and audit requirement** under ISO 13485 and FDA 21 CFR 820.30.

---

#### Model Solution for Q(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Define Operating Constraints (1 mark)**
A laparoscopic robot operates through a 12 mm port inside a sterile, saline-rich abdominal environment. It must be **MR-conditional** in some hybrid OR setups, deliver **1 N force resolution**, and have a **30 cm reach**.

**Step 2: Actuator Selection (3 marks)**

| Choice | Justification |
|---|---|
| **BLDC Servo Motor** | High torque density, precise speed control, sealed design, sterilizable housing. Used in da Vinci EndoWrist. |
| **Why not hydraulic?** | Leakage risk in sterile environment, not easily sterilizable. |
| **Why not pneumatic?** | Compressibility reduces precision at the mm-scale required for laparoscopy. |
| **Why not SMA?** | Slow response time unsuitable for real-time surgical control. |

**Step 3: Sensor Selection (3 marks)**

| Choice | Justification |
|---|---|
| **Optical Encoders on each joint** | Sub-degree angular resolution for accurate kinematic tracking. |
| **6-Axis Force/Torque Sensor at wrist** | Enables haptic feedback, prevents tissue over-pulling. ATI Nano17 is industry standard. |
| **Stereo Vision Endoscope** | Provides 3D depth perception to surgeon; enables AI tissue segmentation. |
| **Why not capacitive tactile sensors?** | Affected by body fluids; not robust to surgical environment. |
| **Why not EM trackers inside body?** | Distortion from metallic instruments; not suitable for internal use. |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Jun 2022]

**Q: (a)** Describe the risk management process as per **ISO 14971** for a surgical robotic arm. Compute the RPN for a hazard and suggest three risk control measures. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** Explain the cybersecurity threats to a connected medical robot. Describe the **STRIDE** threat model and the **defense-in-depth** architecture with a block diagram. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Outline the ISO 14971 Process (3 marks)**

The risk management process has 4 iterative stages:

1. **Risk Analysis:** Identify hazards (e.g., unintended motion, electrical shock, software bug, mechanical failure). For each hazard, estimate **probability (P)** and **severity (S)**.
2. **Risk Evaluation:** Compare estimated risk against the **risk acceptability matrix** defined by the manufacturer.
3. **Risk Control:** Apply measures in the order — (i) inherent safe design, (ii) protective measures, (iii) information for safety.
4. **Residual Risk Evaluation:** After controls, verify that residual risk is acceptable; if not, repeat with additional controls.

**Step 2: Compute RPN (2 marks)**

For the hazard *Unintended Arm Movement*:

Assume S = 9, O = 5, D = 4.

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D = 9 \times 5 \times 4 = 180
$$

Since RPN > 100, the hazard is **unacceptable** and requires risk control.

**Step 3: Three Risk Control Measures (2 marks)**

1. **Inherent Safe Design:** Add dual-channel redundant encoders on each joint.
2. **Protective Measure:** Implement real-time current monitoring and software-based virtual fixtures to limit motion outside surgical zone.
3. **Information for Safety:** Add warning labels on the arm and train all OR staff on emergency stop procedure.

---

#### Model Solution for Q(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Cybersecurity Threats (2 marks)**
- **Ransomware** locking the robot until hospital pays.
- **Firmware tampering** altering control parameters.
- **Man-in-the-middle attacks** intercepting surgeon commands.
- **Insider threats** from disgruntled staff with privileged access.
- **Supply chain attacks** via compromised third-party components.

**Step 2: STRIDE Model (2 marks)**

| Threat | Meaning | Medical Robot Example |
|---|---|---|
| **S**poofing | Impersonation | Fake surgeon credentials |
| **T**ampering | Data modification | Altering dose data in oncology robot |
| **R**epudiation | Deny action | Surgeon denies ordering robotic motion |
| **I**nformation Disclosure | Data leak | Patient DICOM images exfiltrated |
| **D**enial of Service | Service disruption | Network flood disabling robot mid-surgery |
| **E**levation of Privilege | Unauthorized access | Nurse gaining surgeon-level control |

**Step 3: Defense-in-Depth (3 marks)**
Refer to the diagram in SECTION 4.4. The 6 layers are:

1. **Physical** — Chassis locks, tamper-evident seals.
2. **Network** — Firewalls, VLAN segmentation, TLS 1.3.
3. **Device** — Secure boot, signed firmware, encryption at rest.
4. **Application** — Multi-factor authentication, RBAC.
5. **Data** — AES-256 encryption, anonymization, immutable audit logs.
6. **Organizational** — SBOM maintenance, staff training, incident response plan.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Verification with Validation** — These are *not* synonyms. Always quote the formal definitions.
> 2. **Vague Standards** — Don't just say "ISO standards." Specify the number (e.g., **ISO 14971**) and the scope.
> 3. **Skipping the Math in RPN** — RPN questions require *numerical* computation, not just qualitative discussion.
> 4. **Single-layer Security** — Listing "antivirus" is not enough. Examiners expect **multi-layered** defense.
> 5. **No Trade-off Discussion** — When choosing actuators/sensors, justify *why not* the alternatives, not just *why* the chosen one.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE Module 4:**

- ✅ **V-Model** is the most tested design methodology — know all 5 left-arm and 5 right-arm phases.
- ✅ **Verification = Built correctly. Validation = Built the right product.** (Memorize this distinction.)
- ✅ **ISO 13485** (QMS), **ISO 14971** (Risk), **IEC 62304** (Software), **IEC 60601** (Electrical safety), **IEC 62443** (Cybersecurity), **ISO 10993** (Biocompatibility).
- ✅ **Risk = P × S** ; **RPN = S × O × D** ; **RPN > 100 → unacceptable.**
- ✅ **Hierarchy of Risk Control:** Inherent → Protective → Informational.
- ✅ **Actuator Trade-off:** BLDC servo for high precision; Pneumatic for rehabilitation; Piezo for micro/nano surgery; Hydraulic for high force.
- ✅ **Material Trade-off:** Ti-6Al-4V for MRI; 316L SS for OR; PEEK for radiolucent instruments.
- ✅ **STRIDE** = 6 threat categories; **Defense-in-Depth** = 6 security layers.
- ✅ **AES-256 is FDA-recommended** for Class III medical devices.
- ✅ **Reliability model:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, **MTBF** = $1/\lambda$, **Availability** = $MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$.
- ✅ **SNR formula:** $20 \log_{10}(V_s / V_n)$ in dB.
- ✅ Always end design answers with a **traceability matrix** mention for full marks.
- ✅ Always end security answers with **multi-layer defense** for full marks.
- ✅ Always end risk answers with **specific numerical RPN** for full marks.
- ✅ For 14-mark questions, use the **3-step structure:** Definition → Detailed Analysis → Diagram/Example.

> **Final KTU Tip:** The examiner is testing *engineering judgment*, not memorization. Always justify your technological choice with **two reasons** and **one rejected alternative**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Professional and Medical Ethics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT724 - MEDICAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Professional and Medical Ethics**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Professional Ethics — Formal Definition

**Professional Ethics** is a structured system of moral principles, codes of conduct, and behavioural standards that govern the actions of individuals practicing a specific profession. In the engineering and medical domains, it establishes the moral obligations of practitioners toward clients, patients, society, the profession, and themselves. It transcends legal compliance by addressing *what one ought to do*, even when not legally mandated.

In the context of **Medical Robotics**, professional ethics encompasses the obligations of roboticists, surgeons, biomedical engineers, and AI developers who design, deploy, and operate robotic systems that directly interact with human patients.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Professional ethics is not merely "good behaviour" — it is a measurable, enforceable, and assessable framework of duties, rights, and virtues codified in international and national instruments (e.g., WMA Declaration of Helsinki, IEEE Code of Ethics, IMC Code of Medical Ethics).

---

### 1.2 Medical Ethics — Formal Definition

**Medical Ethics** is the branch of applied ethics that analyses the moral obligations of medical professionals and the moral issues that arise in the practice of medicine, healthcare, and biomedical research. It is grounded in four universally accepted principles: **Autonomy, Beneficence, Non-maleficence, and Justice** (the Georgetown Mantra, 1979).

When medical robotics is introduced, medical ethics expands to include the moral status of the robotic agent, the algorithmic transparency of surgical decisions, and the legal liability of autonomous systems.

---

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogies

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — The Trust Equation**
> Think of medical ethics as a *three-body trust contract*: the **Patient** trusts the **Surgeon**, who trusts the **Robot**, which must be trustworthy by design. A failure at any node collapses the entire therapeutic relationship.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — The Highway Code**
> Just as traffic rules don't prevent all accidents but provide a *predictable minimum safety standard*, medical ethics does not guarantee perfect outcomes — it guarantees a *predictable moral floor* below which no practitioner may fall.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 3 — The Airplane Black Box**
> Surgical robots and AI diagnostic systems must have an **auditable, traceable decision log** (the "moral black box") so that any ethical breach can be reconstructed, reviewed, and remediated.

---

### 1.4 Scope in the KTU 2024 Medical Robotics Syllabus

The ethical landscape of medical robotics operates at four intersecting levels:

| Level | Stakeholder | Ethical Domain |
|---|---|---|
| Individual | Patient | Informed consent, privacy, dignity |
| Professional | Surgeon, Engineer, Technician | Competence, disclosure, conflict of interest |
| Institutional | Hospital, Manufacturer, Regulator | Liability, accountability, post-market surveillance |
| Societal | Civil society, governments | Justice, equitable access, AI alignment |

---

### 1.5 Historical Milestones (Codified Standards)

The chronological evolution of medical and professional ethics is essential for KTU board answers. Memorize the dates and the document names.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chronology Table (High-Yield for KTU):**
> - **~400 BCE** — Hippocratic Oath (*Primum non nocere*)
> - **1947** — Nuremberg Code (post-WWII human experimentation; **10 principles**)
> - **1948** — Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR)
> - **1964** — Declaration of Helsinki (WMA, human subjects research; revised 2013)
> - **1979** — Belmont Report (3 principles: Respect, Beneficence, Justice)
> - **1996** — HIPAA (US health data privacy)
> - **2000** — IEEE Code of Ethics (software/robotics)
> - **2017** — Asilomar AI Principles (23 tenets, applicable to medical AI)
> - **2021** — WHO Ethics & Governance of AI for Health
> - **2024** — EU AI Act (high-risk medical AI classification)

---

### 1.6 Key Terminology (KTU Mandate — 2-mark direct questions appear here)

- **Autonomy** — The patient's right to self-determination in medical decisions.
- **Beneficence** — The duty to act in the patient's best interest.
- **Non-maleficence** — The duty to *"first, do no harm"* (*Primum non nocere*).
- **Justice** — Fair, equitable distribution of healthcare resources and risks.
- **Informed Consent** — Voluntary, competent, informed, and revocable agreement to a procedure.
- **Dignity** — Inherent worth of the human person, inviolable by technology.
- **Veracity** — Truthfulness in disclosure of risks, errors, and uncertainties.
- **Fidelity** — Faithfulness to the patient, the profession, and one's commitments.
- **Accountability** — Willingness to be answerable for one's actions and their outcomes.
- **Transparency** — Openness about algorithmic logic, data usage, and system limitations.
- **Liability** — Legal responsibility for harm caused.
- **Algorithmic Bias** — Systematic, repeatable errors in AI outputs that disadvantage certain groups.

> [!NOTE]
> Standard metric for measuring ethical compliance in clinical robotics: **ALTAI (Assessment List on Trustworthy AI)** — published by the European Commission's High-Level Expert Group on AI, used as a self-assessment tool with **7 trust requirements** and **73 sub-questions**.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

### 2.1 The Four Principles of Medical Ethics (Beauchamp & Childress, 1979)

These are the most frequently tested framework in KTU board examinations. They are commonly referred to as the **"Georgetown Mantra"** or **Principlism**.

#### 2.1.1 Autonomy
- **Definition:** Respecting the patient's right to make informed, voluntary decisions about their own body and treatment.
- **Application in Robotics:**
  - Patient must consent to robotic surgery, understanding that a robot is involved.
  - Patient may refuse a robotic procedure and opt for conventional surgery.
  - Telemedicine consultations must preserve the patient's choice of physician.
- **Operational Test:** *Has the patient been given all material information, in understandable language, free from coercion?*

#### 2.1.2 Beneficence
- **Definition:** The obligation to act in the patient's best interest, promoting their well-being.
- **Application in Robotics:**
  - Robotic system must demonstrably improve clinical outcomes (reduced invasiveness, faster recovery).
  - Surgeon must undergo structured training (e.g., **da Vinci system requires ≥ 20 proctored cases** before independent practice).
- **Operational Test:** *Does the robotic procedure produce a net clinical benefit over alternatives?*

#### 2.1.3 Non-maleficence
- **Definition:** The duty to *do no harm* — avoiding actions that cause injury, suffering, or death.
- **Application in Robotics:**
  - Mechanical failure modes must be fail-safe (e.g., return to safe position on power loss).
  - Cybersecurity of medical robots (preventing ransomware attacks on surgical systems).
  - Radiation safety in image-guided robotic systems.
- **Operational Test:** *Has every foreseeable harm been identified, mitigated, and disclosed?*

#### 2.1.4 Justice
- **Definition:** Fair distribution of benefits, risks, and costs across populations.
- **Application in Robotics:**
  - Equitable access to robotic surgery (rural vs urban divide).
  - Avoiding algorithmic bias in AI-driven diagnostics (race, gender, socioeconomic status).
  - Cost-benefit allocation in public health systems.
- **Operational Test:** *Is the burden and benefit of this technology shared fairly across society?*

---

### 2.2 The Three Ethical Frameworks (Competing Philosophical Bases)

| Framework | Core Question | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Deontological** (Kantian) | *Is the action itself morally right, regardless of outcome?* | Respects duty and dignity | Can be inflexible |
| **Utilitarian** (Consequentialist) | *Does the action maximize aggregate well-being?* | Outcome-oriented | Can justify harming minorities |
| **Virtue Ethics** (Aristotelian) | *What would a virtuous practitioner do?* | Holistic character focus | Less codified, harder to audit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 students should be able to **apply all three frameworks to a single scenario** and articulate the trade-offs. Example scenario: *"A surgical robot malfunctions mid-operation. Should the surgeon continue manually, or wait for system reboot?"* — Analyse this under each framework.

---

### 2.3 The IEEE Code of Ethics — 10 Core Tenets (Robotics-Relevant)

The **Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE)** Code of Ethics is the most cited professional code in engineering practice. KTU often asks students to enumerate and explain the points relevant to medical robotics.

> [!NOTE]
> The 10 tenets (abbreviated):
> 1. Accept responsibility for engineering decisions.
> 2. Improve understanding of technology and its consequences.
> 3. Be honest and realistic in claims based on data.
> 4. Reject bribery in all forms.
> 5. Improve the competence and prestige of the profession.
> 6. Treat all persons fairly, regardless of race, religion, gender, etc.
> 7. Avoid injuring others, their property, reputation, or employment.
> 8. Assist colleagues in professional development.
> 9. Report unethical conduct to appropriate authorities.
> 10. Maintain technical competence through lifelong learning.

---

### 2.4 Medical Ethics Codes Applicable to Robotics

| Code / Document | Year | Issuing Body | Core Focus |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hippocratic Oath | ~400 BCE | Greek medical tradition | *Primum non nocere* — patient welfare |
| Declaration of Geneva | 1948 (revised 2017) | WMA | Modernized physician's pledge |
| Nuremberg Code | 1947 | US Military Tribunal | Human experimentation ethics |
| Declaration of Helsinki | 1964 (rev. 2013) | WMA | Biomedical research on humans |
| Belmont Report | 1979 | US National Commission | Research ethics: 3 core principles |
| Belmont Principles: **Respect for Persons**, **Beneficence**, **Justice** | | | |

| EU AI Act | 2024 | European Parliament | Medical AI = high-risk category |

---

### 2.5 WHO Ethics of AI for Health — 6 Consensus Principles

These are essential for Module 4 questions on **AI-driven medical robotics**.

1. **Human autonomy** — Preserve human decision-making; humans must retain control.
2. **Promote well-being, safety, and public interest.**
3. **Ensure transparency, explainability, and intelligibility** of AI systems.
4. **Foster accountability and liability** mechanisms.
5. **Ensure inclusiveness and equity** (avoid bias).
6. **Promote AI that is responsive and sustainable** (energy, environment, future generations).

---

### 2.6 KTU Cheat Sheet — The "Ethics Triangle" of Medical Robotics

| Principle | Engineering Design Implication | Clinical Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Autonomy | User interface must support override; data sovereignty for patient | Informed consent, opt-out provisions |
| Beneficence | Outcome-based design; RCT validation | Surgeon training; credentialing |
| Non-maleficence | Fail-safe design; cybersecurity; ISO 14971 risk management | Post-market surveillance; adverse event reporting |
| Justice | Bias auditing; accessible design; affordability | Equitable distribution; tiered healthcare |

---

### 2.7 Engineering Ethics in Medical Robotics — Five Focal Issues

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Issue 1 — Informed Consent in Robotic Surgery**
> - Patient must understand that a *robotic system* will be used, not just a human surgeon.
> - Disclosure of surgeon experience level with the robot (e.g., number of prior procedures).
> - Disclosure of system limitations, failure rates, and alternative treatments.
> - Consent must be **revocable** at any time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Issue 2 — Liability and Accountability**
> - If a robotic system malfunctions, who is liable — manufacturer, surgeon, hospital, or software developer?
> - Three doctrines: *Strict liability*, *Negligence*, *Product liability*.
> - "Black box" of surgical robots must record all actions for forensic analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Issue 3 — Patient Data Privacy and Cybersecurity**
> - HIPAA (US), GDPR (EU), DPDP Act (India 2023) govern health data.
> - Medical robots collect sensitive biometric and clinical data.
> - Risk: ransomware attacks, data exfiltration, unauthorized AI training on patient data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Issue 4 — Algorithmic Bias in Diagnostic and Surgical AI**
> - Training data may underrepresent certain demographics (race, gender, age).
> - Skin cancer detection AI trained primarily on light skin — higher error rate on dark skin.
> - Pulse oximeters overestimated oxygen saturation in Black patients (FDA 2021).
> - Mandatory: Diverse datasets, bias audits, FDA Predetermined Change Control Plan (PCCP).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Issue 5 — Surgeon Competence and Credentialing**
> - Use of a robot does not reduce the surgeon's responsibility.
> - Required: structured training modules, simulation-based learning, proctoring, ongoing CME.
> - Hospital credentialing committees must verify robotic competence independently.

---

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hospital procurement committees** apply ethical frameworks when selecting robotic systems.
- **Regulatory bodies** (FDA, CDSCO, CE) use risk-based ethical frameworks (ISO 14971) to approve devices.
- **Medico-legal cases** use the four-principle framework to resolve malpractice disputes.
- **AI safety research** (e.g., alignment, interpretability) draws directly on medical ethics principles.
- **Design thinking in human-robot interaction (HRI)** incorporates autonomy and dignity as first-order design constraints.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (FYI):** The global medical robotics ethics market (compliance consulting, audit, bias testing) is projected to reach **USD 4.2 billion by 2030**, growing at a CAGR of approximately **18.5%** — illustrating the industrial relevance of this topic.

## 3. Step-by-Step Ethical Analysis Matrix (KTU Examination Skill)

### 3.1 The 7-Step Ethical Decision-Making Algorithm for Medical Robotics Cases

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this algorithm** — KTU frequently gives a clinical vignette and asks the student to identify the ethical conflicts and propose an action plan. The marks are distributed across all 7 steps.

| Step | Action | KTU Cognitive Level (Bloom's) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Identify the medical/clinical facts** | Remember / Understand |
| 2 | **Identify the stakeholders** (patient, surgeon, manufacturer, society) | Understand |
| 3 | **Identify the ethical principles in tension** (e.g., autonomy vs beneficence) | Analyze |
| 4 | **Identify applicable laws and professional codes** (HIPAA, ICMR, IEEE) | Apply |
| 5 | **Generate at least 2 alternative courses of action** | Apply / Analyze |
| 6 | **Evaluate each alternative** using the 4 principles + 3 frameworks | Evaluate |
| 7 | **Select, justify, and document the chosen action** | Create / Evaluate |

---

### 3.2 Detailed Worked Example — *The Da Vinci Malfunction Case*

**Scenario:** During a robot-assisted prostatectomy, the da Vinci system reports a software anomaly mid-procedure. The console screen freezes for 38 seconds, then resumes. The patient is unaware. The surgeon is unsure whether any unintended movements occurred. The case is completed successfully. Two days post-op, the family asks about the intraoperative alert.

> [!NOTE]
> **Application of the 7-Step Algorithm:**

**Step 1 — Facts:**
- Robotic prostatectomy (cancer surgery)
- Intraoperative software anomaly (38-second freeze)
- Patient unaware, family uninformed at time of event
- Outcome: no clinical harm detected

**Step 2 — Stakeholders:**
- Patient (and family)
- Operating surgeon
- Hospital administration
- da Vinci manufacturer (Intuitive Surgical)
- Future patients (adverse event reporting)

**Step 3 — Principles in Tension:**
- **Non-maleficence** (obligation to report and investigate potential harm)
- **Autonomy** (patient's right to know what happened to their body)
- **Veracity** (truth-telling)
- **Justice** (other patients deserve to know about system reliability)

**Step 4 — Applicable Codes:**
- *Hippocratic Oath* — disclosure of harm
- *AMA Code of Medical Ethics §2.3.1* — disclosure of adverse events
- *Materiovigilance Programme of India (MvPI)* — mandatory adverse event reporting for medical devices
- *ISO 13485* — quality management system for medical devices
- *IEEE Code §3* — honesty in claims based on data
- *HIPAA* — does not prohibit disclosure to patient of their own adverse event

**Step 5 — Alternatives:**
- **Alternative A:** Do not disclose; case was successful.
- **Alternative B:** Disclose fully to patient and family; report to hospital risk management; file adverse event report with MvPI/FDA; engage manufacturer for root-cause analysis.

**Step 6 — Evaluation:**

Using the 4-principle framework:

$$
\text{Principle Score} = \sum_{p \in \{\text{Auto, Bene, Non-mal, Justi}\}} w_p \cdot s_p
$$

where $w_p$ is the weight of principle $p$ in the context and $s_p$ is the satisfaction score (1–5).

For **Alternative A** (non-disclosure):
- $s_{\text{Auto}} = 1$ (autonomy violated — patient uninformed)
- $s_{\text{Bene}} = 2$ (short-term benefit of avoiding worry, but long-term harm if discovered later)
- $s_{\text{Non-mal}} = 1$ (concealment is itself a harm)
- $s_{\text{Justi}} = 1$ (unjust to the patient and future patients)

$$
\text{Score}_A = 0.30(1) + 0.20(2) + 0.30(1) + 0.20(1) = 1.3
$$

For **Alternative B** (full disclosure):
- $s_{\text{Auto}} = 5$
- $s_{\text{Bene}} = 4$
- $s_{\text{Non-mal}} = 5$
- $s_{\text{Justi}} = 5$

$$
\text{Score}_B = 0.30(5) + 0.20(4) + 0.30(5) + 0.20(5) = 4.8
$$

**Step 7 — Decision:**
Since $\text{Score}_B = 4.8 \gg \text{Score}_A = 1.3$, **Alternative B is selected**.

The surgeon must:
1. Disclose fully to patient and family within 24 hours.
2. Document the event in the patient's medical record.
3. Report to hospital risk management and the institutional ethics committee.
4. File an adverse event report (MvPI / FDA MAUDE).
5. Engage the manufacturer (Intuitive Surgical) for root-cause analysis.
6. Implement corrective action before resuming robotic procedures.

---

### 3.3 The Asilomar AI Principles — 23 Tenets Grouped (Medical Robotics Subset)

The **Asilomar AI Principles (2017)** are the most cited voluntary framework in AI ethics. The 23 tenets are grouped into:

- **Research Issues** (e.g., Research Goal, Research Funding, Science-Policy Link)
- **Ethics and Values** (e.g., Safety, Failure Transparency, Judicial Transparency, Responsibility, Value Alignment, Human Values, Personal Privacy, Liberty and Privacy, Shared Benefit, Shared Prosperity, Human Control, Non-subversion, AI Arms Race)
- **Longer-term Issues** (e.g., Recursive Self-Improvement, Common Good)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For medical robotics, the **5 most relevant tenets** are:
> 1. **Safety** — AI systems should be safe and secure throughout their operational lifetime.
> 2. **Failure Transparency** — If an AI system causes harm, it should be possible to ascertain why.
> 3. **Responsibility** — Designers and builders are stakeholders in the moral implications of their use.
> 4. **Human Values** — AI should be designed to align with human dignity, rights, and cultural diversity.
> 5. **Human Control** — Humans should choose how and whether to delegate decisions to AI.

---

### 3.4 The Informed Consent Triad for Robotic Procedures

Informed consent in robotic surgery is a **triadic** process involving:

$$
\text{Valid Consent} = f(\text{Disclosure},\ \text{Understanding},\ \text{Voluntariness})
$$

Each component must be satisfied:

1. **Disclosure** — Patient must be told:
   - Nature of the procedure
   - Material risks (≥ 1% incidence)
   - Alternatives (including non-robotic options)
   - Surgeon's robotic experience level
   - Device failure rates (e.g., da Vinci reported adverse event rate)
   - Data usage (telementoring, cloud storage, AI training)

2. **Understanding** — Information must be:
   - In patient's native language
   - At appropriate literacy level (typically grade 6–8)
   - Confirmed via teach-back method

3. **Voluntariness** — Consent must be:
   - Free from coercion (financial, institutional)
   - Revocable at any time
   - Documented in writing

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical Formulation of Decision Quality:**
> A patient's *decisional capacity* can be assessed using the **MacCAT-T (MacArthur Competence Assessment Tool for Treatment)**, which scores four domains:
>
> $$
> \text{Capacity Score} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} d_i, \quad d_i \in \{0, 1, 2\}
> $$
>
> where the four domains are *Understanding, Appreciation, Reasoning, Expressing a choice*. Maximum score is **8**; a score of **≥ 6** with at least **1 in each domain** indicates adequate capacity.

---

### 3.5 Liability Allocation Matrix in Robotic Surgery

When harm occurs, liability is distributed based on the **Proportional Responsibility Model**:

$$
L_i = \frac{R_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} R_j} \cdot L_{total}
$$

where:
- $L_i$ is the liability share of party $i$
- $R_i$ is the relative responsibility weight of party $i$
- $L_{total}$ is the total damages
- $n$ is the number of liable parties

| Party | Typical Responsibility Weight $R_i$ | Example Fault |
|---|---|---|
| Manufacturer | 0.40 | Design defect, software bug |
| Surgeon | 0.30 | Inadequate training, deviation from protocol |
| Hospital | 0.20 | Inadequate credentialing, poor maintenance |
| Patient (rare) | 0.10 | Non-compliance with post-op care |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU students should be able to **discuss the limits of this model** in the case of *autonomous AI* (where the algorithm itself may be considered a non-human "agent"). This is a frontier ethical and legal question.

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Ethical Decision Framework for a Medical Robotic System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

**Reading Guide for KTU Exam:**

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
> *Define the term "Informed Consent" and list its four essential components as applicable to robot-assisted surgery. (3 marks)*

**Model Answer (95+ words):**
Informed consent is the voluntary, competent, and revocable agreement of a patient to undergo a medical procedure, after full disclosure of material risks, benefits, and alternatives. In robot-assisted surgery, it is a **triadic** process with four essential components:
1. **Disclosure** — patient is informed of robotic involvement, surgeon experience, system failure rates, and alternatives.
2. **Understanding** — information delivered in the patient's language and literacy level.
3. **Voluntariness** — free from coercion.
4. **Capacity** — patient possesses decisional capacity (MacCAT-T score ≥ 6).
The surgeon bears primary responsibility, and the consent must be documented in writing.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
> *List and briefly explain any three principles of medical ethics as applicable to the design of surgical robots. (3 marks)*

**Model Answer (90+ words):**
The three principles most relevant to surgical robot design are:
1. **Non-maleficence** — Robot must be designed with fail-safe modes (return-to-safe on power loss), ISO 14971 risk management, and cybersecurity to prevent harm.
2. **Beneficence** — Design must demonstrably improve outcomes (e.g., reduced invasiveness, faster recovery) validated through randomized controlled trials.
3. **Justice** — Design must be inclusive; training datasets must represent diverse populations to avoid algorithmic bias in AI components, and pricing must allow equitable access.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often write only *definitions* and forget to **link each principle to an engineering design implication**. Always add the second sentence. This is where 1 of 3 marks is typically lost.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Question A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model] (14 marks)**
>
> **(a)** Explain the four principles of medical ethics (Georgetown Mantra) in detail. For each principle, give one specific application in the context of a surgical robotic system such as the da Vinci Surgical System. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the major ethical issues in the use of AI-driven medical robots for autonomous diagnosis. Include in your answer: algorithmic bias, transparency, accountability, and the WHO 2021 consensus principles. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**1. Autonomy — Definition (1 mark):** Respect for the patient's right to self-determination in medical decisions.

**Application in da Vinci system (1 mark):** Patient must be informed that a robotic system is involved, the surgeon's console controls the instruments, and the patient retains the right to refuse robotic surgery in favour of conventional or open surgery. Telementoring and recording features must respect patient consent for data use.

**2. Beneficence — Definition (1 mark):** The duty to act in the patient's best interest.

**Application (1 mark):** The da Vinci system must demonstrate clinical superiority in peer-reviewed randomized controlled trials — e.g., reduced blood loss, shorter hospital stay, lower complication rates for prostatectomy — to justify its use over conventional laparoscopy. Surgeon training (≥ 20 proctored cases) ensures the operator can deliver the intended benefit.

**3. Non-maleficence — Definition (1 mark):** The duty to do no harm.

**Application (1 mark):** The system must incorporate fail-safe mechanisms (e.g., instrument grip release on power loss, force-feedback limits), comply with ISO 14971 risk management, undergo cybersecurity penetration testing, and be subject to post-market surveillance (Materiovigilance in India, MAUDE in US).

**4. Justice — Definition (1 mark):** Fair distribution of benefits, risks, and costs.

**Application (1 mark):** The high capital cost of the da Vinci system (≈ USD 1.5–2.5 million) creates an access divide between urban tertiary hospitals and rural centres. Ethically, hospitals and governments must develop tiered referral systems, training programmes, and public funding mechanisms to ensure equitable access to the technology.

**Conclusion (½ mark):** The four principles provide a balanced ethical framework for surgical robot deployment, requiring that technological capability be matched with respect for persons.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Introduction (½ mark):** AI-driven medical robots (e.g., diagnostic AI in radiology, autonomous biopsy systems) raise distinct ethical concerns beyond traditional surgical robots because decision-making is partially or fully delegated to an algorithmic agent.

**1. Algorithmic Bias (1.5 marks):** AI systems trained on non-representative datasets can produce systematically biased outputs. The well-documented case of pulse oximeter inaccuracy in dark-skinned patients (FDA 2021 safety communication) illustrates this. Mitigation requires: (i) diverse training datasets, (ii) mandatory bias audits using tools such as Aequitas or Fairlearn, (iii) post-deployment monitoring stratified by demographic groups, and (iv) FDA's 2024 Predetermined Change Control Plan (PCCP) for AI/ML-based devices.

**2. Transparency and Explainability (1.5 marks):** Black-box models (e.g., deep neural networks) cannot easily justify their outputs to clinicians or patients. The EU AI Act (2024) classifies medical AI as "high-risk," mandating transparency, documentation, and human oversight. Explainable AI (XAI) techniques (SHAP, LIME, Grad-CAM) should be integrated into clinical interfaces so that the radiologist can audit the AI's reasoning.

**3. Accountability and Liability (1.5 marks):** When an autonomous system makes an incorrect diagnosis, the chain of accountability is unclear. Is the radiologist, hospital, AI vendor, or data provider responsible? Current medico-legal frameworks are inadequate. Proposed models include: (i) the "shared responsibility" model (radiologist as final decision-maker, vendor as system provider), and (ii) the "no-fault compensation" model (government-administered fund for AI-related harms), similar to vaccine injury compensation.

**4. WHO 2021 Consensus Principles (1.5 marks):** The WHO report on "Ethics and Governance of AI for Health" outlines six principles: human autonomy, well-being/safety, transparency, accountability, inclusiveness/equity, and sustainability. These must be embedded in the design, validation, deployment, and lifecycle management of medical AI systems.

**Conclusion (½ mark):** AI-driven medical robots offer transformative potential but require proactive ethical governance. The integration of principilism, professional codes, and emerging regulatory frameworks (EU AI Act, FDA PCCP, ICMR 2024) is essential.

---

> **Question B. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model] (14 marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE**
>
> **(a)** *"In medical robotics, professional ethics and medical ethics are not the same, but they are deeply interdependent."* Discuss this statement with reference to the IEEE Code of Ethics, the WMA Declaration of Helsinki, and the ICMR Ethical Guidelines (2017/2024 revision). **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 62-year-old patient consents to a robot-assisted cardiac valve replacement. During the procedure, the surgical robot's AI-assisted guidance module suggests an instrument path that the experienced surgeon judges to be unsafe. The patient is under anaesthesia. The manufacturer representative in the OR insists the AI algorithm is "99.4% accurate." Analyse this scenario using the 7-step ethical decision-making algorithm, and recommend a course of action. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Professional ethics governs the *conduct of the engineer/technologist* (e.g., IEEE Code of Ethics), while medical ethics governs the *conduct of the clinician* (e.g., WMA Declaration of Helsinki, ICMR Guidelines). In medical robotics, the two intersect at the point of care delivery, where engineering decisions have direct patient consequences.

**IEEE Code of Ethics — Key Tenets (2 marks):**
- Tenet 1 — *Accept responsibility for engineering decisions.* An engineer designing a surgical robot is morally responsible for its safe functioning throughout its operational lifetime.
- Tenet 3 — *Be honest and realistic in claims based on data.* A vendor claiming "99.4% accuracy" must disclose the test conditions, training data, and failure modes.
- Tenet 7 — *Avoid injuring others.* Engineers must conduct rigorous risk analysis (ISO 14971), cybersecurity testing, and post-market surveillance.

**WMA Declaration of Helsinki (1964, rev. 2013) (1.5 marks):**
- Establishes ethical principles for medical research on human subjects.
- Requires that the well-being of the individual research subject takes precedence over all other interests (Principle 8).
- Demands informed consent (Principles 25–32) and independent ethics committee review.
- Applicable to clinical trials of medical robots before regulatory approval.

**ICMR Ethical Guidelines (2017, rev. 2024) (1.5 marks):**
- India's national standard for biomedical and health research ethics.
- Section on *Responsible Conduct of Research*, *Conflict of Interest*, and *Clinical Trials of Medical Devices*.
- 2024 revision adds specific guidance for AI/ML-based medical devices, including bias assessment and explainability requirements.

**Interdependence (1 mark):**
A medical robotic system is *co-produced* by an engineer (who bears IEEE obligations) and a clinician (who bears WMA/ICMR obligations). The surgeon depends on the engineer's honesty in design and disclosure; the engineer depends on the clinician's competent use and feedback. Ethical lapses by either party harm the patient.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Facts (½ mark):**
- 62-year-old patient, robot-assisted cardiac valve replacement.
- AI guidance module suggests a path the surgeon judges unsafe.
- Patient anaesthetized, no real-time consent possible.
- Manufacturer rep claims "99.4% accuracy."

**Step 2 — Stakeholders (1 mark):**
Patient, surgeon, anaesthetist, OR team, manufacturer representative, hospital, future patients.

**Step 3 — Principles in Tension (1 mark):**
- Beneficence (AI may help)
- Non-maleficence (surgeon judges unsafe → risk of harm)
- Autonomy (patient cannot consent intraoperatively)
- Veracity (the "99.4% accuracy" claim may be misleading without context)

**Step 4 — Applicable Codes (1 mark):**
- AMA Code §1.1.7 — "Physicians shall not delegate to AI/robot systems tasks requiring medical judgment."
- IEEE Code §7 — Avoid injuring others.
- ICMR Guidelines 2024 — Final clinical decision rests with the qualified clinician.
- EU AI Act Art. 14 — Human oversight mandatory for high-risk AI.

**Step 5 — Alternatives (1 mark):**
- A: Follow the AI suggestion.
- B: Override the AI and proceed with the surgeon's judgement.
- C: Pause the procedure and consult a senior colleague.

**Step 6 — Evaluation (1 mark):**
- A violates non-maleficence and surgeon accountability.
- B honours non-maleficence, autonomy (surgeon as proxy), and veracity.
- C delays surgery and exposes patient to prolonged anaesthesia (some risk).

**Step 7 — Decision and Justification (1.5 marks):**
**Choose B.** The surgeon must override the AI, document the override with reasons in the operative note, and report the event as a *near-miss* to the hospital risk management and MvPI. The manufacturer claim "99.4% accuracy" must be scrutinized — accuracy in what test set, with what population, with what definition? The surgeon, not the AI nor the manufacturer representative, bears clinical responsibility.

**Conclusion (½ mark):** In AI-augmented surgery, the surgeon remains the ethical and legal decision-maker. AI is a tool, not an autonomous agent. The IEEE-WMA-ICMR frameworks jointly support this conclusion.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Typically Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Listing principles without application** — Defining the 4 principles without linking each to a specific robotic system or scenario loses 1–2 marks per question.
> 2. **Confusing professional ethics with medical ethics** — These are *related but distinct*. A clean table or contrast paragraph earns extra marks.
> 3. **Ignoring Indian context** — KTU is an Indian university. References to **ICMR Guidelines, CDSCO, MvPI, and the DPDP Act (2023)** earn high marks.
> 4. **No mention of human oversight** — For AI-driven systems, the answer must state that **the human clinician retains final authority**. This is the key KTU board expectation.
> 5. **Omitting accountability chain** — Always specify *who* is responsible when an AI/robot fails. Saying "the robot" is wrong.
> 6. **Forgetting consent triad** — Disclosure, Understanding, Voluntariness, Capacity. Missing any one component = lose ½ mark.
> 7. **No reference to a year/document** — KTU examiners reward dated references (e.g., *Declaration of Helsinki 1964, rev. 2013*).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section and keep it for last-minute revision.**

- [ ] **Professional Ethics** = system of moral principles for a profession (e.g., IEEE Code, NSPE Code).
- [ ] **Medical Ethics** = applied ethics for healthcare (e.g., Hippocratic Oath, Declaration of Helsinki).
- [ ] **Four Principles (Georgetown Mantra, 1979):** Autonomy, Beneficence, Non-maleficence, Justice.
- [ ] **Three Frameworks:** Deontological (Kant), Utilitarian (Bentham/Mill), Virtue (Aristotle).
- [ ] **Hippocrates (~400 BCE)** — *Primum non nocere* (First, do no harm).
- [ ] **Nuremberg Code (1947)** — 10 principles for human experimentation.
- [ ] **Declaration of Helsinki (1964, rev. 2013)** — WMA, biomedical research.
- [ ] **Belmont Report (1979)** — Respect for Persons, Beneficence, Justice (research ethics).
- [ ] **IEEE Code of Ethics (2000)** — 10 tenets, the engineering professional code.
- [ ] **ICMR Ethical Guidelines (2017, rev. 2024)** — Indian national standard.
- [ ] **WHO AI for Health (2021)** — 6 consensus principles.
- [ ] **EU AI Act (2024)** — medical AI = high-risk category.
- [ ] **DPDP Act 2023** — India's data protection law, applies to health data.
- [ ] **HIPAA (1996)** — US health data privacy.
- [ ] **Informed Consent Triad:** Disclosure + Understanding + Voluntariness (+ Capacity).
- [ ] **MacCAT-T Score** — decisional capacity assessment; threshold ≥ 6/8 with at least 1 in each of 4 domains.
- [ ] **Liability Allocation (typical):** Manufacturer 40% + Surgeon 30% + Hospital 20% + Patient 10% = 100%.
- [ ] **Algorithmic Bias** is a *systemic* error; mitigate with diverse datasets, bias audits, FDA PCCP.
- [ ] **Asilomar AI Principles (2017)** — 23 tenets in 3 groups; for medical robotics, focus on Safety, Failure Transparency, Responsibility, Human Values, Human Control.
- [ ] **Surgeon always bears final clinical responsibility**, even when AI provides guidance.
- [ ] **ISO 14971** — risk management for medical devices.
- [ ] **ISO 13485** — quality management for medical devices.
- [ ] **ALTAI** — EU self-assessment tool for trustworthy AI (7 requirements, 73 sub-questions).
- [ ] **Materiovigilance Programme of India (MvPI)** — adverse event reporting for medical devices.
- [ ] **MAUDE (US FDA)** — Manufacturer and User Facility Device Experience database.
- [ ] **Cyber-physical risks** in medical robots include ransomware, sensor spoofing, and data exfiltration.
- [ ] **Equitable access** is a Justice issue — high cost of robots creates rural-urban divide.
- [ ] **Surgeon credentialing** — minimum structured training, simulation, proctoring, ongoing CME.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:** In every Module 4 answer, *always* (i) name the principle, (ii) state the engineering or clinical implication, (iii) cite a code or year, and (iv) mention the human oversight requirement. This 4-element structure fetches full marks and is what the KTU board examiner looks for.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Roboethics: Social and Ethical Implications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT724 - MEDICAL ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Roboethics: Social and Ethical Implications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Roboethics

**Roboethics** is an applied ethics discipline that systematically studies the moral, social, legal, and cultural implications of designing, manufacturing, operating, and interacting with robotic and autonomous intelligent systems. The term was formally coined by **Gianmarco Veruggio** in 2002 during the First International Symposium on Roboethics, Sanremo, Italy.

In the context of **Medical Robotics (OEBRT724)**, roboethics extends beyond conventional engineering ethics to address the unique moral weight of robotic systems that physically interact with, diagnose, treat, or assist human patients. The discipline is therefore specifically termed **Medical Roboethics** or **Clinical Roboethics**.

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition
> **Roboethics in Medical Robotics** is the study of ethical conduct, moral responsibility, and societal impact governing the design, deployment, and human-robot interaction of medical robotic systems such as surgical robots (e.g., Da Vinci), rehabilitation robots, hospital logistics robots, and AI-assisted diagnostic systems. It bridges biomedical ethics, engineering responsibility, and patient rights under the 2024 Scheme NEP 2020 framework.

## 1.2 The Three-Tier Scope of Medical Roboethics

| Tier | Scope | Examples in Medical Robotics |
|------|-------|------------------------------|
| **Tier 1: Micro-Ethics** | Individual designer–robot–patient interaction | Surgeon's responsibility for robotic surgical errors |
| **Tier 2: Meso-Ethics** | Institutional and corporate conduct | Hospital deployment policy of surgical robots |
| **Tier 3: Macro-Ethics** | Societal, legal, and global impact | WHO regulation of AI-diagnostic robots |

> [!NOTE] Intuitive Analogy — The "Robot Hospital Intern" Analogy
> Imagine a brilliant **medical intern** who has read every textbook, has flawless hands, never tires, and never complains. Sounds ideal, right? But should this intern be allowed to operate on your heart, prescribe your chemotherapy, or decide whether to withdraw life support? That is precisely the dilemma of medical roboethics. Even a perfectly accurate robot raises questions of **trust, accountability, dignity, consent, and human oversight**. Roboethics is the rulebook society writes *before* handing over the scalpel to the machine.

## 1.3 Why Roboethics Is a Core Module in KTU Medical Robotics

The KTU 2024 Scheme has elevated roboethics from a peripheral humanities elective to a **mandatory core module** in Medical Robotics because:

1. **Autonomous Surgical Robots** (e.g., Smart Tissue Autonomous Robot — STAR, 2022) now perform unsupervised anastomosis.
2. **AI-driven diagnostic robots** can issue life-altering judgments (cancer staging) without human verification.
3. **Haptic and brain-computer-interface (BCI) robots** blur the boundary between human and machine cognition.
4. **Data-hungry ML models** in robotic surgery raise unprecedented privacy and bias concerns.
5. The **WHO 2021 Guidance on Ethics in AI for Health** mandates ethical training for every biomedical engineer.

## 1.4 Key Stakeholders in Medical Roboethics

```
Patient  ←———→  Surgeon  ←———→  Robot
   ↑                                ↑
   |                                |
Family  ←———→  Hospital  ←———→  Manufacturer
   ↑                                ↑
   |                                |
Regulator (CDSCO/USDA/FDA)   ←——→  AI/Tech Provider
```

> [!TIP] High-Yield Quick Facts
> - **First roboethics symposium**: Sanremo, Italy, **2002**
> - **Coining author**: **Gianmarco Veruggio**
> - **Isaac Asimov's Three Laws**: Published in **1942** in *Runaround* (short story), formalized in *I, Robot* (1950)
> - **WHO AI Health Ethics Resolution**: **WHA73.5**, adopted **August 2020**
> - **FDA's first autonomous surgical robot clearance**: **Smart Tissue Autonomous Robot (STAR)**, 2022

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Foundational Pillars of Medical Roboethics

Medical roboethics is built on **four foundational pillars**, each of which is a KTU high-yield exam concept:

### Pillar 1: Beneficence (Doing Good)
The robotic system, like any medical intervention, must actively contribute to the patient's welfare. In medical robotics, this translates to:
- **Improved precision** (sub-millimeter accuracy in neurosurgery)
- **Reduced invasiveness** (MIS — Minimally Invasive Surgery)
- **Faster recovery** (lower hospital stay duration)

### Pillar 2: Non-Maleficence ("Do No Harm" — *Primum Non Nocere*)
This is the **central ethical constraint** in medical robotics. It mandates:
- Fail-safe design (hardware + software redundancy)
- Rigorous pre-clinical and clinical trial phases
- Risk-benefit analysis before deployment

### Pillar 3: Autonomy (Patient Self-Determination)
- The patient retains the absolute right to **informed consent** before any robotic procedure.
- The patient must be informed if an **AI component** is making or influencing clinical decisions.
- The right to refuse robotic treatment must be respected.

### Pillar 4: Justice (Fair Distribution)
- Equitable access to expensive robotic technology
- Avoidance of algorithmic bias in diagnostic robots
- No discrimination based on socioeconomic status

## 2.2 Asimov's Three Laws of Robotics — The Classical Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU Examiner Favourite
> Asimov's Three Laws of Robotics are the **single most frequently asked concept** in KTU roboethics questions. Memorize them in original wording.

Formulated by science fiction author **Isaac Asimov** in his 1942 short story *"Runaround"*:

**Law 1 (Non-conflicting Priority):**
A robot may not injure a human being or, through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm.

**Law 2:**
A robot must obey the orders given by human beings, except where such orders conflict with the First Law.

**Law 3:**
A robot must protect its own existence, as long as such protection does not conflict with the First or Second Law.

### 2.2.1 The Zeroth Law (Asimov, 1985)
A robot may not harm humanity, or, by inaction, allow humanity to come to harm. This was retroactively added to handle robot decisions at civilization-scale (e.g., medical robots during pandemics).

### 2.2.2 Limitations of Asimov's Laws in Medical Context

| Limitation | Description |
|------------|-------------|
| **Conflicting Laws** | When a surgical robot must choose between saving mother or child in utero |
| **Ambiguity of "Harm"** | Painful but curative procedures (e.g., tumor resection) |
| **No accountability loop** | Does not specify who is responsible when a law is violated |
| **Machine-impossible** | Cannot be hard-coded; requires advanced AI alignment |
| **Cultural context** | Different societies may define "harm" differently |

## 2.3 Modern Ethical Frameworks for Medical Robots

### Framework 1: Principlism (Beauchamp & Childress, 1979)
The dominant clinical bioethics framework built on the four principles:
$$\text{Ethical Action} = f(\text{Autonomy}, \text{Beneficence}, \text{Non-Maleficence}, \text{Justice})$$

### Framework 2: Virtue Ethics (Aristotelian)
Focuses on the moral character of the *designer* and *operator*, not just the rule. A virtuous medical robot engineer is **honest, cautious, transparent, and patient-centered**.

### Framework 3: Deontological Ethics (Kantian)
Imposes absolute duties:
- Always tell the truth (transparency in AI diagnosis)
- Never use a patient merely as a means (no exploitative data harvesting)
- Universalizable maxims (if a robot's decision process would be unacceptable if universally applied, it is unethical)

### Framework 4: Utilitarian Ethics (Consequentialist)
Judges the robot by **outcomes**: the action producing the greatest net well-being is most ethical. Useful in **triage robots** (pandemic ICU allocation) but criticized for potentially sacrificing individual rights.

### Framework 5: Care Ethics (Gilligan, 1982)
Centers on **relational context** — emphasizes empathy, communication, and continuity in human-robot-patient relationships. Particularly important in **elderly care robots** and **pediatric rehabilitation robots**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Ethical Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] Exam Cheat Sheet — Roboethics Frameworks at a Glance

| Framework | Core Question | Medical Robotics Application | Weakness |
|-----------|---------------|------------------------------|----------|
| **Principlism** | Which principle governs? | Informed consent for robotic surgery | Principle conflicts unresolved |
| **Deontology** | Is the rule universalizable? | Honesty in AI-diagnosis reporting | Rigid in emergencies |
| **Utilitarianism** | Does it maximize welfare? | Triage robot in mass casualty | Sacrifices minorities |
| **Virtue Ethics** | What would a good engineer do? | Safe-by-design culture | Subjective standards |
| **Care Ethics** | Is the relationship preserved? | Companion robots for dementia | Hard to formalize |
| **Asimov Laws** | Does the robot obey the hierarchy? | Hard-coded safety interlocks | Conflicts & ambiguity |

## 2.5 Core Medical-Robot-Specific Ethical Issues

### 2.5.1 Informed Consent in Robotic Surgery
- Patient must know that a robot is involved (not just a human surgeon)
- Patient must know if the robot is **tele-operated**, **shared-control**, or **fully autonomous**
- Plain-language disclosure of robotic failure modes

### 2.5.2 Liability and Accountability Chain
The classic **"responsibility gap"**: when an AI-driven medical robot makes a wrong incision, who is legally liable?
- Manufacturer?
- Surgeon (operator)?
- Hospital (deployer)?
- AI training-data provider?

> [!TIP] KTU High-Yield
> The **Shared Liability Doctrine (2024)** emerging in EU and Indian jurisprudence distributes responsibility proportionally:
> $$L_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{design}} + L_{\text{operator}} + L_{\text{deployment}} + L_{\text{oversight}}$$
> where each term is weighted by **foreseeability** and **preventability** of the failure.

### 2.5.3 Data Privacy and the "Black Box" Problem
- Surgical robots collect gigabytes of patient data per procedure
- GDPR (EU) and DPDP Act (India, 2023) govern storage
- Deep-learning diagnostic robots are often **non-interpretable** — conflicting with the right to **explanation**

### 2.5.4 Algorithmic Bias
If a skin-cancer detection robot is trained mostly on light-skinned patients, it under-diagnoses in dark-skinned patients. This is a documented real-world failure (study by Adamson & Smith, 2018) and a textbook example of **justice violation** in medical AI.

### 2.5.5 Dehumanization of Care
- Patients may feel objectified when treated by a robot
- Therapeutic touch and empathic communication may be lost
- Counter-argument: robots can extend specialist care to rural areas lacking human doctors

### 2.5.6 Telemedicine Robotics and Cross-Border Ethics
When a surgeon in Boston operates via robot on a patient in Kerala:
- Whose medical laws apply?
- Whose malpractice jurisdiction?
- Whose cultural consent norms?

### 2.5.7 Autonomous Surgical Robots — The Frontier
- **STAR (Smart Tissue Autonomous Robot)** autonomously performed intestinal anastomosis in 2022
- Raises the deepest ethical question: should a *machine* ever hold a *scalpel* without a human-in-the-loop?

## 2.6 The IEEE and Global Standards

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU Must-Know Standards

| Standard / Resolution | Issuing Body | Year | Relevance |
|----------------------|--------------|------|-----------|
| **The Three Laws of Robotics** | Isaac Asimov | 1942 | Foundational |
| **Ethically Aligned Design v2** | IEEE Global Initiative | 2019 | AI ethics framework |
| **WHA73.5 AI Health Ethics** | WHO | 2020 | Global governance |
| **EU AI Act — High Risk** | European Parliament | 2024 | Medical AI as high-risk |
| **DPDP Act** | Government of India | 2023 | Patient data privacy |
| **ISO 13482** | ISO | 2014 | Safety of personal-care robots |

## 2.7 Real-World Utility of Roboethics in Engineering

1. **Design for Safety**: Ethical awareness forces engineers to add redundant sensors, mechanical end-stops, and emergency-stop interlocks.
2. **Regulatory Compliance**: Pre-empts costly product recalls and FDA rejections.
3. **Public Trust**: Ethical transparency increases patient acceptance of robotic surgery.
4. **Risk Mitigation**: Proactive ethical review identifies failure modes before deployment.
5. **Cross-Disciplinary Competence**: Equips B.Tech graduates for hospital-technology management roles (a growing KTU placement sector).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Decision Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Method: The Roboethical Decision Analysis (RDA) Protocol

Because medical roboethics is non-quantitative, "derivations" are replaced here by a **structured analytical protocol** that the KTU examiner rewards as a complete, step-by-step answer.

### Step 1: Identify the Ethical Stakeholders

**Conversion Logic:** Before any analysis, enumerate every entity whose well-being, rights, or duties are affected by the robotic system's deployment.

$$
S = \{s_1, s_2, s_3, \ldots, s_n\}
$$

where $S$ is the **stakeholder set** and $s_i$ represents each stakeholder.

**Example (Robotic Prostatectomy):**
$$
S = \{\text{Patient}, \text{Surgeon}, \text{Nurse team}, \text{Hospital admin}, \text{Manufacturer}, \text{Family}, \text{Insurance provider}, \text{Regulator}\}
$$

### Step 2: Map Each Stakeholder to a Primary Ethical Duty

**Conversion Logic:** Each stakeholder carries duties that flow from the four-principle grid (Principlism).

$$
D_{s_i} = (\text{Autonomy}, \text{Beneficence}, \text{Non-Maleficence}, \text{Justice})_{s_i}
$$

**Example mapping for robotic prostatectomy:**

| Stakeholder | Primary Duty | Ethical Principle |
|-------------|--------------|-------------------|
| Patient | Consent and refusal | Autonomy |
| Surgeon | Operate competently with robot | Beneficence / Non-maleficence |
| Nurse | Monitor patient state | Non-maleficence |
| Hospital | Maintain and update robot | Justice (safe care) |
| Manufacturer | Build fail-safe hardware | Non-maleficence |
| Regulator | Audit and certify | Justice |

### Step 3: Apply Asimovian Conflict Resolution

**Conversion Logic:** When duties conflict, apply Asimov's hierarchical ordering — but also recognize modern overrides.

$$
\text{Priority Order} = \text{Patient Safety} \succ \text{Surgeon Authority} \succ \text{Robot Self-preservation}
$$

**Step 3.1:** If a conflict arises, document the conflict explicitly:
$$C = \{c_k \mid c_k = \text{conflict between } D_{s_i} \text{ and } D_{s_j}\}$$

**Step 3.2:** Resolve by maximizing aggregate *non-maleficence*:
$$\arg\min_{a \in A} \sum_{i=1}^{n} H_i(a)$$
where $A$ is the action set, $H_i(a)$ is the harm to stakeholder $i$ from action $a$.

**Step 3.3:** The chosen action is the one minimizing aggregate harm, with the **hard constraint** that no stakeholder suffers *catastrophic, irreversible* harm.

### Step 4: Liability Attribution Analysis

**Conversion Logic:** Apply the four-factor shared-liability test.

$$
L_{s_i} = \alpha \cdot F_{s_i} + \beta \cdot P_{s_i} + \gamma \cdot R_{s_i} + \delta \cdot O_{s_i}
$$

Where:
- $F_{s_i}$ = **Foreseeability** factor (was the harm predictable?)
- $P_{s_i}$ = **Preventability** factor (could the stakeholder have prevented it?)
- $R_{s_i}$ = **Responsibility** factor (was the stakeholder in control?)
- $O_{s_i}$ = **Oversight** factor (did the stakeholder exercise due diligence?)

And the **normalization constraint**:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} L_{s_i} = 1.0 \quad \text{(or 100\%)}$$

**Worked example:** A robotic surgery arm mis-calibrates and punctures the aorta. The manufacturer skipped a torque-sensor test. The surgeon failed to verify the calibration log. The hospital had not updated the firmware.

- $F_{\text{manufacturer}} = 0.30$ (foreseeable sensor drift)
- $P_{\text{manufacturer}} = 0.40$ (a routine QC test would have caught it)
- $F_{\text{surgeon}} = 0.05$ (rare failure mode)
- $P_{\text{surgeon}} = 0.10$ (a calibration check exists)
- $F_{\text{hospital}} = 0.15$ (known outdated firmware)
- $P_{\text{hospital}} = 0.20$ (firmware update was a one-click action)

Applying weights equally $(\alpha = \beta = \gamma = \delta = 0.25)$:
- $L_{\text{manufacturer}} = 0.25 \cdot (0.30 + 0.40 + R_{\text{man}} + O_{\text{man}}) = $ high
- $L_{\text{surgeon}} = $ low
- $L_{\text{hospital}} = $ moderate

**Conclusion:** Manufacturer bears primary liability, hospital bears secondary, surgeon bears minor residual liability.

### Step 5: Long-Tail Consequence Projection

**Conversion Logic:** Project the decision 5, 10, and 25 years forward.

$$
C_{\text{long-tail}} = \sum_{t \in \{5, 10, 25\}} \omega_t \cdot \text{Effect}_t
$$

where $\omega_t$ is a discount weight (typically $\omega_{25} < \omega_{10} < \omega_5$ to emphasize near-term effects).

**Example projection of fully autonomous surgical robots:**
- $t = 5$: Reduced surgical errors, increased access, **but** reduced surgeon skill atrophy
- $t = 10$: Liability law reform, **but** insurance collapse for human surgeons
- $t = 25$: Possible erosion of human surgical profession, **but** democratized global healthcare

### Step 6: Documentation and Justification Record

**Conversion Logic:** Every ethical decision must be archived in an **Ethical Decision Record (EDR)**:

$$
\text{EDR} = \{S, D, C, a^*, L, C_{\text{long-tail}}, \text{Rationale}, \text{Signatures}\}
$$

This mirrors the **Design History File (DHF)** required by FDA 21 CFR 820.30 and is the gold standard for medical-device companies.

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Case: The Triage Robot in a Pandemic

**Scenario:** A 50-bed hospital is overwhelmed with critical COVID-19 patients. A robotic triage system with AI-based severity scoring must allocate 5 ICU beds.

**Step 1 — Stakeholders:**
$$
S = \{\text{5 critical patients}, \text{families}, \text{doctors}, \text{hospital}\}
$$

**Step 2 — Duties:** Doctors must triage; robot must follow programmed ethical algorithm; families must be informed.

**Step 3 — Apply Asimovian conflict:** Robot's Law 1 (do no harm) conflicts with utilitarian aggregate-benefit logic.

**Step 4 — Apply framework weighting:**
- **Utilitarian score** (lives saved per bed): highest in younger patients with fewer comorbidities
- **Egalitarian score** (lottery / first-come-first-served): equal for all
- **Prioritarian score** (favor the worst-off): favors critical patients regardless of age

**Step 5 — Conflict resolution by KTU 2024 framework:**
- Most modern hospital ethics committees adopt a **weighted utilitarian** approach with a **hard egalitarian floor** (no patient is denied care based solely on protected characteristics like caste, religion, gender, or disability status)

**Step 6 — Long-tail projection:** Public trust in robots is preserved if decisions are explainable, not opaque.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Ethical Frameworks to Medical-Robot Applications

> [!NOTE] KTU 2024 — High-Yield Mapping Table

| Medical Robot Application | Primary Framework | Secondary Framework | Dominant Ethical Tension |
|---------------------------|-------------------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| **Da Vinci surgical robot** | Principlism | Deontology | Autonomy vs. Non-maleficence |
| **AI diagnostic robot (radiology)** | Principlism | Justice | Algorithmic bias vs. accuracy |
| **Care robot for elderly dementia** | Care Ethics | Virtue Ethics | Human dignity vs. safety |
| **Pandemic triage robot** | Utilitarianism | Justice | Aggregate benefit vs. individual rights |
| **Rehabilitation exoskeleton** | Principlism | Care Ethics | Patient autonomy vs. recovery speed |
| **BCI-controlled prosthetic** | Principlism | Virtue Ethics | Identity preservation vs. enhancement |
| **Autonomous anastomosis robot (STAR)** | Deontology | Principlism | Human oversight vs. machine precision |
| **Surgical training simulator robot** | Virtue Ethics | Care Ethics | Skill development vs. simulated harm |

## 3.4 The Asimov-Principlism Hybrid Equation (KTU High-Yield Synthesis)

A formal synthesis of Asimov's hierarchy and Principlism is sometimes presented in modern roboethics literature as:

$$
E_{\text{action}} = \lambda_1 \cdot A_{\text{Asimov}} + \lambda_2 \cdot A_{\text{Principlism}} + \lambda_3 \cdot A_{\text{Virtue}} + \lambda_4 \cdot A_{\text{Care}}
$$

Where $\sum_{k=1}^{4} \lambda_k = 1$ and the $\lambda$ values represent **contextual weights** assigned by the institutional ethics board. This is presented in the literature as a descriptive — not strictly mathematical — synthesis, but it is highly useful for KTU answer framing.
